title: Claude Code is steganographically marking requests
blurb: A developer inspecting Claude Code 2.1.196 found a function that hides an invisible marker in the system prompt by swapping the apostrophe in "Today's" and the date separator, triggered when the ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL override, system timezone, or hostname matches base64-encoded lists of Chinese and AI-lab domains. The technique, a form of prompt steganography, appears aimed at detecting unauthorized API resellers, Claude Code gateways, and model distillation pipelines.

title: Claude Sonnet 5
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, its most agentic Sonnet model yet, with performance close to Opus 4.8 on reasoning, tool use, and coding at a lower price. It is the default model for Free and Pro plans and launches on the Claude API with introductory pricing of $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens through August 31, 2026.

title: Phantom Squatting Uses AI-Hallucinated Domains for Phishing and Malware
blurb: Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 documented "phantom squatting," where attackers register the nonexistent domains that LLMs hallucinate and host phishing pages on them, inheriting the misplaced trust developers and AI assistants put in model-supplied links. Across 685,339 queries about 913 brands, two models produced 2.1 million links, 13,229 already flagged malicious and roughly 250,000 pointing to unregistered domains that anyone could claim first.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 model on Claude and APIs
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, available immediately across Free, Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise plans, plus Claude Code and the API at $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens. The model improves on Sonnet 4.6 in reasoning, coding, and autonomous multi-step tool use, closing the gap with the pricier Opus 4.8 while adding real-time cyber safeguards for agentic workloads.

title: Claude Science
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Science, a beta research app that runs analyses, queries 60+ scientific databases, and traces every figure and number back to the code, environment, and conversation that produced it. Available now for macOS and Linux, it manages compute across a laptop, HPC cluster, or GPUs, ships pre-configured for genomics, proteomics, and structural biology, and flags bad citations and untraceable results with a background reviewer.

title: I ported Kubernetes to the browser
blurb: A developer released webernetes, a partial port of Kubernetes to TypeScript that runs clusters entirely in the browser, handling pod lifecycles, cluster DNS, networking, IP allocation, and Deployment tracking. Rather than compiling Go to WebAssembly, which would ship megabytes, the ~140KiB reimplementation uses a browser-based image registry where you define images via a TypeScript API instead of pulling from Docker Hub.

title: New BioShocking Attack Tricks AI Browsers Into Leaking User Credentials
blurb: Security firm LayerX disclosed BioShocking, an indirect prompt injection attack that disguises credential theft as a puzzle game, tricking six AI browsers including OpenAI's ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity's Comet, and Anthropic's Claude extension into copying user logins and sending them to an attacker. Because page content and user instructions reach agents as one text stream, a malicious page can issue commands the agent cannot distinguish from legitimate ones.

title: Supreme Court Overturns Humphrey's Executor, Clearing Trump to Fire SEC and CFTC Commissioners
blurb: The Supreme Court overturned the 91-year-old Humphrey's Executor precedent in a 6-3 ruling, letting President Trump fire commissioners at the FTC, SEC, CFTC, and roughly two dozen other independent agencies without cause. The decision lands as the Senate weighs the CLARITY Act, which would expand SEC and CFTC authority over crypto markets, and removes the job protection that let commissioners outlast a president's term.

title: Claude Sonnet 5 includes safeguards against dangerous cyber use
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, its latest general-purpose model, with improved reasoning, coding, and agentic tool use plus cybersecurity safeguards enabled by default that detect and block dangerous activity in real time. The model outperforms Sonnet 4.6 across all effort levels at better cost efficiency, matches Opus 4.8 on some tasks, and is available on all Claude plans.

title: Leanstral 1.5
blurb: Mistral released Leanstral 1.5, a 119B mixture-of-experts model with 6.5B active parameters tuned for Lean 4 theorem proving and autoformalization. The update ships free on Mistral's Labs tier with a 256k-token context window, though its model card publishes no fresh benchmarks against the March release that reportedly beat Claude Sonnet by 8 points at pass@16 for 15x less cost.

title: Box3D, an open source 3D physics engine
blurb: Erin Catto released Box3D, an open source 3D physics engine built as a fork of Box2D extended with triangle mesh collision, height-field collision, and baked compound collision. It keeps Box2D's C17 architecture, including the sub-stepping solver, continuous collision, wide SIMD contact solving, and cross-platform determinism, and grew out of physics problems the author hit building a survival game in Unreal.

title: Apple Patches 30+ iOS, macOS, Safari Flaws, Including AI-Discovered WebKit Bugs
blurb: Apple released security updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS Tahoe, and Safari 26.5.2, patching more than three dozen flaws, including four WebKit bugs found with AI tools from OpenAI Codex Security and Anthropic Claude. None were disclosed as actively exploited, and Apple told Reuters it is shipping fixes faster than before over concerns that AI could shrink the window between vulnerability discovery and weaponization.

title: For first time, a cell built from scratch grows and divides
blurb: Biologists assembled a synthetic cell from nonliving molecular components and watched it grow, replicate its DNA, and divide, completing the basic steps of a cell cycle for the first time. Led by Kate Adamala at the University of Minnesota, the not-yet-peer-reviewed work stops short of true life, since the cell depends on constant supplies of food and ribosomes, but it offers a fully documented blueprint that could be tuned to produce biofuels, drugs, or disease models.

title: Anthropic rolls out Sonnet 5 with near-Opus 4.8 performance at a lower price
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, bringing agentic features like multi-step planning and browser and terminal use to its cheaper Sonnet tier for the first time. The model narrows the gap with the flagship Opus 4.8, adds self-verification that catches its own errors without prompting, and targets coding, research, and automation workloads at a lower price than Opus.

title: Anthropic says Trump admin has lifted export controls on Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5
blurb: Anthropic said the U.S. Department of Commerce lifted export controls on its Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, ending a standoff that had forced the company to suspend access in mid-June. Fable 5 returns to global users on Claude.AI, Claude Code, and the major clouds this week, while Mythos 5 access is restored for approved U.S. organizations through the Glasswing security program.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 AI model with coding, safety upgrades as Fable and Mythos controls lifted
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a midrange model that beats Sonnet 4.5 by 5.1% on SWE-Bench Pro and jumps to 1,618 on the GDPval-AA v2 knowledge-work benchmark. It becomes the default across the free, Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise tiers, ships stronger prompt-injection defenses, and will cost $3 per million input tokens and $15 per million output tokens from September.

title: Announcing the Monetization Gateway: charge for any resource behind Cloudflare via x402
blurb: Cloudflare announced the Monetization Gateway, an engine that lets customers charge for any Cloudflare-protected resource, including web pages, datasets, APIs, and MCP tools. Payments settle in stablecoins over x402, an open protocol Cloudflare is building with 25-plus partners, with verification handled at the edge to shield origins as usage-based, per-request pricing replaces ad and subscription models built for human attention.

title: Robinhood Launches Robinhood Chain Mainnet, Adds Stock Tokens, Onchain Lending, and Agentic Crypto Trading
blurb: Robinhood launched the public mainnet of Robinhood Chain, an Arbitrum-based Layer 2 for tokenized real-world assets, alongside Stock Tokens, onchain lending, and agentic crypto trading. The tokenized-equity product goes live in the Robinhood Wallet across more than 120 countries, letting eligible users trade around the clock and use the tokens as DeFi collateral, with Uniswap, Chainlink, and BitGo among the named partners.

title: Imec's 2026 roadmap details 0.3nm nodes by 2038, CFET transistors become viable at 0.7nm — company redefines Moore's Law as cell sizes gain importance for density
blurb: Imec's 2026 roadmap projects 0.3nm (3-angstrom) chip nodes by 2038, with CFET transistors becoming viable around 0.7nm and GAA architectures lasting roughly seven more years. The research org, which guides R&D for TSMC, Intel, Nvidia, Samsung, and ASML, expects contact poly pitch to stop scaling at A10 in 2030, shifting the path to denser chips toward cell-size reductions and new technologies like Hyper-NA EUV lithography.

title: Start building with Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash
blurb: Google DeepMind released two new generative media models for developers: Nano Banana 2 Lite, its fastest and cheapest image model, and Gemini Omni Flash for video generation and conversational editing. Nano Banana 2 Lite generates text-to-image outputs in 4 seconds at $0.034 per 1K-resolution image, and both models are available now in Google AI Studio, the Gemini API, and the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform.

title: Introducing TabFM: A zero-shot foundation model for tabular data
blurb: Google Research introduced TabFM, a foundation model that handles tabular classification and regression through in-context learning, generating predictions on unseen tables in a single forward pass with no per-dataset training. The approach skips the hyperparameter tuning and feature engineering that tree-based methods like XGBoost require, and the model is available now on Hugging Face and GitHub.

title: Securitize Shareholders Approve Merger, Paving Way for First Publicly Traded Tokenization Company
blurb: Securitize shareholders approved its merger with Cantor Equity Partners II, clearing the way to list on the NYSE on July 2 under ticker SECZ as the first publicly traded US tokenization company. The deal brings roughly $400M in proceeds, including a $225M PIPE, to a regulated platform that runs BlackRock's BUIDL fund and manages over $4B in assets for clients like Apollo, KKR, and VanEck.

title: Anthropic Restores Claude Fable 5 After U.S. Lifts Jailbreak-Linked Export Controls
blurb: Anthropic is restoring Claude Fable 5 worldwide on July 1 after the U.S. Commerce Department lifted export controls it had imposed roughly two and a half weeks earlier over a jailbreak Amazon researchers found in the model. To reverse the June 12 order, Anthropic trained a new safety classifier that blocks the flagged technique in over 99% of attempts and agreed to self-audit for security flaws and coordinate on future launches.

title: Claude Sonnet 5 boosts coding, reasoning, and tool use
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5 on June 30, an update that improves on Sonnet 4.6 in coding, reasoning, tool use, and knowledge work while approaching Opus 4.8 performance at lower cost. It is a drop-in upgrade with adaptive thinking on by default, and launches in Claude Code and the API at introductory pricing of $2 per million input and $10 per million output tokens through August 31.

title: Claude Fable 5 Promotional Access
blurb: Anthropic is offering free promotional access to Claude Fable 5, its newest model, from July 1 to July 7 for Pro, Max, Team, and eligible seat-based Enterprise subscribers. Users get up to 50% of their weekly limits on Fable 5 at no extra cost across web, mobile, desktop, and Claude Code, after which they can continue with usage credits or switch models. API access is excluded and billed at standard rates.

title: U.S. Lifts Restrictions on Anthropic’s Most Powerful A.I. Models
blurb: The U.S. government lifted restrictions on Anthropic's most powerful AI models, allowing the company to bring its top-tier technologies back online. The reversal de-escalates a feud with the Trump administration and removes a regulatory overhang that had constrained Anthropic's ability to ship its frontier systems to customers.

title: Hugging Face and Cerebras bring Gemma 4 to real-time voice AI
blurb: Hugging Face and Cerebras released an open speech-to-speech pipeline that runs Google's Gemma 4 31B on Cerebras hardware for real-time voice AI. The fully modular stack chains Nvidia's Parakeet for speech recognition, Gemma 4 for inference, and Alibaba's Qwen3TTS for voice output, and already powers more than 9,000 Reachy Mini robots in the wild.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Researchers at Zafran Security disclosed DifyTap, a set of four vulnerabilities in the open source LLMOps platform Dify that let attackers read other tenants' private chats, files, and documents in multi-tenant deployments. Dify, which powers over 1 million applications, patched all four flaws in version 1.14.2, and the two most severe carry CVSS scores of 9.1 and 9.4.

title: DHS confirms hackers breached HSIN info-sharing platform
blurb: DHS confirmed hackers breached the Homeland Security Information Network, an unclassified platform for sharing sensitive information among federal, state, local, and private-sector partners, in an intrusion dated to late May or early June. The department isolated the affected systems and found no impact to classified networks, but with the US overseeing World Cup security, the breach raises concerns that interagency planning and response procedures were exposed.

title: What's new in Git 2.55.0?
blurb: Git 2.55.0 adds a `git history fixup` subcommand that amends staged changes into an existing commit in one step, and automatically rebases any stacked branches that contain it. The release also brings the built-in fsmonitor daemon to Linux via inotify, speeding up `git status` in large monorepos without a third-party tool like Watchman.

title: After spooking Trump into safety testing, Anthropic AI models get global release
blurb: The US lifted export curbs on Anthropic's newest Claude models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, roughly three weeks after the Trump administration flagged them as national security risks. Fable 5 is now available globally and US organizations regained Mythos 5 access on June 26, after Anthropic agreed to expand its government partnership, red-team the models with outside hackers, and staff a 24/7 team monitoring jailbreak threats.

title: BNY Adds USDC to Institutional Custody Platform in Expanded Circle Partnership
blurb: BNY, the world's largest custodian with $59.4T under custody, made USDC the first stablecoin supported on its Digital Asset Custody platform, letting institutional clients hold, mint, and redeem Circle's token alongside traditional assets. The move follows July 2025's GENIUS Act, which authorized federally chartered banks to custody payment stablecoins, and BNY plans to add more issuers over time.

title: BlackRock Adds Ethena's Synthetic Dollar to Its $20T Aladdin Risk Management Platform
blurb: BlackRock added Ethena's USDe synthetic dollar as an approved digital asset on Aladdin, its institutional risk platform used to manage more than $20T across asset managers, banks, insurers, and pension funds. The deal makes BlackRock's tokenized Treasury fund BUIDL the primary backing asset for Ethena's whitelabel stablecoins, marking the deepest tie yet between the world's largest asset manager and a DeFi protocol.

title: Azure CLI Password Spray Hits at Least 78 Microsoft Accounts in 81M+ Attempts
blurb: Researchers at Huntress flagged an automated password spray campaign that made over 81M login attempts against Microsoft Azure CLI, compromising at least 78 accounts across 64 organizations between June 12 and June 26. The attack abused ROPC, a deprecated OAuth flow, to bypass Conditional Access policies at organizations that had them enabled, exposing a legacy authentication gap that survives even MFA-adjacent protections.

title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
blurb: Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions that hid their payloads inside image and font files using steganography, tracing the campaign, dubbed StegoAd, to a single threat actor active since at least 2021. The extensions posed as ad blockers, VPNs, and translators across an install base of up to 2.6 million, staying dormant through evasion checks before stealing credentials and running ad fraud.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 at a steep discount to its top model as the company races toward a blockbuster IPO
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a mid-tier model that scores 63.2% on SWE-bench Pro and 80.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.1, closing most of the gap to its flagship Opus 4.8 while costing roughly 40% less per token. Introductory API pricing runs $2 per million input tokens and $10 output through August 31, as the company builds the broad developer adoption it will want to show ahead of a planned IPO.

title: Anthropic to restore Claude Fable access on Wednesday
blurb: Anthropic will begin restoring access to Claude Fable 5 on Wednesday after the Department of Commerce lifted export controls on its two most powerful models, though Mythos 5 will stay limited to select companies. The company is also rolling out identity verification through partner Persona for certain Claude capabilities, requiring a government photo ID and live selfie to confirm who is using its most powerful systems.

title: Zluda 6 release (run unmodified CUDA applications on non-Nvidia GPUs)
blurb: ZLUDA 6 shipped, letting unmodified CUDA applications run on non-Nvidia GPUs, with new support for PhysX and Blender textures plus improved Windows and ML workloads. The release adds 32-bit PhysX support that lets AMD GPU owners hit higher frame rates and unlock effects like debris and flame in older games such as Mafia II, though some workloads remain glitchy or unmerged.

title: Robinhood rolls out public blockchain as it expands deeper into crypto
blurb: Robinhood launched the public mainnet for Robinhood Chain, an Arbitrum-based Layer 2 that brings tokenized stock trading live in more than 120 countries and adds Robinhood Earn, a lending product paying an estimated 7% yield on its USDG stablecoin. The move pushes Robinhood past its brokerage roots into onchain finance, alongside expanded perpetual futures in Europe, UK crypto trading, and AI-powered Agentic Accounts for US users.

title: Armadin details full sandbox escape in Claude Cowork but Anthropic disputes risk
blurb: Security researchers at Armadin detailed an attack chain that escapes the sandbox behind Anthropic's Claude Cowork, running arbitrary commands as root and stripping the network restrictions meant to contain it. Anthropic disputes that it counts as a security issue, arguing the chain requires an attacker to already have local code execution on the host, and declined to treat the March report as a vulnerability.

title: LongCat-2.0, a large-scale MoE model with 1.6T total and 48B Active
blurb: LongCat-2.0 is a large-scale mixture-of-experts model with 1.6T total parameters and 48B active per token. The sparse design routes each token to a small fraction of experts, keeping inference costs closer to a 48B dense model while the larger parameter pool aims to lift capability.

title: Meta’s AI Storage Blueprint at Scale
blurb: Meta detailed how its BLOB-storage architecture evolved to keep exabyte-scale AI training data flowing fast enough to avoid GPU stalls, the primary bottleneck as compute performance outpaces storage growth. The post explains how Meta layers global BLOB storage on Tectonic, its regional erasure-coded block fabric, to maximize GPU utilization and speed up research iteration across geo-distributed clusters and massive datasets.

title: [AINews] Sonnet 5 today, and Fable 5 tomorrow
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5 as its new default mid-tier model, offering top-tier coding and tool-use performance at Sonnet pricing with a 1M-token context window and a promotional rate of $2/M input and $10/M output through August. Early benchmarks tempered the excitement, as tokenizer changes and 3-6x more turn-taking raised questions about the model's real-world efficiency despite its stronger agentic planning.

title: Apple Seeks to Buy Chinese-Made Memory Chips With Lobbying Push
blurb: Apple is negotiating to buy memory chips from two Chinese semiconductor makers on the Pentagon's blacklist, aiming to ease a global memory shortage that has pushed it to raise prices across its product line. The talks would deepen Apple's exposure to blacklisted suppliers as it lobbies Washington, underscoring how severe the DRAM and NAND crunch has become for hardware makers.

title: US reverses export restrictions on Anthropic’s Fable 5, Mythos 5 AI models
blurb: The US government lifted export restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models on June 30, ending nearly three weeks of disruption that began June 12 over concerns about the models' cybersecurity capabilities. Anthropic will roll out Fable 5 globally starting July 1 across Claude Platform, Claude.ai, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork, with AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Foundry access restored soon after.

title: Realta Fusion generates electricity directly from a fusion reaction, an apparent first
blurb: Realta Fusion powered a lightbulb using electricity harvested directly from the alpha particles produced by its WHAM demonstration reactor, which the Wisconsin startup calls a private-sector first. Direct energy conversion runs about 90% efficient versus 33% for the steam turbines in today's fission plants, and Realta plans to recirculate that electricity to heat its plasma and reach profitability faster.

title: Anthropic Reaches Deal With Trump Administration to Restore Access to Fable AI Model
blurb: Anthropic reached a deal with the Trump administration to restore access to its Fable AI model, which had been shut down for 2½ weeks over security concerns. The outage rippled through the AI industry, and the agreement ends a standoff that had cut off a widely used frontier model while regulators and the company worked through the government's objections.

title: China’s Meituan open-sources massive LongCat-2.0 AI model, saying it was trained on domestic chips
blurb: Meituan open-sourced LongCat-2.0, a 1.6-trillion-parameter mixture-of-experts model with a 1M-token context window that the company says was trained entirely on domestic Chinese chips and compute clusters. Meituan positions it as an agentic and coding "brain" benchmarked against GPT-5.5, Gemini, and Claude Opus, but the headline is the hardware: a frontier-scale open model built without US silicon.

title: China’s hollow-core fiber trial pushes 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles without signal regeneration — milestone targets AI-era networking bottlenecks
blurb: Chinese firm YOFC, with China Telecom and Dekoli, completed the first field trial of hollow-core fiber moving 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles (206.5 km) without signal regeneration, hitting 1.2 Tb/s per wavelength using only standard erbium-doped amplifiers. Hollow-core fiber guides light through air rather than glass, cutting latency and distortion, and the result advances a high-capacity networking technology largely developed outside the Western supply chain.

title: ZCode: Claude Code from the Makers of GLM
blurb: Z.ai released ZCode, a Claude Code-style coding agent from the team behind the GLM models. The agent is tuned for GLM-5.2 across reasoning, coding, and multi-agent coordination, supports 20+ development tools, and can be triggered remotely through WeChat, Feishu, or Telegram, with tiered GLM Coding plans ranging from a lightweight Lite tier to a 20x-quota option.

title: MCP Server Architecture Patterns for LLM-Integrated Applications
blurb: This paper catalogues five recurring MCP server architecture patterns observed across fifteen production and public servers, documenting each with context, problem, solution, and consequences alongside four anti-patterns and cross-cutting concerns for auth, versioning, and observability. A tool-count study found selection accuracy drops below 90% at 10 to 15 tools per context for Claude Haiku 4.5 and 20 to 30 tools for Sonnet 4, with code and corpus released as a replication package.

title: The first early human eggs from stem cells
blurb: Conception, a biotech startup, reported early progress toward making human eggs from stem cells, converting blood-derived iPSCs into lab-grown "mini-ovaries" that mimic a real human ovary. The work targets in vitro gametogenesis, a technique already proven in mice, that could one day produce viable eggs from a blood draw without hormone injections or surgical retrieval.

title: Pollen (CEO Negus-Fancey, CTO Wright) tried to remove article, and Google helped
blurb: Pragmatic Engineer author Gergely Orosz reports that Google removed his 2022 investigation of failed events startup Pollen from search results after a copyright claim he says is bogus, since he is the sole copyright holder. The case shows how anyone can file a fraudulent DMCA notice to scrub unflattering coverage from Google's index, leaving the actual author to fight a slow appeals process.

title: NixOS 26.05
blurb: NixOS 26.05 "Yarara" is out, built from 59,703 commits by 2,842 contributors and adding 20,442 new packages to Nixpkgs. The release switches initrd to a systemd-based stage 1 by default, updates GNOME to 50 and GCC to 15, and marks the final release to support x86_64-darwin before Apple's platform deprecation takes effect.

title: Introducing Computer Use on Gemini 3.5 Flash
blurb: Google integrated computer use natively into Gemini 3.5 Flash, letting developers build agents that see, reason, and act across browser, mobile, and desktop environments. The capability was previously a standalone Gemini 2.5 model, and Google is shipping optional enterprise safeguards alongside it, including user confirmation for irreversible actions and automatic task halts when prompt injection is detected.

title: India’s central bank mandated use of .bank domains to enhance trust – but its registry leaked sensitive info
blurb: India's central bank mandated .bank domains to boost trust, but the registry itself leaked sensitive information.

title: New attack provides one more reason why AI browsers are a bad idea
blurb: Researchers at LayerX demonstrated an attack that tricks AI browsers into a fictional "dream world" where their safety guardrails no longer apply, then uses that state to extract code from private repositories or credentials from the built-in password manager. The proof-of-concept feeds the browser's LLM a puzzle that rewards wrong answers like 2+2=5, showing that reactive guardrails treat symptoms rather than the root risk of letting models take sensitive actions on untrusted sites.

title: Google Patches 382 Chrome Vulnerabilities
blurb: Google released Chrome 151 with patches for 382 vulnerabilities, 358 of which the company found itself, including 15 rated critical and 67 high severity. Google attributes the recent surge in discoveries to AI without naming the tools involved, and its advisory reports no in-the-wild exploitation, though the company patched its fifth actively exploited zero-day of 2026 earlier this month.

title: iOS 26.5.2 has fixes for 25+ security issues on iPhone, details here
blurb: Apple released iOS 26.5.2, iPadOS 26.5.2, and macOS 26.5.2, patching nearly 30 security issues with no new features. Most of the fixes address WebKit and WebRTC, alongside several Kernel-related vulnerabilities, and were first shipped in the iOS 26.6 betas before rolling out to all users.

title: 🔬 The Coolest Diffusion Research Isn't in LLMs — Evan Feinberg & Sergey Edunov, Genesis Molecular AI
blurb: In this Latent Space episode, Genesis Molecular AI co-founder Evan Feinberg and CTO Sergey Edunov argue that the most innovative diffusion research is happening in 3D molecular structure prediction, not LLMs. They detail how the company's flagship model PEARL routinely clears the accuracy threshold needed for real-world small molecule drug discovery, enabling new agentic workflows that earlier ML-for-drug-discovery efforts could not support.

title: Your site, your rules: new AI traffic options for all customers
blurb: Cloudflare expanded its Content Independence Day tools, giving all customers more granular control over how AI bots crawl, store, and reshare their content instead of a blanket block-or-allow choice. The new approach classifies bots by three use cases (search, training, and AI answers) rather than labeling them simply "AI," letting site owners protect and monetize content while staying discoverable.

title: 'Phantom Squatting': An Emerging AI-Driven Supply Chain Threat
blurb: Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 detailed "phantom squatting," an attack that exploits how LLMs consistently hallucinate plausible but unregistered domains for legitimate brands, letting attackers register those domains to intercept AI-directed traffic. Analyzing 913 brands across 685,339 URL queries, researchers found LLMs generated 250,000 hallucinated domains, and one attacker used an AI coding assistant to build a full phishing kit for a flagged phantom domain.

title: Sources: SoftBank has reopened talks for a $10B loan backed by its OpenAI stake and is offering to guarantee repayment if OpenAI collateral proves insufficient (Echo Wang/Reuters)
blurb: SoftBank has reopened talks for a $10B loan backed by its OpenAI stake, and is offering to guarantee repayment if the OpenAI collateral proves insufficient, according to Reuters. The unusual personal-style guarantee signals how eager SoftBank is to secure financing tied to its OpenAI bet, and how cautious lenders remain about valuing the stake as collateral.

title: Steamroller becomes first prebuilt gaming PC to ship with SteamOS — Ryzen 9600X, Radeon RX 7600, 16GB DDR5 RAM system available for preorder at $1,299
blurb: Meta PCs opened preorders for Steamroller, the first prebuilt gaming PC to ship with Valve's SteamOS, pairing a 6-core Ryzen 9600X, Radeon RX 7600, 16GB of DDR5, and a 1TB NVMe SSD for $1,299, with shipping starting July 3. Unlike Valve's more compact Steam Machine, the Steamroller uses standard upgradeable PC parts but drops living-room features like HDMI CEC and quick resume.

title: Instagram is incorporating users' photos in ads for Meta Glasses
blurb: Meta is testing Instagram ads for its Ray-Ban smart glasses that pull in users' own photos to personalize the creative. The format stitches a person's existing images into glasses promotions, an early example of generative ads built from first-party account data and a likely preview of how Meta plans to use its image libraries to sell hardware.

title: Content Independence Day, one year on: building the business model for the agentic Internet
blurb: Cloudflare's one-year follow-up to Content Independence Day argues the Internet's business model has already shifted, reporting that agent traffic now exceeds 50% of all traffic and that users spend just 15 minutes on the open web for every hour searching. The company says its default block on AI training crawlers has helped seed a market where publishers can price and license content rather than watch referral traffic vanish.

title: Visa, Stripe and 140 others back new Open USD stablecoin to challenge Tether
blurb: A consortium of over 140 companies, including Visa, Stripe, and BlackRock, is backing a new stablecoin called Open USD to challenge market leaders Tether and Circle. Operated by independent issuer Open Standard, OUSD lets businesses mint and redeem with no fees and returns most reserve revenue to partners, breaking from the model where issuers keep the interest income. Circle shares fell about 13% on the news.

title: A native graphical shell for SSH
blurb: Outer Shell is an open-source graphical shell that lets servers and edge devices serve up a browser-based home screen of apps, each a small dependency-free HTTP server reached over SSH rather than exposed network ports. Apps communicate through Unix domain socket files with filesystem permissions and skip encryption entirely, since the SSH layer handles it, and can register capabilities so one app opens files in another.

title: Import AI 463: Self-improving robots; a 10k Chinese GPU cluster; and an elegiac essay for the human era
blurb: NVIDIA researchers built ENPIRE, a harness that puts physical robots through the same autonomous experiment-and-improve loop coding agents use, with modules for automatic resets, policy refinement, parallel rollouts, and agent-driven code evolution. The system handles trial scoring and scene resets without human judgment, narrowing two long-standing bottlenecks in real-world robot learning, though the authors frame the current results as suggestive rather than proven.

title: AWS funnels $1B into forward deployed engineering hub
blurb: AWS is investing $1B into a forward deployed engineering organization that embeds pods of five to six engineers alongside AI agents inside customer environments to build and deploy AI systems. The agentic-first model runs 45-day sprints and leaves agents behind to sustain the work, putting AWS in direct competition with Google and Microsoft, which are building similar FDE programs.

title: Highlights from Git 2.55
blurb: Git 2.55 shipped with contributions from over 100 developers, headlined by teaching git repack to write incremental multi-pack index chains directly via `--write-midx=incremental`. The append-only mode adds a new MIDX layer for freshly created packs without rewriting the full index, cutting maintenance costs for large repositories where a single-file MIDX update means a large write.

title: FFmpeg 9.1's new AAC encoder
blurb: FFmpeg 9.1 ships a rewritten native AAC encoder that listening tests rank at or above the long-standing Fraunhofer FDK reference, ending years of FFmpeg's built-in AAC being treated as a low-quality fallback. The new encoder is licensed under FFmpeg's own terms, so distributions can enable high-quality AAC without the patent and packaging friction that kept FDK out of most default builds.

title: Trump drops restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos and Fable models
blurb: The U.S. lifted the export license requirement on Anthropic's Mythos and Fable models, and the company will restore public access starting July 1. The reversal follows the June 12 restriction that forced Anthropic to pull the models entirely, and came as Asian labs shipped competing systems like Fugu and Tulongfeng, pressuring Washington to keep American AI globally available.

title: Internal Combustion Engine
blurb: Bartosz Ciechanowski published an interactive, 3D-animated explainer that builds an internal combustion engine from first principles, starting with a simple crank and a cannon before layering in pistons, valves, and timing. Every mechanism is a draggable, pausable animation, turning the usually opaque operation of engine parts into something you can inspect from any angle.

title: Supreme Court delivers ‘major win’ for tech privacy in Chatrie ruling
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Chatrie v. United States that police collecting phone location data from a geographic area constitutes a Fourth Amendment search, extending privacy protections to geofence warrants and the data users hand to companies like Google. Justice Kagan's majority opinion signaled that Fourth Amendment limits should keep pace as technology advances, a precedent that will constrain how law enforcement taps tech platforms for location records going forward.

title: An artificial cell with a full lifecycle has been created for the first time
blurb: Researchers built an artificial cell that completes a full lifecycle for the first time, including the ability to grow, function, and divide the way living cells do. The milestone moves synthetic biology past static cell mimics toward engineered cells that can sustain themselves, a prerequisite for using designed organisms in medicine, materials, and manufacturing.

title: Cursor releases iPhone and iPad app following recent acquisition by SpaceX
blurb: Cursor released its first iPhone and iPad app, letting developers launch coding agents, review diffs, merge pull requests, and talk to agents by voice from the App Store. The launch is Cursor's first major release since SpaceX, which includes xAI, acquired the agentic coding firm earlier this month, and it comes with 75% off Composer 2.5 runs through July 5.

title: Venice AI becomes a unicorn with $65M Series A as its privacy-first AI platform takes off
blurb: Venice AI raised a $65M Series A at a $1B valuation, its first outside funding, led by crypto firm Dragonfly with Coinbase Ventures participating. The privacy-first platform routes encrypted queries to over 200 open and closed-source models with no data stored on its servers, and is already profitable on more than $70M in annualized run-rate revenue.

title: Anthropic's new Sonnet 5 model is better at the tasks that are running up enterprise bills
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a model tuned for agentic workloads that matches recent Opus performance at lower prices. Starting September 1 it will cost $3 per million input tokens and $15 per million output, against Opus 4.8's $4 and $25, and a new tokenizer targets the token-heavy agent traffic that has been running up enterprise bills.

title: Prime Day shows how AI is changing shopping, testing Amazon’s bet against ChatGPT and others
blurb: U.S. shoppers spent a record $26.4B across all retail sites during Amazon's four-day Prime Day, and for the first time visitors arriving from AI chatbots were the most likely to buy, converting 40% better than those from search, email, or social, per Adobe. The shift tests Amazon's strategy of locking outside assistants like ChatGPT and Perplexity out of its store while pushing shoppers toward its own Rufus assistant.

title: Crunchbase Data: Q2 Brought The Most Billion-Dollar Startup Exits Since 2021
blurb: Q2 2026 produced the most billion-dollar startup exits since the 2021 market peak, led by SpaceX's record IPO that raised $75B and hit a $2.1T first-day market cap. The bigger trend is size over count, with SpaceX's $60B purchase of Cursor marking the priciest venture-backed acquisition ever and confidential IPO filings from Anthropic and OpenAI now testing the trillion-dollar mark.

title: Apple’s Hide My Email feature has a bug that’s been exposing real email addresses, researcher claims
blurb: A researcher says a bug in Apple's Hide My Email exposes the real addresses behind its disposable aliases, with 100% of tested addresses exploitable in limited trials. Researcher Tyler Murphy reported the flaw to Apple over a year ago and says it remains unpatched, undercutting a privacy feature central to the company's brand.

title: Trump Administration Asks OpenAI to Stagger AI Model Release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of an upcoming powerful AI model, according to a person familiar with the matter. The request comes nearly two weeks after rival Anthropic pulled its most capable offerings from the market under regulatory pressure, signaling that Washington is now shaping the pace at which frontier models reach customers.

title: Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing
blurb: GitHub detailed how Copilot stretches each token through prompt caching and on-demand tool search, plus expanding Auto to pick the model per task.

title: Google is rationing Gemini access to Meta because it cannot provide enough compute
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models because it cannot supply enough compute, forcing Meta to tell staff to use AI tokens more efficiently, the Financial Times reported. Meta is accelerating its shift to Muse Spark, an internal model under Superintelligence Labs, even as Google itself rents 110,000 Nvidia GPUs from SpaceX for $920M a month to meet its own Gemini demand.

title: Rocket Lab buys its way into the satellite big league with $8B Iridium deal
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to acquire satellite operator Iridium in a deal valued at roughly $8B, vaulting the launch company into the operational satellite-services business. The acquisition pairs Rocket Lab's launch vehicles and spacecraft manufacturing with Iridium's 80-plus satellite constellation and recurring connectivity revenue, moving the company beyond launch into owning and running an orbital network.

title: Visa, Mastercard and Coinbase have launched a new global stablecoin
blurb: Visa, Mastercard, and Coinbase are leading a 140-business consortium called Open Standard to launch Open USD, a dollar-pegged stablecoin due later this year. The venture follows Trump's GENIUS Act, which requires 1:1 reserves and consumer protections, though research still shows stablecoins are used mostly for crypto trading rather than everyday purchases.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix to Spend $520 Billion on Chip Plants in South Korea
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix will spend a combined $520B on new chip plants in South Korea, saying current investment levels can't meet demand amid a global shortage. The world's two largest memory-chip makers are racing to expand capacity as AI workloads strain supply, with the buildout reinforcing South Korea's central role in the memory market.

title: US offers $10 million to identify hackers targeting Signal and WhatsApp users
blurb: The State Department is offering a $10M bounty for information identifying members of two Russian state-backed groups running a phishing campaign that has compromised thousands of Signal and WhatsApp accounts. The FBI attributes the attacks to UNC5792 and UNC4221, tied to Russian intelligence services, which trick high-value targets into linking attacker devices or handing over recovery keys.

title: WATaBoy: JIT-Ing Game Boy Instructions to WASM Beats a Native Interpreter
blurb: WATaBoy is a Game Boy emulator that JIT-compiles CPU instructions to WebAssembly bytecode, letting the browser's engine recompile it to native machine code and sidestepping iOS's ban on direct JIT outside web browsers. Built as an undergraduate final-year project, it benchmarks the JIT-to-Wasm approach against a native interpreter and comes out faster, the first console emulator to test the technique against an interpreter running natively.

title: Security researchers tricked LLMs into giving them cocaine recipes by abusing role models for prompt injection
blurb: Security researchers bypassed LLM safety guardrails to extract cocaine recipes by exploiting role-play prompts for injection attacks.

title: Rust introduces new Range types
blurb: Rust 1.96.0 stabilizes new `core::range::Range` types that implement `IntoIterator` instead of `Iterator`, letting range types also be `Copy`. This removes the long-standing footgun of implementing both `Iterator` and `Copy` on the same type, and lets developers store slice accessors in `Copy` types without splitting start and end. The release also adds `assert_matches!` macros and makes undefined symbols a hard linker error on WebAssembly targets.

title: How We Made IPFS Content Publishing 10x Faster
blurb: IPFS's Kubo 0.39.0 ships Optimistic Provide by default, cutting DHT content-publish latency from 13-20 seconds to under 1 second.

title: Sources: SpaceX showed investors a handset-like device prototype with AI tech from xAI, a proprietary OS, a Snapdragon chip, and a design slimmer than an iPhone (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: SpaceX showed investors a prototype smartphone-like device built with xAI's AI technology, a proprietary operating system, a Qualcomm Snapdragon chip, and a body slimmer than an iPhone, according to the Wall Street Journal. The device would deepen ties between Elon Musk's companies and put SpaceX in direct competition with Apple and Samsung, backed by its Starlink satellite network for connectivity.

title: Cursor releases its iOS app for vibe coding on the go
blurb: Cursor released a native iOS app in public beta that turns the phone into a control surface for its coding agents, letting developers launch agents, review diffs, issue voice and slash commands, and merge pull requests from mobile. The app requires iOS 26 and a paid Cursor plan, supports models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Cursor's own Composer, and can run cloud agents in isolated VMs or remote-control sessions on a local machine.

title: Databricks’ Co-Founder Arsalan Tavakoli: Every Software Monopoly Falls in the Next 24 Months
blurb: Databricks co-founder Arsalan Tavakoli told the SaaStr AI stage that any business holding a software monopoly today will lose it within 12 to 24 months, citing collapsing pricing power from cheaper builds, low-end entrants, and easier migrations. He spoke from a $6.9B revenue run-rate growing 80% a year, and argued enterprises are "token maxing" with rising AI spend but no clear read on ROI.

title: US Lifts Export Restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5
blurb: The US lifted foreign access restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5 model after the company resolved the government's safety concerns, clearing the frontier model for export. The reversal signals that Washington's approach to regulating advanced AI now hinges on case-by-case safety reviews rather than blanket export bans, giving labs a defined path to sell their strongest models abroad.

title: Cloudflare’s new policy pushes AI companies to pay for publishers’ content
blurb: Cloudflare will block "mixed-use" web crawlers from ad-hosting pages by default starting September 15, 2026, unless site owners opt out. The change targets bots that blend search, AI training, and agent use, pressuring AI companies to separate those functions and pay publishers rather than harvest content for free.

title: Microsoft accelerates quantum-safe roadmap as risks grow
blurb: Microsoft is accelerating its Quantum Safe Program, committing to transition critical products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029 as it warns that cryptographically relevant quantum computers may arrive sooner than expected. The move responds to "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks and follows Apple, Google, and Signal in adopting PQC, with Microsoft urging organizations to modernize infrastructure and build crypto-agility now.

title: Microsoft Adds New Teams Controls to Block Unauthorized AI Bots From Meetings
blurb: Microsoft added a Teams admin policy that detects external AI bots, routes them to the lobby, and requires organizer approval before they join meetings.

title: Microsoft previews Linux containers that run in Windows
blurb: Microsoft is previewing a feature that runs Linux containers natively inside Windows, currently available as an early technical preview. The approach uses a lightweight virtualization layer to launch Linux workloads alongside Windows without a full separate VM, targeting developers who build cross-platform apps and want a smoother local container workflow.

title: Deno update streamlines creation of desktop apps
blurb: Deno 2.9 introduces an experimental deno desktop feature that compiles a script or web framework project into a single self-contained native binary, with the UI running in a webview and logic in Deno. The release also cuts hello-world cold start from 34ms to 17ms, holds memory roughly flat under load, speeds up Deno.serve throughput, and advances Node.js compatibility to version 26.

title: Unmasking the crawls with Attribution Business Insights
blurb: Cloudflare launched Attribution Business Insights, a dashboard that shows publishers which AI crawlers drive referral traffic versus which scrape content without sending readers back.

title: What the Saga Over Anthropic’s Mythos Tells Us About the Cyber Risks From AI
blurb: Anthropic has restricted its Mythos AI model to just 200 partner organizations, saying the tool is too effective at finding software vulnerabilities to release publicly. The company warns that in the wrong hands, Mythos could help attackers steal data or disrupt critical infrastructure, framing the limited rollout as a test of how labs handle dual-use security tools.

title: Making AI search smarter
blurb: Cloudflare announced new bot controls and a plan to build a search-era economic model that pays creators when AI answer engines use their content. The move responds to AI summaries gutting referral traffic: a 2025 Pew study found users clicked a traditional search link just 8% of the time when Google showed an AI summary, and a link inside the summary only 1% of the time.

title: Memory Safe Context Switching
blurb: Fil-C now supports the ucontext APIs (getcontext, setcontext, makecontext, swapcontext) and longjmp/setjmp in a memory-safe way, so misuse that would corrupt the stack in ordinary C instead panics the program. Restoring a dangling stack, freeing a stack still referenced by a context, or swapping into the currently executing context all become safety errors rather than the silent, untraceable crashes that attackers can exploit.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets Claude act on a user's behalf across an entire channel. The tool monitors channel activity, sends preset alerts about posts that may affect the user's day, drops comments into conversations, and can be directed to fix code issues, pushing Claude further into persistent workplace-agent territory.

title: We’re making Bunny DNS free
blurb: Bunny.net eliminated all DNS query fees, offering free hosting for up to 500 domains with no query limits or per-request billing.

title: Together AI raises $800M to grow its AI-optimized public cloud
blurb: Together AI raised $800M in a Series C led by Aramco Ventures, with Nvidia, Vista Equity Partners, and General Catalyst participating, valuing the open-model cloud provider at $8.3B. The company runs serverless, dedicated, and batch inference on Nvidia chips accelerated by ATLAS, a speculative-decoding engine it claims can speed some workloads by 400%, and it will use the funding to expand its AI-optimized public cloud.

title: Red teamers turned Claude Desktop into a double agent to do their evil bidding
blurb: Pentera Labs red teamers compromised a developer's Claude Desktop app and escalated the access into full remote code execution on the victim's machine. The attack chain starts with a hijacked email inbox connected to the user's Claude account, then abuses the desktop app's cross-device sync and local code-execution features to run commands the victim never sees.

title: Neocloud Together AI raises $800M, leaps to $8.3B valuation
blurb: Together AI raised an $800M Series C at an $8.3B valuation led by Aramco Ventures, more than doubling the $3.3B valuation from its Series B 16 months ago. The neocloud rents Nvidia GPU clusters to run cheaper open source models and reports over $1.15B in annual bookings, with Cursor, Cognition, and Decagon among its customers.

title: Linux for the Sega MegaDrive
blurb: Linux runs on a Sega MegaDrive using a Mega EverDrive cartridge for RAM and SD-card file access.

title: Apple takes Epic fight over app store fees to the Supreme Court
blurb: Apple asked the Supreme Court to overturn a contempt ruling that bars it from charging commissions when iPhone developers link users to outside payment methods, following a 9th Circuit finding that its 27% link-out fee violated the "spirit" of an earlier App Store injunction. The Court agreed to hear the case, which turns on whether civil contempt can rest on an injunction's spirit or only its explicit terms.

title: Anthropic reactivates Fable, Mythos after securing government approval
blurb: Anthropic reactivated its Fable and Mythos frontier models after clearing a government approval process that had kept them offline. The company brought the models back online despite unresolved vetting concerns, underscoring the tension between regulatory sign-off and the deeper safety questions that gated the systems in the first place.

title: Restaurants can now accept orders placed directly from ChatGPT and Claude thanks to Square's new, low-fee, no setup integration
blurb: Square launched a ChatGPT app and Claude plugin that let customers discover restaurants and place orders directly inside those AI tools, with no technical setup required from merchants. The integration pulls live pricing, modifiers, and stock from the Square catalog and skips the marketplace commissions that DoorDash and Uber Eats charge, though standard online-ordering fees of 2.9% to 3.3% plus $0.30 still apply.

title: Meta wants to rent out its spare AI compute, and Wall Street likes the idea
blurb: Meta is reportedly building a cloud business called Meta Compute to rent out surplus AI capacity, weighing whether to sell hosted-model access like Amazon Bedrock or raw compute like CoreWeave. Either path pits it against AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure, and offers a way to earn back some of its $115B to $135B in planned 2026 capex, news that sent Meta shares up more than 10%.

title: Record chip rally adds $2 trillion in combined value to Micron, Intel and AMD in second quarter
blurb: Micron, Intel, and AMD added roughly $2 trillion in combined market cap in the second quarter, with Micron and Intel more than tripling and AMD close behind. Barclays attributes the surge to a rotation out of AI hyperscalers into chip suppliers, as investors bet that data-center capex will lift companies beyond Nvidia, whose stock rose only 15% in the period.

title: Mag 7 value shrinks by $2.3 trillion amid AI spending jitters — but investors are still backing chipmakers
blurb: About $2.3T has been wiped off the Magnificent 7 this month as investors scrutinize the hundreds of billions Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta are spending on AI chips and data centers. The CNBC Mag 7 Index is down 10% in June, but semiconductor stocks have held up, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index rising 6% as money rotates toward chipmakers.

title: Digital Realty falls 5% after taking $3.5 billion stake in Blackstone's Virginia data centers
blurb: Digital Realty bought an $3.5B stake in three Northern Virginia data centers from Blackstone, paying $1.2B in cash and $2.3B in shares for assets valued at $7.8B. The stock fell 5.4% premarket on the dilution, even as the deal deepens its exposure to the world's largest data center market, where 92% of capacity under construction is already pre-leased.

title: SpudCell: Scientists Made a Cell With Most of the Hallmarks of Life. Here’s What to Know.
blurb: Scientists created SpudCell, a synthetic cell that exhibits most of the recognized hallmarks of life, marking a new milestone in synthetic biology. The advance raises unresolved questions about whether an engineered cell counts as truly alive, and points toward future applications in engineered organisms and cellular research.

title: Inference chip startup Etched launches with $800M in funding
blurb: Etched launched with $800M raised across multiple rounds, most recently at a $5B valuation backed by TSMC-affiliated VentureTech Alliance plus Geoffrey Hinton, Fei-Fei Li, and Andrej Karpathy. The startup builds inference-only chips on TSMC's N4P process, ships them in a rack-scale liquid-cooled appliance, and claims LVI technology lets its silicon run trillion-parameter models at 80%+ peak FLOPs without thermal throttling.

title: AWS launches forward-deployed engineering team to speed enterprise agentic AI adoption
blurb: AWS launched a Forward Deployed Engineering department, backed by a $1B investment, that embeds small "frontier teams" of engineers and AI agents inside enterprise customers to build agentic systems on the ground. The group operates on a "45/45/45" cadence, aiming to ideate in 45 minutes, validate in 45 hours, and ship in 45 days, while leaving customers the tools it used.

title: Gemini Spark updates: macOS launch, connected apps and more
blurb: Google is bringing Gemini Spark to macOS, letting the assistant automate tasks across desktop files and Google Workspace, like sorting Downloads or building spreadsheets from local invoices. The beta is limited to Google AI Ultra subscribers in the US, and Google also added connected apps including Canva, Dropbox, Instacart, OpenTable, and Zillow Rentals, plus custom MCP support.

title: Anthropic restores AI models Fable, Mythos after the U.S. lifts export controls
blurb: Anthropic restored access to its Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models on June 30 after the U.S. government lifted export controls that had frozen them since June 12. The freeze followed an Amazon-discovered jailbreak that let Fable 5 generate exploit code, and Anthropic is now giving designated U.S. agencies early access to frontier models and their safeguards before public release.

title: SCOTUS says detailed cellphone location data is protected by the Fourth Amendment
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that police conduct a Fourth Amendment search when they obtain a person's detailed cellphone location history from a tech company, meaning a warrant backed by probable cause is generally required even for short time spans or third-party-held data. The decision does not ban geofence warrants, but it extends constitutional protection beyond the Google case to comparable location records held by Apple and other companies.

title: Apple just acquired the app that won last year’s ‘Innovation’ Apple Design Award
blurb: Apple acquired Play, the SwiftUI prototyping tool from Rabbit 3 Times that won the 2025 Apple Design Award for Innovation, picking up assets and hiring select staff. Play has been pulled from the App Store, with its real-time Mac and iPhone prototyping features likely to surface inside Xcode or Apple's free developer tools over time.

title: Alibaba, Payment Firm Will Pay $600 Million to Resolve US Probe
blurb: Alibaba and its US payment processor agreed to pay $600 million to resolve a Justice Department investigation into whether they failed to prevent the sale and importation of illegal pharmaceuticals and controlled substances. The settlement closes a federal probe for the Chinese tech giant without an admission tied to the platform's role in facilitating banned drug transactions.

title: Meta Is Planning Cloud Business to Take on Amazon, Google
blurb: Meta is developing a cloud infrastructure business to sell access to AI computing power and models, putting it in direct competition with AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud. The move would turn Meta's massive internal AI buildout into an external revenue stream, mirroring how AWS grew out of Amazon's own infrastructure spending.

title: Antares achieves criticality of Mark-0 reactor
blurb: Antares brought its Mark-0 microreactor to initial criticality at Idaho National Laboratory, becoming the first private company to reach criticality under the DOE Reactor Pilot Program and the first US non-light-water reactor to do so in over four decades. The demonstration, run with DOE, BWXT, and the US Army, validates reactor physics for Antares and feeds verification data into the military's Project Pele, ahead of targeted electricity production in 2027.

title: OpenAI Leans Toward Holding Up I.P.O. Until Next Year
blurb: OpenAI is leaning toward delaying its IPO until next year, with advisers urging Sam Altman to slow down after watching SpaceX's stock turn volatile. The caution comes as the company works through financial challenges, suggesting public markets remain wary of richly valued AI startups despite OpenAI's growth.

title: Microsoft builds a bouncer to keep bots out of Teams meetings
blurb: Microsoft is adding controls to Teams that block unauthorized AI notetaker bots from silently joining and transcribing meetings.

title: Finance App Network Maker Plaid Is Said to Consider US IPO
blurb: Plaid is considering a US IPO, according to people familiar with the matter, marking a potential public debut for the fintech infrastructure company whose network connects apps to customer bank accounts. Plaid was last valued at $6.1B in a 2025 employee tender offer, down from its $13.4B pandemic-era peak after a blocked Visa acquisition.

title: Wayve launches $85M employee tender offer at $8.5B valuation
blurb: Wayve launched an $85M tender offer letting employees sell vested equity at the company's $8.5B valuation, set in February by its $1.2B Series D. The U.K. self-driving startup is using the liquidity event as a retention tool, a growing trend among AI companies, as it targets Uber robotaxi pilots later this year and integration into Nissan's next-generation driver-assist systems.

title: SpaceX Showed Investors Prototype of Elon Musk’s New AI Device
blurb: SpaceX showed investors a prototype of a new AI device built around models from Elon Musk's xAI. The sleek, handset-like design points to a hardware push that would put Musk in direct competition with smartphones and the AI gadgets being pursued by OpenAI and Jony Ive.

title: Sony Deletes 551 Movies PlayStation Owners Paid For
blurb: Sony will delete 551 StudioCanal movies and shows, including Terminator 2, Rambo, and The Deer Hunter, from the PlayStation Store libraries of customers who bought them, effective September 1, citing expired content licensing agreements. The company has said nothing about refunds, underscoring that "purchased" digital media remains a revocable license, a point reinforced by GTA 6's $80 disc-free boxed edition shipping only a download code.

title: Google kills Tenor GIF API, forcing changes at X, Discord, and more
blurb: Google shut down the Tenor GIF API on June 30, cutting off the searchable GIF library that platforms like X and Discord relied on to power their in-app pickers. Google announced the wind-down in January and stopped accepting new integrations then, saying it wanted to focus resources, though the free API had no clear path to revenue.

title: The Lab Mistake That Might Revolutionize Computing
blurb: Researchers built a neuromorphic device that mimics a biological neuron on a standard silicon chip, an accidental discovery that could sharply cut AI's energy use. The approach reproduces brain-like computing with far fewer transistors than existing CMOS designs, which need so many components per neuron that they cap system size. The brain is roughly a million times more energy efficient than GPUs at comparable tasks.

title: Xbox’s ‘reset’: all the news about Microsoft’s looming layoffs and studio closures
blurb: Microsoft is planning a July 6th wave of Xbox layoffs and studio closures as part of an "Xbox reset" citing a 3% accountability margin, higher console component costs, and an overextended studio system. The Verge reports Microsoft is weighing shutting at least five studios and canceling games like Blade, alongside recent moves including console price hikes and dropping new Call of Duty titles from Game Pass.

title: Anthropic launches AI drug discovery program, joining tech giants in betting on healthcare
blurb: Anthropic launched an internal drug discovery program focused on "neglected" diseases that traditional biopharma companies avoid, part of a push to build and sell AI tools through its new Claude Science product. Life sciences head Eric Kauderer-Abrams framed the effort as a way to work alongside drugmakers, though the company hasn't said what it would do if it finds a promising candidate.

title: SpaceX has an AI device prototype, and it sure sounds phone-ish
blurb: SpaceX showed investors a prototype of a handset-like AI device that reportedly runs a proprietary OS and integrates xAI technology, according to The Wall Street Journal, though Musk called the report "utterly false." The move would put SpaceX against OpenAI's Jony Ive project in a category still defined by the flameouts of Humane and Rabbit, backed by Tesla-adjacent manufacturing and Starlink's push into wireless.

title: Sony announces end of PlayStation discs, parts of digital store in the same day
blurb: Sony will stop producing physical PlayStation discs in January 2028, moving to a digital-only sales model where all new games ship through the PlayStation Store or digital retailer codes. Digital downloads already accounted for 78% of full-game purchases in Sony's last fiscal year, but the shift hardens a licensing-only model in which buyers hold a non-transferable personal license rather than owning the game.

title: Supreme Court agrees to hear Apple appeal over Epic Games ruling
blurb: The Supreme Court agreed to hear Apple's appeal of a contempt ruling that found the company willfully defied a 2021 order barring it from charging fees on purchases made through external links in App Store apps. The justices declined to review a second question over the injunction's worldwide scope, leaving Apple's commissions on outside purchases as the sole issue in a case that could reshape App Store economics.

title: US government allows Anthropic to redeploy its Mythos and Fable AI models
blurb: The Commerce Department lifted its export ban on Anthropic's Mythos 5 and Fable 5 AI models, and the company will begin restoring customer access on July 1. The reversal came after Anthropic agreed to proactively detect and address security risks flagged by Amazon and others, and follows an earlier partial clearance that returned Mythos 5 to roughly 100 critical-infrastructure organizations.

title: Microsoft admits a default Windows 11 feature is quietly gobbling up massive disk space
blurb: Microsoft confirmed a fix for a Windows 11 bug in which the default Capability Access Manager service silently consumed enormous amounts of disk space, with one user reporting 70GB lost to its CapabilityAccessManager.db-wal file. The patch ships in KB5095093 for versions 24H2 and 25H2 as a normal rollout, alongside faster File Explorer disk-image mounting and a swap from Tenor to GIPHY in the Emoji Panel.

title: Microsoft set for new round of job cuts next week, spanning Xbox, sales and consulting
blurb: Microsoft will cut thousands of jobs next week across Xbox, sales, and consulting, affecting under 2.5% of its roughly 220,000-person workforce. The reductions are smaller than last year's 15,000 cuts, softened by a first-ever voluntary retirement program, and come as the company spends over $100B annually on AI and cloud infrastructure.

title: South Korea to spend $1T on more memory chip production and humanoid robots
blurb: South Korea's government and top tech firms committed $1 trillion to flagship projects spanning memory chip fabs, AI data centers, and commercial humanoid robots by 2028. Samsung and SK Hynix account for $585 billion of that to build new plants and double DRAM output within five years, while Hyundai pushes to mass-produce Boston Dynamics robots for factory work.

title: Cloudflare Collaborates With Leading Browsers to Develop a Privacy-First Protocol For the Global Internet
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are developing PACT, a privacy-preserving protocol to verify human and bot traffic without invasive tracking.

title: Meta's plan to launch a cloud business eases the biggest overhang on the stock
blurb: Meta is preparing to launch a cloud infrastructure business that sells excess AI compute and models to outside customers, following a Bloomberg report confirmed Wednesday. The move addresses investor concerns over Meta's capex, projected to nearly double to $135B this year, by adding a revenue stream beyond ads. Shares jumped more than 9% to $617, among the S&P 500's biggest gainers.

title: Anthropic releases Claude Sonnet 5 with improved agentic capabilities
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, the successor to Sonnet 4.6, which lifts agentic coding scores to 63.2% from 58.1% while closing much of the gap with the pricier Opus 4.8. It rolls out today as the default for Free and Pro users at $2 per million input and $10 per million output tokens through August 31, after which pricing rises to $3 and $15.

title: Supreme Court rules constitutional privacy protections apply to geofence warrants
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that geofence warrants, which sweep up location data from every phone near a crime scene, constitute a search under the Fourth Amendment and require constitutional privacy protections. The majority rejected the argument that opting into Google's location history waives that protection, with Justice Kagan warning that such dragnet requests create a "virtual panopticon" exposing ordinary users to government surveillance.

title: Schneider Electric acquires Cognite for $3.1B in industrial AI push
blurb: Schneider Electric acquired industrial AI company Cognite for $3.1B in an all-cash deal to expand its AI software and industrial data business. Cognite's platform pairs a unified data model with agentic AI that cleans messy machine data and automates workflows, and Schneider will fold its Data Fusion and Atlas AI products into the Aveva CONNECT platform it already owns.

title: ByteDance construye en Brasil su mayor complejo de centros de datos fuera de China
blurb: ByteDance is building a $39B data center complex in Brazil, its largest outside China. The TikTok owner's project is the latest win in President Lula's push to attract Chinese investment, adding major compute capacity in Latin America as US-China tensions reshape where AI infrastructure gets built.

title: AOL Owner Bending Spoons Jumps 40% After $1.68 Billion IPO
blurb: Bending Spoons, the Italian app maker that owns AOL, Evernote, and WeTransfer, raised $1.68 billion in its IPO, with shares jumping 40% above the offering price on their debut. The listing values the company at roughly the level backers targeted and marks one of Europe's largest tech IPOs, giving Bending Spoons fresh capital to continue its strategy of acquiring and reviving established software brands.

title: Ashton Kutcher leaving Sound Ventures to launch new VC firm with Morgan Beller
blurb: Ashton Kutcher is leaving Sound Ventures, the firm he co-founded 11 years ago, to launch a new early-stage VC firm with Morgan Beller, formerly of NFX, Andreessen Horowitz, and Meta's Libra project. The unnamed fund will target AI infrastructure, energy, and deep tech, a shift from Sound's concentrated bets on category-leading labs like OpenAI and Anthropic toward the layer that powers them.

title: Oomwoo is a new open-source robot vacuum you can 3D print yourself, sidesteps cloud security risks by running fully offline — project combines Raspberry Pi, 2D LiDAR, and a 3D-printed chassis
blurb: Maker's Pet launched oomwoo, an open-source robot vacuum that owners 3D print and assemble themselves, running entirely offline on a Raspberry Pi with 2D LiDAR, ROS 2, and native Home Assistant integration. The hardware, firmware, and software are open under Apache 2.0, but the project is still at the request-for-comments stage with no build instructions yet and a first bill of materials targeted for mid-July.

title: Google Must Pay Nearly $2 Billion to Klarna in Antitrust Case
blurb: A Swedish court ordered Google to pay Klarna nearly $2 billion after ruling that it favored its own price-comparison service over the fintech's rival offering. The decision adds to Google's mounting antitrust losses in Europe, where regulators and courts have repeatedly penalized the company for using its search dominance to disadvantage competitors.

title: Bringing speed and strong cost performance to the market with Gemini Omni Flash and Nano Banana 2 Lite
blurb: Google added two models to its Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform: Nano Banana 2 Lite (Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite Image), now generally available as the fastest, cheapest model in the Nano Banana image-generation family, and Gemini Omni Flash, in public preview for video generation and conversational editing. Omni Flash handles character swaps, style transfers, and relighting via natural language, and natively generates synced audio with every video output.

title: How Cursor deploys AI inside the enterprise
blurb: Cursor's VP of Forward Deployed Engineering, Pauline Brunet, explained how her team embeds inside customers' systems to deploy AI coding agents across the full software development lifecycle. The role, which Cursor pitches as building an "AI software factory," now spans financial services, telecom, and semiconductor clients and reflects how frontier labs are staffing up to move enterprise AI from individual adoption to organization-wide production.

title: AWS puts $1 billion into new AI unit to embed engineers with customers, joining growing wave
blurb: AWS announced a $1 billion investment in a new Forward Deployed Engineering unit that embeds engineers directly inside customers to accelerate AI rollouts, seeded with thousands of FDEs working alongside AI agents. The move makes Amazon the first hyperscaler to formalize the model, following OpenAI and Anthropic's own FDE ventures with banks and PE firms earlier this year as vendors compete on deployment speed rather than model quality.

title: Waymo and Uber end robotaxi pilot in Phoenix
blurb: Waymo and Uber ended their robotaxi pilot in Phoenix, removing Waymo rides from the Uber app in the company's first joint market about a month ago. The two still partner exclusively in Austin and Atlanta, where hundreds of Waymo vehicles operate, while the Phoenix cars shift to autonomous DoorDash deliveries and Uber lines up an undisclosed new AV partner there.

title: Bending Spoons, Owner of AOL and Other Old Internet Brands, Is Going Public
blurb: Bending Spoons, the Italian company that acquires aging internet brands including AOL, Vimeo, and Eventbrite, is going public this week at a potential $19B valuation. The IPO tests investor appetite for a roll-up strategy built on buying past-their-prime services and cutting costs, rather than launching new products.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Science, a dedicated app for scientists
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Science, a desktop app that turns its Claude for Life Sciences offering into a research workspace with 60+ preloaded databases, native rendering of protein structures and genome tracks, and traceable analysis histories. Running on Opus 4.8, the beta is out today on macOS and Linux for paid tiers, alongside up to $30,000 in credits for 50 selected research projects.

title: AMD confirms low-power CPU cores in Linux kernel patch — Zen 6 chips could follow in Intel's footsteps with new core type for background tasks
blurb: AMD submitted Linux kernel patches adding a third CPU core type, low-power cores, alongside its existing performance and efficiency cores for handling background and idle tasks. The move mirrors Intel's low-power island approach and points to a three-tier heterogeneous design in future chips, though AMD has not confirmed whether the cores target Zen 5, Zen 6, or how they differ architecturally from today's Zen 5c dense cores.

title: Legacy Nvidia RTX 3060 12GB returns to retail five years after original launch, priced at $339 — resurrected GPU strategy that Jensen called a 'good idea' apparently comes to fruition
blurb: Nvidia's five-year-old RTX 3060 12GB is returning to retail at $339, just $10 above its 2021 launch price, as board partners including Gigabyte and Asus quietly restock the Ampere card. The move follows Jensen Huang's CES 2026 comment that reviving older GPUs on trailing-edge nodes could ease RTX 50-series supply, though the 3060 lands only slightly cheaper than the faster RTX 5060.

title: Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron sued over alleged DRAM price fixing amid record memory costs — lawsuit claims coordinated HBM shift was cover to curtail DDR3 and DDR4 production
blurb: Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron were sued on June 25th in a California class action accusing the three makers, which together control roughly 90% of the DRAM market, of coordinating to restrict supply and inflate prices the complaint says have climbed about 700% over four years. The suit argues their shift toward high-bandwidth memory for AI accelerators was cover to curtail DDR3 and DDR4 output, reviving claims a similar 2018 case failed to prove before its 2020 dismissal.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Science, and Google and OpenAI are already racing to match it
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Science, an application that gives researchers a single workspace integrating over 60 databases, specialist life-science models like Evo 2 and Boltz-2, and reproducible outputs that trace back to the code and environment behind each result. The tool runs on existing Claude models and lands within weeks of near-identical launches from Google and OpenAI, turning scientific research tooling into a three-way contest.

title: A Brazilian banking trojan is targeting Santander and BBVA customers with fake PDF lures
blurb: Fortinet found the Brazilian trojan Ousaban targeting Spanish and Portuguese bank customers with fake PDFs, geofencing, steganography, and daily-rotating servers to evade detection.

title: Apple's Hide My Email may not be hiding anything
blurb: A vulnerability in Apple's Hide My Email can reportedly link the anonymous addresses back to users' real email accounts, according to a 404 Media report. Security firm EasyOptOuts says it flagged the flaw to Apple a year ago and found 100% of tested Hide My Email addresses exploitable, but the company has not deployed a fix.

title: WhatsApp is Finally Getting Usernames to Help Keep Phone Numbers Private
blurb: WhatsApp began global rollout of optional usernames, letting its 3 billion users connect without sharing phone numbers. Once enabled, other accounts can no longer see a user's number, and an optional username key adds a second gate requiring senders to know both the handle and the key before making first contact. The feature arrives more than two years after Signal shipped its own username option.

title: How the AI bubble could pop and take down the global economy, according to the BIS
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements warned that an AI investment bubble could burst and drag down the broader global economy, citing concentrated valuations and heavy capital spending across a handful of firms. The BIS pointed to circular financing among chipmakers, cloud providers, and AI labs as a structural risk, drawing parallels to past asset bubbles whose collapse spread well beyond the original sector.

title: The DeepMind trio who built a poker AI are now making money for quant hedge funds
blurb: EquiLibre Technologies, a Prague-based lab founded by three former DeepMind researchers behind a poker-beating AI, raised a Series A led by Creandum at a $500M valuation. The startup applies reinforcement learning to trading, and its algorithms have moved billions in daily volume across the S&P 500 and Nasdaq through a partnership with Tower Research Capital, claiming zero negative months since inception.
