title: Meta loses bid to dismiss US states' claims that FB, Instagram addict children
blurb: A federal judge ruled that Meta must face claims from more than 30 US states alleging that Facebook and Instagram were designed to addict children. The decision lets the multistate suit proceed toward trial, keeping Meta exposed to potential damages and design-change mandates over engagement features regulators say harm minors.

title: Blue Origin pivots to redesigned launchpad after explosion in push to fly by end of 2026
blurb: Blue Origin will rebuild its Florida launchpad in a new "horizontal/vertical hybrid" configuration after a New Glenn rocket exploded during a May 28 hot-fire test, aiming to return to flight by the end of 2026. The redesign reuses infrastructure already planned for the larger 9x4 variant and keeps Blue Origin as NASA's "plan A" to launch an uncrewed Blue Moon lander for the Artemis program.

title: Google unveils Nano Banana 2 Lite aka Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite for low cost, 4-second fast enterprise image generations
blurb: Google released Nano Banana 2 Lite, designated Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite Image in its API, which generates 1K-resolution images in under four seconds at a flat $0.034 per 1,000 images. It targets high-throughput enterprise workflows through Google AI Studio, the Gemini API, and GEAP, trading the customization of rivals like Krea 2 Turbo for low cost and bundling with Google's Workspace and AI stack.

title: Warp CEO Zach Lloyd on why software factories are the next phase of coding
blurb: Warp CEO Zach Lloyd argues that most significant software projects will run on automated "software factories" within a year, shifting engineers from interactive work with agents to orchestrating systems that continuously triage, implement, review, and monitor changes. Warp's new Oz platform embodies the bet, connecting multiple models and coding harnesses across local and cloud sandboxes after the company open-sourced its core CLI in April amid pressure from Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI.

title: Anthropic finally brings back Claude Fable 5, but you’ll have to live with a temporary usage limit
blurb: Anthropic restored global access to Claude Fable 5 on July 1, weeks after a US government directive forced it to suspend the model over a jailbreak that coaxed it into flagging exploitable software vulnerabilities. Through July 7, Fable 5 counts toward up to 50% of the weekly usage limit on paid plans, and a new government-coordinated safety classifier blocks the flagged technique in over 99% of cases, rerouting failures to Opus 4.8.

title: Launch Preview: Swift rescue mission, final Atlas satellite launch
blurb: A NASA mission this week will attempt to rescue the aging Swift observatory using the final air-launched Pegasus rocket ever built, flying from Kwajalein Atoll in the Pacific. The week's schedule also includes the last satellite launch by an Atlas family rocket, the debut all-up flight of South Korea's solid-fuel GYUB military launcher, and a Rocket Lab Electron mission for Japan's iQPS from New Zealand.

title: Alibaba and its US payment processor AUS agree to pay $600M to resolve DOJ allegations that they failed to prevent illegal sales of drugs and other products (Reuters)
blurb: Alibaba and its US payment processor AUS agreed to pay $600M to settle DOJ allegations that they failed to prevent illegal sales of drugs and other prohibited products on their platforms. The settlement resolves claims tied to lax marketplace controls, adding to the regulatory pressure Alibaba faces as it expands US commerce operations.

title: Anthropic upgrades Claude with new Sonnet 5 model, details here
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a new default model for free and pro users that it calls its most agentic Sonnet yet, with performance close to Opus 4.8 at lower cost. Sonnet 5 replaces February's Sonnet 4.6 and launches at introductory pricing of $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens through August 31, before rising to standard $3 and $15 rates.

title: Anthropic just released a brand-new Claude Science app for Mac
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Science, a public beta desktop app for macOS and Linux that runs full scientific analyses, connects to 60+ databases, and saves provenance on every result. The app uses the Claude models already in a user's plan and ships with specialists for genomics, proteomics, and structural biology, plus native access to NVIDIA BioNeMo models like Evo 2 and Boltz-2.

title: Apple accelerates security updates in response to AI-powered hacking risks
blurb: Apple released iOS, iPadOS, and macOS 26.5.2 today with security fixes pulled forward from the planned 26.6 release, patching kernel, WebKit, and WebRTC vulnerabilities ahead of schedule. The company told Reuters it accelerated the timeline because AI models now speed the development of malicious hacking tools, shrinking the window between a fix going public and reaching customers before attackers can exploit it.

title: Apple may struggle to get clearance for Chinese RAM, even for Chinese iPhones
blurb: Apple is lobbying the Trump administration for permission to buy memory chips from blacklisted Chinese manufacturers CXMT and YMTC, even for iPhones sold only in China. The push faces long odds: a similar 2022 request under Biden drew bipartisan Senate opposition, and acting National Security Advisor Marco Rubio was among the lawmakers who signed a letter warning that YMTC's CCP ties posed supply-chain security risks.

title: Massive 3,200 Megapixel Camera Will Take 700 Photos Every Night for 10 Years
blurb: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory began its Legacy Survey of Space and Time, a 10-year mission using the world's largest digital camera to shoot 3,200-megapixel images every 40 seconds and map the entire southern sky every few nights. The 189-CCD instrument will build a high-resolution time-lapse of the cosmos aimed at probing dark energy and dark matter.

title: Microsoft Plans New Round of Job Cuts as AI and Cloud Spending Tops $100 Billion
blurb: Microsoft plans to cut thousands of jobs next week across sales, consulting, and Xbox, affecting less than 2.5% of its workforce, Business Insider reported. The round is smaller than last year's 15,000-plus cuts and lands as the company tracks toward more than $100 billion in AI and cloud infrastructure spending this fiscal year, up from $88.7 billion.

title: Chamath Palihapitiya Takes CEO Role at 8090 Labs After $135M Raise
blurb: Chamath Palihapitiya's enterprise AI coding startup 8090 Labs closed a $135M Series A led by Salesforce Ventures, and he will step in as CEO. It is his first full-time operating role since leaving Facebook, and the round drew his own circle, including All-In co-hosts David Friedberg and Jason Calacanis, David Sacks' Craft Ventures, and angels Nikesh Arora and Adam D'Angelo.

title: DATADOG rachète ADAPTIVE ML pour renforcer son laboratoire d’IA
blurb: Datadog acquired Adaptive ML, a startup specializing in reinforcement learning operations (RLOps), to strengthen its internal AI research lab and internalize post-training expertise. The deal is primarily a talent buy aimed at fine-tuning and continuously improving deployed models, an edge Datadog can sharpen using the billions of operational data points its observability platform already collects. Adaptive ML had raised $20M in 2024, and the purchase price was not disclosed.

title: BENDING SPOONS réussit son IPO : Wall Street valide le modèle du « serial consolidator » logiciel
blurb: Bending Spoons raised $1.68B in its Nasdaq IPO at an $18.4B valuation, pricing above its indicative range. The Italian company runs a "serial consolidator" model, buying and restructuring software firms like Evernote, Vimeo, WeTransfer, and AOL, and posted $601M in Q1 2026 revenue with 500M active users as investors bet on its acquisition machine over any single product.

title: Google copybara: moving code between repositories
blurb: Google open-sourced Copybara, its internal tool for transforming and syncing code between repositories, such as keeping a private and public repo in sync.

title: Tell HN: Installing Cursor on iOS irreversibly changes your privacy settings
blurb: Installing Cursor's iOS app silently switches accounts from the strict "Privacy Mode (Legacy)" no-storage setting to a softer policy that permits code storage for background agents, and support says the change cannot be reversed. The legacy option vanishes from every menu after the switch, leaving affected users with no path back and raising questions about consent and data-handling disclosure.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron Sued in US over Memory Price Fixing
blurb: Fourteen consumers and three small businesses sued Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron in a California federal court, alleging the three DRAM makers colluded on supply and pricing since 2022 to drive prices up roughly 700% over four years. The suit, filed by antitrust firm Bathaee Dunne, seeks class-action status and triple damages, citing Apple's recent price hikes as the trigger and the makers' coordinated shift to high-bandwidth memory.

title: Sony is killing discs — and showing us why it’s a terrible idea
blurb: Sony will stop producing physical PlayStation discs starting in January 2028, making all new PS5 games digital-only, and will begin shutting down the PS3 and PS Vita digital stores. Around 80% of PS5 games already sell digitally, but the shift raises a preservation problem: when storefronts close, the games sold there vanish with them, a risk the Video Game History Foundation flagged when it called 87% of pre-2010 titles critically endangered.

title: AI Models Capable of Devastating Attacks on Governments and Businesses are Months Away
blurb: The Five Eyes intelligence alliance issued a rare joint statement warning that AI models capable of devastating cyberattacks on governments and businesses are months, not years, away, and urged leaders to act now. The agencies said frontier models will sharply increase the speed, scale, and sophistication of attacks by lowering barriers for bad actors, framing cyber risk as a core business and leadership responsibility rather than a purely technical one.

title: Config 2026: New Materials, New Tools, and a More Expressive Canvas
blurb: Figma recapped its Config 2026 announcements, centered on code layers that turn any design layer into an interactive, prompt-editable code block on the canvas. The release also adds Figma Motion, shaders, generative plugins, and Weave tools, all composable in the same multiplayer file, with the ability to extract design frames back into editable layers when needed.

title: Japan wants 10 million more robots by 2040, some providing medical care
blurb: Japan's government set a target of adding 10 million robots by 2040 to offset a shrinking workforce, with deployments spanning medical care, elder support, and disaster response. The plan builds on lessons from the Fukushima cleanup and layers in AI, aiming to keep essential services staffed as the country's population ages and contracts.

title: UK regulator wants Apple and Google to let devs steer clear of app store fees
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority proposed rules requiring Apple and Google to let developers steer users toward cheaper payment options outside their app stores, sidestepping the standard commission. The measures target the two companies' "strategic market status" in mobile ecosystems, echoing similar anti-steering mandates under the EU's Digital Markets Act and threatening a core source of app store revenue.

title: South Korea’s hot new sensation is 3S+1F – a quadrillion-Won AI plan, not a band
blurb: South Korea unveiled a national AI strategy called 3S+1F, backed by spending that runs into the quadrillions of Won. The plan organizes investment around three "S" pillars and one "F", aiming to build sovereign models, infrastructure, and a domestic AI workforce as Seoul tries to keep pace with the US and China.

title: NASA is investing $590 million in private contractors to build humanity’s first Moon outpost
blurb: NASA awarded $590M to Astrobotic, Firefly Aerospace, and Intuitive Machines for four uncrewed lunar lander missions launching in late 2028, the opening phase of its $30B Moon Base program. The robotic runs are meant to prove landers, rovers, and cargo systems before astronauts arrive, though the track record is uneven, with only Firefly's Blue Ghost having landed cleanly so far.

title: Your Kids' School Bus Is About to Become a Roaming Surveillance Vehicle
blurb: BusPatrol, which operates over 40,000 stop-arm cameras on school buses across 24 states, reportedly plans to run those cameras continuously as license plate readers and sell the collected data to law enforcement, according to leaked documents reported by 404media. The shift turns violation-triggered safety cameras into always-on trackers of any nearby vehicle, drawing warnings from the ACLU that unregulated AI enforcement risks widespread over-policing.

title: Lime begins life as a public company after years of uncertainty
blurb: Lime raised $167M in its Nasdaq IPO at a roughly $1.66B valuation, ending nearly a decade as a private, Uber-backed micromobility company. The scooter and bike operator sold 6.68M shares at $25, and needs the proceeds to address around $1B in liabilities after warning in its filing of "substantial doubt" it could continue as a going concern.

title: The New York Times app adopts Liquid Glass on iPhone
blurb: The New York Times iOS app added Apple's Liquid Glass design to its tab bar and mini audio player.

title: Amazon blames piracy apps with malware for killing new Fire Stick sideloading
blurb: Amazon says the threat of malware from piracy apps drove its decision to block sideloading on new Vega OS Fire Sticks, a break from the Android-based Fire OS that let users load outside apps. The shift also gives Amazon tighter control over its devices, letting it enforce ad placement, block tracking-avoidance tools, and push features like its Alexa+ chatbot.

title: NASA may send a backup, nuclear-powered Mars rover to the Moon
blurb: NASA is seriously considering sending "Promise," the full-scale engineering twin of the Perseverance rover, to the Moon's south pole instead of using it only as a Mars testbed. The one-ton rover would carry a plutonium-238 MMRTG rather than solar panels, letting it survive the lunar night and make long traverses into permanently shadowed terrain, delivered by Blue Origin's Blue Moon or SpaceX's Starship.

title: NVIDIA's warm-water fix for AI's thirsty data centers
blurb: NVIDIA proposed a sealed warm-liquid cooling loop for AI data centers that rejects heat straight to ambient air, cutting on-site water use from millions of gallons per facility to near zero. Running the coolant warm also lets power-hungry chillers switch off for much of the year in favorable climates, though critics note on-site cooling is only part of AI's total water footprint.

title: Even Honda is pivoting to data centers
blurb: Honda began producing batteries for stationary energy storage systems, three months after canceling its US EV programs and redirecting cells once bound for cars toward data centers and the grid. The pivot follows a $15.7B writedown tied to Honda's EV restructuring and tracks a stationary-storage market growing 32% year-over-year, where Tesla earns roughly 30% gross margins on its Megapacks and Powerwalls.

title: Google's new Nano Banana 2 Lite image model is its fastest and cheapest yet
blurb: Google DeepMind released Nano Banana 2 Lite, technically Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite Image, which generates images in about 4 seconds versus 20 for standard Nano Banana. It averages $0.034 per 1K images, roughly half the price of Nano Banana 2, trading some accuracy on text, infographics, and character consistency for the speed and lower cost.

title: A Giant Cable Exposes Ireland’s AI Ambitions and Security Risks
blurb: A new Amazon transatlantic fiber-optic cable landing in Ireland underscores the country's rise as a European AI and data-center hub. The link also spotlights a vulnerability, as Ireland's chronic underinvestment in defense leaves critical subsea infrastructure exposed at a moment when undersea cables have become geopolitical targets.

title: Xbox testing disc-to-digital feature that digitizes a physical game collection
blurb: Microsoft is testing a disc-to-digital feature that grants Xbox owners a digital entitlement for their physical Xbox One and Series X games by inserting the disc once. The entitlement ties to the disc and moves between accounts if the game is loaned or sold, positioning the tool as Microsoft prepares to follow Sony in winding down physical disc production.

title: Employers who laid off workers citing AI are already starting to regret it
blurb: Companies that cut staff to deploy AI are rehiring the workers they let go, with Ford bringing back hundreds of engineers to fix quality issues automation missed and CBA reversing customer-service layoffs after its voice bot buckled. An Orgvue report found 39% of business leaders made AI-driven redundancies, and 55% of those now admit the decision was wrong.

title: Alphabet stock pops 4% on Dow debut, but the tech giant faces major AI questions
blurb: Alphabet stock rose 4% on Monday as the company joined the Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon, even as it tracks toward its worst month since early last year after six down weeks in seven. The blue-chip designation is mostly symbolic, arriving as investors question the payoff from Alphabet's AI spending amid DeepMind departures to Anthropic and OpenAI, cheaper Chinese models, and compute shortages that left it unable to meet enterprise demand.

title: How an AI Bust Could Ripple Through The Global Economy
blurb: The author argues that an AI investment bust could spread far beyond tech, dragging down a global economy increasingly propped up by data-center buildouts and chip spending. With memory makers posting record profits and South Korea planning new fabs, the piece warns that concentrated AI capex leaves markets exposed if returns fail to materialize.

title: AI Data Centers Have Been Great for the Steel Industry. Now, a Power Crisis Looms.
blurb: AI data centers have driven a surge in demand for steel, but steelmakers now warn they are competing with those same facilities for electricity. The power crunch threatens an industry that relies on cheap, abundant electricity to run its furnaces, setting up a fight over grid capacity as data center buildouts accelerate across the US.

title: Google's Gemini Omni Flash hits the API, turning enterprise video production into a conversation
blurb: Google released Gemini Omni Flash to its API, bringing conversational video editing to enterprise developers after a consumer debut at I/O 2026. The model takes text, images, and video and returns a finished clip with synced audio, collapsing a five-tool pipeline of script, image, video, lip-sync, and voice generators into a single model that teams can revise clip by clip through follow-up instructions.

title: Stathera nabs $55M to make vacuum-sealed silicon oscillators for AI chips
blurb: Stathera raised $55M in a Series A led by Maverick Silicon, with MediaTek's venture arm and Celesta Capital joining, to build vacuum-sealed silicon oscillators for AI chips. The Montreal company merges a processor's clock and timekeeping oscillator into one silicon device that can be made at standard chip fabs, cutting oscillator space requirements by up to 85% while lowering power draw and improving durability over quartz.

title: Positioning Without Satellites Or Base Stations
blurb: Terra is an open-source positioning system that calculates location using existing FM broadcast transmitters as fixed reference points, avoiding both GPS satellites and dedicated ground stations. Rather than tapping the transmitters' timing signals directly, it relies on internet-connected reference receivers that share timing data with client software, and a working demo runs in Denver on nothing more than an inexpensive software-defined radio and a computer.

title: NASA awards nearly $600 million in lunar lander missions
blurb: NASA selected Astrobotic, Firefly Aerospace, and Intuitive Machines for four robotic lunar lander missions worth $590.4 million, all scheduled for late 2028. Each lander will carry the same three payloads to study landing plumes, radiation, and laser rangefinding, building a shared network of environmental data across the lunar surface as NASA scales its Moon base ambitions.

title: Beck: Iridium acquisition the “logical next step” for Rocket Lab
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to acquire satellite operator Iridium for $54 per share in cash and stock, valuing the company at $8 billion, with the deal expected to close in mid-2027 pending regulatory approval. CEO Peter Beck framed the purchase as a move into recurring space services revenue, adding communications and navigation to Rocket Lab's existing launch and satellite-manufacturing business.

title: China establishes VLEO industry alliance as satellites demonstrate sustained low-orbit operations
blurb: China established a national VLEO Technology Innovation and Industry Development Alliance, co-founded by 34 universities, research institutes, and commercial space firms, as two of its satellites sustain operations below 300 kilometers. The demonstrator Shiyan-25 has held an altitude near 270 kilometers since September 2023, where atmospheric drag is roughly 10 times greater than at the ISS, signaling progress on the propulsion needed for high-resolution, low-latency orbits.

title: Alex Karp calls US reliance on AI labs for military tech "effing insane", pans AI fees for businesses, and touts Palantir's Nvidia Nemotron deal for US agencies (Ty Roush/Forbes)
blurb: Palantir CEO Alex Karp called US military reliance on AI labs for defense technology "effing insane" and criticized AI vendors for charging businesses recurring fees. He also promoted Palantir's new deal to deploy Nvidia's Nemotron models across US government agencies, extending the company's push to embed itself in federal AI infrastructure.

title: Uber-Backed Lime Raises $174 Million in Public Debut
blurb: Lime raised $174M in its Nasdaq debut, pricing at the midpoint of its range as the IPO window reopens.

title: Tim Cook and EU tech chief hold ‘constructive’ virtual meeting over Siri AI standoff
blurb: Tim Cook held a virtual meeting with EU tech chief Henna Virkkunen to resolve the standoff over Siri AI's delayed EU launch, which both sides described as "constructive." Apple blocked the feature in the bloc over the Digital Markets Act after regulators rejected its proposed Trusted System Agent workaround, with the two parties publicly blaming each other for the impasse.

title: Google Makes Gemini's Personalized Image Generation Free in the US
blurb: Google made Gemini's personalized image generation free for eligible US users on Monday, ending a requirement that limited the Nano Banana-powered feature to Plus, Pro, and Ultra subscribers. The tool builds images from a user's inferred interests and real likeness by drawing on connected Gmail, Google Photos, YouTube, and Search data, with the opt-in Personal Intelligence layer now reaching Gemini's 750 million monthly users.

title: Legible Shared Autonomy: Implicit Communication of Robot Belief through Motion
blurb: This paper introduces legible shared autonomy, a control method that makes a robot arm's motion reveal which goal it has inferred, so users with motor impairments can see whether the robot understood their intent. The system modulates how assertively it assists based on confidence, ceding control when uncertain, and user studies with a six-degree-of-freedom arm showed better understanding of the robot's beliefs and less control effort than standard shared autonomy.

title: CERN bids farewell to the LHC and enters Long Shutdown 3
blurb: CERN switched off the Large Hadron Collider after its final physics run, beginning Long Shutdown 3 to prepare for the High-Luminosity LHC upgrade. The successor, scheduled to start in 2030, will boost collision luminosity by up to tenfold over the original design, extending the search that yielded the 2012 Higgs boson discovery and more than 85 new hadrons.

title: I built a mmWave material classification radar (2025)
blurb: An engineer built an FMCW mmWave radar that classifies wall materials to detect asbestos without lab sampling. The device pairs a Texas Instruments IWRL6432 board and an ESP32 with a Capon beamforming DSP chain that feeds a density spectrum into a neural network, aiming to replace the $60 per-sample lab tests common across Europe.

title: County with 37 Data Centers Asks Schools to 'Conserve Electricity'
blurb: Henrico County, Virginia, which hosts 37 data centers with 17 more planned, asked its own employees and schools to conserve electricity after being told power rates would jump 25% on July 1. The increase adds an estimated $5 million to the county's next fiscal year, illustrating how the compute buildout near Richmond is now pushing energy costs onto the local governments that host it.

title: India's Tata Electronics Hit By Cybersecurity Breach Claiming to Expose Apple, Tesla Trade Secrets
blurb: Tata Electronics, a key Apple and Tesla supplier, was hit by a cyberattack in which an attacker claims to have stolen internal documents and trade secrets from both companies. The breach raises supply-chain security concerns for the iPhone assembler as it expands manufacturing in India, though Tata has not confirmed the scope or whether customer data was compromised.

title: Hyundai and Kia built a UV system that kills bacteria inside a car while you are sitting in it
blurb: Hyundai and Kia introduced Plasma Care UVC, a far-ultraviolet cabin sanitization system that kills airborne viruses and bacteria while passengers are inside the car. The system emits UVC light in the 200 to 230 nanometre range, which cannot penetrate human skin but killed nearly 97% of airborne viruses in 30 minutes in lab tests, and it is not yet in any production vehicle.

title: Inside Genebench-Pro
blurb: OpenAI introduced Genebench-Pro, a benchmark for evaluating how AI models perform on genomics and molecular biology tasks, alongside a set of case studies documenting real runs. The case studies detail where models succeed and fail on the benchmark, giving researchers a reference point for applying frontier models to biology workflows.

title: iOS 27 breaks 15 years of muscle memory on iPhone and iPad
blurb: Enabling Siri AI in iOS 27 hijacks the top-center swipe, shrinking Notification Center to a top-left-corner gesture after 15 years.

title: The Gemini app is bringing personalized image creation to more users.
blurb: Google expanded personalized image generation in the Gemini app to all eligible US users for free, connecting its Personal Intelligence feature with the Nano Banana model and Google Photos. The opt-in setup pulls context from Gmail, Photos, YouTube, and Search, letting users generate images of themselves from simple prompts like "create an illustration of me and my favorite things" without manual uploads.

title: ESA to seek lunar mapping capability for Argonaut lander
blurb: The European Space Agency will use third-party lunar topographic data from providers like NASA, India, or China to design its Argonaut cargo lander, lacking its own sovereign mapping capability. ESA is developing dedicated smaller missions to map the lunar south pole, where Argonaut's first flight targets a 2030 landing delivering up to 1,500 kilograms of cargo for Artemis support.

title: Pegasus XL set to air launch Swift Boost Mission to save NASA space telescope
blurb: A Northrop Grumman Pegasus XL rocket will air-launch NASA's Swift Boost Mission, a Katalyst Space Technologies servicing spacecraft tasked with raising the orbit of the aging Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory. The gamma-ray burst telescope has logged 1,760 bursts since 2004 but has been losing altitude to atmospheric drag, making this the first Pegasus XL flight since 2021.

title: NASA chief praises progress Blue Origin is making after launch failure
blurb: NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman praised Blue Origin's cleanup and rebuild of Launch Complex 36A, destroyed when a New Glenn test firing exploded on May 28. The agency needs New Glenn back in service to launch its Mk. 1 and Mk. 2 lunar landers, though independent observers peg a realistic return to flight at 12 to 18 months rather than the company's targeted end of this year.

title: Tidal AI Policy
blurb: Tidal published an AI policy that, starting June 29, blocks fully AI-generated tracks from earning royalties, direct-to-fan revenue, or any monetization, and will badge them with an "AI" label from mid-July. The streamer is not banning AI music outright but will use automated tools to remove tracks impersonating real artists and expects distributors to flag AI content before it reaches the platform.

title: New Alexa+ Agentic Ads let shoppers purchase directly within an ad
blurb: Amazon introduced Alexa+ Agentic Ads, the first ad format that lets shoppers complete a purchase through conversation without leaving the ad. The format launches during Prime Day with Papa Johns for food ordering and artists Beck, Jill Scott, and Omar Courtz for Ticketmaster concert sales, with Amazon planning to add more brands over time.

title: Apple ‘in negotiations’ with two banned Chinese RAM firms after price increases
blurb: Apple is in negotiations to buy memory chips from ChangXin Memory Technologies and Yangtze Memory Technologies, two Chinese firms on the Pentagon's blacklist, and is lobbying Washington for clearance. The deal would supply devices sold in China and ease a global memory shortage that has already forced Apple to raise prices on Macs and iPads to protect margins.

title: Telecom Shakeup: Comcast, Verizon Make Deals
blurb: Comcast will spin off NBCUniversal and its European media unit Sky into a separately traded company, keeping its cable-TV, broadband, and wireless businesses in-house. Separately, Verizon and the UK's BT Group agreed to merge their international units into a joint venture, offloading low-margin operations so both carriers can concentrate on their home markets.

title: National Grid's Big Bet on US AI Power Firm
blurb: National Grid invested $1.75B in Jouleant, a US-based AI power firm, betting on surging electricity demand from AI data centers. CEO Zoë Yujnovich framed the deal as a move to position the utility closer to the compute buildout driving America's fastest-growing source of power consumption.

title: iPhone 17 Pro just got an exclusive new pro-focused camera feature
blurb: Apple updated Final Cut Camera, its free pro-focused iOS app, with a Clean HDMI Out feature that sends an overlay-free video feed to external monitors or recorders, exclusively on iPhone 17 Pro and 17 Pro Max. The release accompanied broader Apple Creator Studio updates across Final Cut Pro and Pixelmator Pro, and adds direct file transfer from iPhone into Final Cut Pro over a Mac connection.

title: US home battery installations hit record high on rising electricity costs
blurb: US home battery installations reached a record 673 megawatts of storage in the first quarter of 2026, according to the Energy Information Administration. California and Hawaii led the growth, driven by state incentives and residential electricity prices that rose more than 7% year-over-year, giving homeowners reason to store cheap power and draw it during peak demand.

title: Honda starts making batteries for data centres at a factory built for EVs it cancelled
blurb: Honda has begun making energy storage batteries for data centres at its Ohio plant, repurposing a factory built for three EVs it cancelled in March. The retreat triggered up to $16B in write-downs and Honda's first annual loss as a listed company, joining Ford and GM in redirecting battery capacity toward a US stationary-storage market that hit a record 10 GWh of installations in Q1.

title: Blue Origin decides not to re-create ruined pad but will move on to a different launch concept
blurb: Blue Origin will not rebuild the New Glenn launch pad destroyed in a May explosion, instead shifting to a hybrid horizontal/vertical configuration already planned for a second Cape Canaveral pad. CEO Dave Limp said early analysis traces the blast to the first stage's aft section, and the common setup across both pads should increase flight cadence as the company targets a return to flight this year.

title: L’Autriche invite Bruxelles à explorer une implantation d’Anthropic dans l’Union européenne
blurb: Austria asked the European Commission to study bringing an Anthropic operation into the EU, with digitalization state secretary Alexander Pröll arguing in a letter to Commissioner Henna Virkkunen that Europe cannot ignore US restrictions on access to frontier AI models. The proposal is unlikely to materialize, but it signals a shift in how European policymakers view AI, treating access to advanced models as a strategic concern on par with regulation.

title: Micron and Anthropic Announce Strategic Agreement to Scale Next-Generation AI Infrastructure
blurb: Micron announced a strategic agreement with Anthropic that covers joint memory and storage architecture design, a multi-year supply deal for Micron's data center HBM, DRAM, and SSDs, and an investment in Anthropic's Series H round. The partnership ties frontier model demand directly to infrastructure, giving Anthropic guaranteed supply for training and serving Claude while positioning Micron's memory portfolio at the center of AI compute scaling.

title: iPhone Ultra 3D-printed hinge problems reportedly solved, ready for September launch
blurb: Apple has resolved the durability and tolerance problems with the 3D-printed hinge on its first foldable, the iPhone Ultra, clearing the device for a September launch, according to a new supply-chain report in The Elec. Hinge modules from Taiwan's Shinjuxing and US-based Amphenol caused noise and defect-rate issues during testing, but the phone has now entered test production ahead of mass manufacturing.

title: Grammarly parent Superhuman buys AI detector GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman, the company formerly known as Grammarly, agreed to acquire AI-detection startup GPTZero for an undisclosed sum. GPTZero brings 19 million registered users, roughly $30M in annual recurring revenue, and tools that flag machine-written text, fake citations, and invented statistics, all of which Superhuman will fold into its Go assistant as part of an "authenticity layer" for verifying where content came from.

title: FCC to vote on satellite licensing overhaul July 22
blurb: The FCC will vote July 22 on its Space Modernization Order, replacing the decades-old Part 25 satellite rules with a new Part 100 that compresses reviews from years into months and cuts the public notice window to 15 days from 30. The order would let operators make more minor license changes without prior approval and require them to share tracking data with a space situational awareness provider to curb collisions in low Earth orbit.

title: NASA unsure Boeing Starliner will ever be certified for human flight
blurb: NASA said it is unsure whether Boeing's Starliner capsule will ever be certified to carry astronauts, more than a year after thruster failures stranded its crew and forced a return on SpaceX. The agency is weighing whether to require another uncrewed test flight, extending a program already years behind schedule and billions of dollars over budget.

title: Meta names Chief Marketing Officer Alex Schultz as its first-ever chief data officer, to manage AI analytics across the company, and names Denise Moreno as CMO (Sara Fischer/Axios)
blurb: Meta named marketing chief Alex Schultz as its first-ever chief data officer, tasking him with managing AI analytics across the company. Denise Moreno steps into the vacated CMO role, a reshuffle that signals Meta is treating unified data governance as central to its AI ambitions rather than a back-office function.

title: Meta is reportedly building its own cloud business
blurb: Meta is reportedly building a cloud business to monetize the AI infrastructure it has amassed, potentially selling access to models run on its servers or leasing data-center compute to other companies, according to Bloomberg. The move would put Meta in direct competition with AWS, Google, and SpaceX, and help recoup a $600B US infrastructure commitment through 2028.

title: Phantom Squatting Uses AI-Hallucinated Domains for Phishing and Malware
blurb: Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 documented "phantom squatting," in which attackers register AI-hallucinated domains and host phishing pages to capture traffic that LLMs point at fake but plausible addresses. Across 685,339 questions about 913 brands, two models produced 2.1M links, with 13,229 already flagged malicious and roughly 250,000 invented domains unregistered and available to whoever claims them first.

title: Claude Code is steganographically marking requests
blurb: A developer inspecting Claude Code 2.1.196 found that the binary can encode a hidden marker in the date string it inserts into the system prompt, swapping the apostrophe in "Today's" and the date separator when certain conditions are met. The triggers are a custom ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, an Asia/Shanghai or Asia/Urumqi timezone, and hostnames matching base64-encoded, XOR-decoded lists of AI lab keywords and reseller domains. The author reads it as an attempt to flag API resellers, unauthorized gateways, and distillation pipelines.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 model on Claude and APIs
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5 across all Claude plans, Claude Code, and the Claude API, with pricing starting at $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens before an increase after August 31, 2026. The model improves on Sonnet 4.6 in reasoning, coding, and agentic tool use, closing much of the gap with Opus 4.8 at a lower cost while adding real-time cyber safeguards.

title: Claude Sonnet 5
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, its most agentic Sonnet model, with performance close to Opus 4.8 on reasoning, tool use, coding, and knowledge work at lower prices. It ships today across all plans as the default for Free and Pro, with introductory Claude Platform pricing of $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens through August 31, 2026.

title: New BioShocking Attack Tricks AI Browsers Into Leaking User Credentials
blurb: Security firm LayerX disclosed BioShocking, an indirect prompt injection attack that tricked six AI browsers and assistants, including OpenAI's ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity's Comet, and Anthropic's Claude extension, into copying a user's credentials and sending them to an attacker. The attack disguises malicious commands as game rules a puzzle page, and vendor responses were uneven, with OpenAI patching Atlas while Perplexity closed the report without acting.

title: Claude Science
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Science, a beta research app that runs analyses, queries 60+ scientific databases, and traces every step from data wrangling to publication. It renders proteins, structures, and molecules natively, keeps figures reproducible by linking them to the exact code and environment that produced them, and manages compute across a laptop, HPC cluster, or GPUs.

title: Leanstral 1.5
blurb:
Verified details. Here's the blurb:

Mistral released Leanstral 1.5, a Lean 4 formal proof engineering model optimized for automated theorem proving and autoformalization, with 119B total parameters, 6.5B active, and a 256k-token context window. The model is available for free on Mistral's Labs tier via the console playground and iterates on the March 2026 release that beat Claude Sonnet by 8 points at pass@16 at 15x lower cost.

Sources: [Mistral Docs](https://docs.mistral.ai/models/model-cards/leanstral-1-5-26-06), [AI Weekly](https://aiweekly.co/alerts/mistral-ships-leanstral-15-for-lean-4-proof-work-free-in-labs)

title: Anthropic rolls out Sonnet 5 with near-Opus 4.8 performance at a lower price
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a cheaper Sonnet-tier model that narrows the performance gap with its flagship Opus 4.8. The model brings agentic capabilities previously reserved for Opus-class models to the lower price tier, including multi-step planning, browser and terminal tool use, and the ability to verify its own output without being prompted.

title: Claude Sonnet 5 includes safeguards against dangerous cyber use
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a general-purpose model with improved reasoning, coding, and agentic tool use, and enabled cybersecurity safeguards by default that detect and block dangerous activity in real time. Sonnet 5 outperforms Sonnet 4.6 across effort levels, matches Opus 4.8 on some tasks at higher settings, and cannot develop a working exploit.

title: Anthropic says Trump admin has lifted export controls on Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5
blurb: Anthropic said the U.S. Department of Commerce lifted export controls on its Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, ending a standoff that had forced the company to suspend all foreign access in mid-June. Fable 5 returns to global users on Claude.ai and Claude Code starting Wednesday, while Mythos 5 access has been restored for some U.S. organizations following government approval on June 26.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 AI model with coding, safety upgrades as Fable and Mythos controls lifted
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a midrange model that beats Sonnet 4.5 by 5.1% on SWE-Bench Pro and 13.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.1, and jumps to 1,618 on GDPval-AA v2 from 1,395. It becomes the default across Claude's consumer tiers and costs $3 per million input tokens and $15 per million output tokens starting in September.

title: Imec's 2026 roadmap details 0.3nm nodes by 2038, CFET transistors become viable at 0.7nm — company redefines Moore's Law as cell sizes gain importance for density
blurb: Imec's 2026 roadmap targets 0.3nm nodes by 2038 and viable CFET transistors at 0.7nm, redefining Moore's Law around cell size.

title: Start building with Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash
blurb: Google DeepMind released two generative media models for developers: Nano Banana 2 Lite, its fastest and most cost-efficient image model, and Gemini Omni Flash, a model for video generation and conversational editing. Nano Banana 2 Lite produces text-to-image outputs in 4 seconds at $0.034 per 1K-resolution image, and both are available in Google AI Studio, the Gemini API, and the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform.

title: Introducing TabFM: A zero-shot foundation model for tabular data
blurb: Google Research introduced TabFM, a zero-shot foundation model for tabular classification and regression that frames prediction as an in-context learning problem, generating results on unseen tables in a single forward pass. The approach eliminates the manual hyperparameter tuning and feature engineering that tree-based methods like XGBoost require, and the model is available on Hugging Face and GitHub.

title: Anthropic Restores Claude Fable 5 After U.S. Lifts Jailbreak-Linked Export Controls
blurb: Anthropic restored global access to Claude Fable 5 on July 1 after the U.S. Commerce Department lifted the export controls it had imposed June 12 over a jailbreak found by Amazon researchers. To resolve the concern, Anthropic trained a new classifier that blocks the flagged technique in more than 99% of attempts and routes blocked requests to the weaker Opus 4.8.

title: GuardFall Exposes Open-Source AI Coding Agents to Decades-Old Shell Injection Risks
blurb: Adversa AI's GuardFall research shows that AI coding agents' command blocklists can be bypassed with shell tricks that have been public for decades, such as writing `r''m` so a text filter misses it while bash strips the quotes and runs `rm` anyway. The bypass worked against ten of eleven open-source coding and computer-use agents tested, including Aider, Cline, Goose, and OpenHands. The researchers frame it as a class of problems rather than a patchable bug, so adding blocklist patterns fixes nothing.

title: 282 iOS AI Apps Leak API Keys and Open AI Proxy Access in Network Traffic Study
blurb: Researchers at Wake Forest University found that 282 of 444 tested iOS AI chatbot apps, nearly two-thirds, leaked paid LLM access through their network traffic in the form of plaintext API keys, open backend relays, or replayable tokens. Attackers can run requests on the developer's account under a practice called LLMjacking, which Sysdig estimated could reach over $46,000 a day, yet only 28% of notified developers had fixed the issue after three months.

title: Claude Sonnet 5 boosts coding, reasoning, and tool use
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5 on June 30, an update that improves on Sonnet 4.6 in coding, reasoning, tool use, and knowledge work while running agentically at a level that previously required larger models. It ships as a drop-in upgrade with adaptive thinking on by default, introductory pricing of $2 per million input and $10 per million output tokens through August 31, and performance close to Opus 4.8.

title: Claude Fable 5 Promotional Access
blurb: Anthropic is offering free promotional access to Claude Fable 5, its newest model, to Pro, Max, Team, and eligible seat-based Enterprise subscribers from July 1 to July 7, 2026. Subscribers can spend up to 50% of their weekly usage limits on Fable 5 at no extra cost, after which they can continue with usage credits or switch to another model.

title: U.S. Lifts Restrictions on Anthropic’s Most Powerful A.I. Models
blurb: The U.S. government lifted restrictions on Anthropic's most powerful AI models, allowing the company to bring its top-tier technologies back online. The decision de-escalates a feud with the Trump administration that had forced Anthropic to pull its highest-capability systems from availability.

title: After spooking Trump into safety testing, Anthropic AI models get global release
blurb: The US lifted export curbs on Anthropic's newest Claude models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, roughly three weeks after the Trump administration flagged them as national security risks. Fable 5 is now available globally and US organizations regained Mythos 5 access on June 26, after Anthropic expanded its government partnership, launched a hacker red-teaming program, and stood up a 24/7 team monitoring jailbreak threats.

title: Hugging Face and Cerebras bring Gemma 4 to real-time voice AI
blurb: Hugging Face and Cerebras released an open speech-to-speech pipeline that runs Google DeepMind's Gemma 4 31B on Cerebras for real-time voice AI. The fully modular stack pairs Nvidia's Parakeet for speech recognition with Alibaba's Qwen3TTS for text-to-speech, and already powers more than 9,000 Reachy Mini robots by cutting language-model latency at the P95 tail.

title: Critical Cursor Flaws Could Let Prompt Injection Escape Sandbox and Run Commands
blurb: Cato AI Labs disclosed DuneSlide, a pair of critical Cursor flaws (CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549, both rated 9.8) that let a zero-click prompt injection escape the AI editor's terminal sandbox and run arbitrary commands on a developer's machine. Both bugs trick the agent into writing a file it shouldn't to disable the sandbox, and both are patched in Cursor 3.0, so every prior version remains affected.

title: Core dump epidemiology: fixing an 18-year-old bug
blurb: OpenAI traced a set of seemingly impossible crashes in Rockset, its C++ ChatGPT data infrastructure, to two unrelated causes: silent hardware corruption on one Azure host and an 18-year-old race condition in GNU libunwind. The team identified both by building a population-level dataset of every crash and analyzing it like an epidemiologist rather than debugging individual failures.

title: Microsoft Warns Poisoned MCP Tool Descriptions Can Make AI Agents Leak Data
blurb: Microsoft's Incident Response and Defender teams show how a poisoned MCP tool description can turn an AI agent into a data-exfiltration channel without breaking any rule. Using an invoice-processing example, the research demonstrates how a hidden instruction buried in a third-party tool's description makes the agent collect and leak unpaid invoices during a routine query. Because MCP picks up description changes on the fly, poisoned versions can go live with no re-approval, and in a default setup no alarm fires.

title: Langflow RCE Exploited to Deploy Monero Miner on Exposed AI App Endpoints
blurb: Threat actors are exploiting CVE-2026-33017, a critical unauthenticated RCE flaw in Langflow, to deploy a Monero miner across exposed AI application endpoints. Trend Micro observed the campaign over a 19-day window, during which the Go-based malware terminates rival miners, disables host security controls, establishes cron persistence, and propagates to other machines via reused SSH keys.

title: Anthropic to restore Claude Fable access on Wednesday
blurb: Anthropic said the Department of Commerce lifted export controls on its two most capable models, Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5, and it will begin restoring Fable 5 access on Wednesday. Mythos 5 will remain limited to select companies, and it is unclear whether Fable 5 will reach users outside the United States or require identity verification through Anthropic's new Persona partnership.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 at a steep discount to its top model as the company races toward a blockbuster IPO
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a mid-tier model that scores 63.2% on SWE-bench Pro and 57.4% with tools on Humanity's Last Exam, closing much of the gap to its flagship Opus 4.8. Introductory API pricing runs $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output through August 31, roughly 60% below Opus, as the company builds developer adoption ahead of a planned IPO.

title: Armadin details full sandbox escape in Claude Cowork but Anthropic disputes risk
blurb: Security researchers at Armadin detailed an attack chain that escapes the sandbox behind Anthropic's Claude Cowork, running arbitrary commands as root inside the isolated VM while a second flaw strips its network restrictions. Anthropic disputes that it qualifies as a security issue, arguing the chain requires an attacker to already have local code execution on the host machine.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Metacognitive Feedback Elicits Faithful Uncertainty Expression in LLMs
blurb: This paper introduces reinforcement learning with metacognitive feedback (RLMF), which refines completion rankings during preference optimization using the quality of a model's self-judgments about its own performance. Applied to faithful calibration, aligning expressed uncertainty with intrinsic uncertainty, RLMF reaches state-of-the-art results while preserving accuracy and surpasses standard RL by up to 63% on assessing and expressing capability limits.

title: Zluda 6 release (run unmodified CUDA applications on non-Nvidia GPUs)
blurb: ZLUDA 6 lets unmodified CUDA applications run on non-NVIDIA GPUs, and the release adds two major workloads: PhysX and Blender with initial texture support. The update introduces 32-bit PhysX support that can raise frame rates and enable previously missing visual effects in older games on AMD hardware, alongside improved Windows compatibility and better ML support.

title: Meta’s AI Storage Blueprint at Scale
blurb: Meta detailed how its BLOB-storage architecture evolved to cut GPU stalls and speed research iteration across geo-distributed training clusters. The system runs on Tectonic, a regional multi-tenant storage fabric that uses erasure coding for durability, tiers data across HDD and flash, and places hot, warm, and cold data to balance I/O across tenants.

title: LongCat-2.0, a large-scale MoE model with 1.6T total and 48B Active
blurb: LongCat-2.0 is a large-scale mixture-of-experts model with 1.6T total parameters and 48B active per token. The sparse design keeps inference costs tied to the smaller active parameter count while the full expert pool supplies the capacity of a much larger dense model.

title: US reverses export restrictions on Anthropic’s Fable 5, Mythos 5 AI models
blurb: The US government lifted export restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 frontier models, ending nearly three weeks of disruption tied to concerns over the models' cybersecurity capabilities. Fable 5 begins rolling out globally on July 1 across the Claude Platform, Claude.ai, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork, with access on AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Foundry restored as quickly as possible.

title: [AINews] Sonnet 5 today, and Fable 5 tomorrow
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5 as its new default mid-tier frontier model, with a 1M-token context window, top-tier coding and tool-use performance, and immediate rollout across Claude, Claude Code, and the API. List pricing holds at $3/M input and $15/M output tokens, discounted to $2/$10 through August, though early testing flagged efficiency concerns tied to tokenizer changes and 3-6x more turn-taking on benchmarks.

title: Fake Bug Report Hijacks AI Coding Agents at Scale
blurb: Tenet Security researchers demonstrated "agentjacking," an attack that hijacks AI coding agents into running arbitrary code by planting a single fake error report in a public bug tracker like Sentry. In controlled tests, assistants including Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex retrieved the poisoned data and often executed attacker-controlled code, exposing cloud credentials, AWS keys, GitHub tokens, and CI/CD secrets.

title: 🔬 The Coolest Diffusion Research Isn't in LLMs — Evan Feinberg & Sergey Edunov, Genesis Molecular AI
blurb: This Latent Space episode argues that the most innovative diffusion research is happening in 3D molecular structure prediction, not LLMs, where transformer variants have seen little conceptual change since "Attention is all you need." Co-founders Evan Feinberg and Sergey Edunov, who led Llama 2 and Llama 3 pretraining at Meta, discuss Genesis Molecular AI's flagship PEARL model, which routinely clears the accuracy threshold needed for real-world small molecule drug discovery and enables new agentic workflows.

title: ZCode: Claude Code from the Makers of GLM
blurb: Z.ai, the maker of the GLM models, released ZCode, a Claude Code-style agentic coding tool optimized for its GLM-5.2 model across reasoning, coding, and multi-agent collaboration. ZCode supports 20+ programming tools, manages multi-step work through persistent Goals that plan and verify, and can be triggered remotely via WeChat, Feishu, or Telegram, with tiered subscription plans.

title: Anthropic Reaches Deal With Trump Administration to Restore Access to Fable AI Model
blurb: Anthropic reached a deal with the Trump administration to restore access to its Fable AI model, which had been shut down for two and a half weeks over security concerns. The suspension disrupted the broader AI industry, cutting off a widely used model, and the agreement clears the way for Fable to come back online.

title: China’s Meituan open-sources massive LongCat-2.0 AI model, saying it was trained on domestic chips
blurb: Meituan open-sourced LongCat-2.0, a 1.6-trillion-parameter mixture-of-experts model with a 1 million-token context window that the company says was trained entirely on domestic Chinese chips and compute clusters. The sparse MoE design activates a curated set of experts per token for cheaper inference, and Meituan positions the model as a "brain" for agentic workflows and coding harnesses like Claude Code.

title: China’s hollow-core fiber trial pushes 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles without signal regeneration — milestone targets AI-era networking bottlenecks
blurb: YOFC field-trialed hollow-core fiber hitting 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles without signal regeneration, targeting AI-era networking bottlenecks.

title: TRIAGE: Role-Typed Credit Assignment for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
blurb: TRIAGE is a role-typed credit assignment framework for agentic reinforcement learning that adds a semantic role axis to standard GRPO, using a structured judge to label each action segment as decisive progress, useful exploration, no-progress infrastructure, or regression. Across ALFWorld, Search-QA, and WebShop, it beats GRPO on success rates and cuts environment-facing turns by up to 14.8%, with reliable regression detection driving most of the gain.

title: Self-Study Reconsidered: The Hidden Fragility of Learning from Self-Generated QA
blurb: This paper shows that training language models on self-generated question-answer pairs is fragile at both the question-selection and answering stages, with generators fixating on salient spans and complying with instruction-like text embedded in documents. Filtering instruction-like spans before answering cut mean prompt-injection compliance from 88% to 13% while retaining nearly all clean text.

title: Geometry-Preserving Orthonormal Initialization for Low-Rank Adaptation in RLVR
blurb: This paper analyzes how LoRA initialization behaves under reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), showing that structurally initialized variants like PiSSA and MiLoRA can underperform standard LoRA and destabilize training despite winning under supervised fine-tuning. The authors prove orthonormal initialization minimizes the gap to full fine-tuning and propose two variants, RLPO and RLMO, which stabilize RLVR training and beat standard LoRA on math reasoning benchmarks.

title: Bridging the Gap Between Latent and Explicit Reasoning with Looped Transformers
blurb: LOTUS is a looped Transformer that performs latent chain-of-thought reasoning, processing K latent blocks in parallel for R iterations with cross-entropy supervision on each position's gold CoT token. It is the first latent-CoT method to match explicit CoT at the 3B scale while cutting thought-phase latency by 2.5x to 6.9x, with post-loop latents that decode back into interpretable reasoning steps.

title: MCP Server Architecture Patterns for LLM-Integrated Applications
blurb: This paper catalogues five recurring MCP server architecture patterns (Resource Gateway, Tool Orchestrator, Stateful Session Server, Proxy Aggregator, and Domain-Specific Adapter) observed across fifteen production and public servers, plus four anti-patterns and cross-cutting concerns around auth, versioning, and observability. A tool-count study found selection accuracy drops below 90% at 10-15 tools per context for Claude Haiku 4.5 and 20-30 tools for Sonnet 4.

title: New attack provides one more reason why AI browsers are a bad idea
blurb: New research from LayerX security demonstrates an attack that tricks AI browsers into a fictional context where their safety guardrails no longer apply. A malicious site presents a puzzle that rewards wrong answers like 2+2=5, pushing the embedded LLM into a "dream world" state where an attacker can extract code from private repositories or steal credentials from the built-in password manager.

title: Sources: SoftBank has reopened talks for a $10B loan backed by its OpenAI stake and is offering to guarantee repayment if OpenAI collateral proves insufficient (Echo Wang/Reuters)
blurb: SoftBank has reopened talks for a $10B loan backed by its OpenAI stake, according to sources. It is offering to guarantee repayment if the OpenAI collateral proves insufficient, a concession that signals SoftBank's willingness to absorb downside risk to keep funding flowing into the company.

title: Z-1: Efficient Reinforcement Learning for Vision-Language-Action Models
blurb: Z-1 is a reinforcement learning post-training framework for flow-based vision-language-action models, built on π0.5 and applying task-wise Group Relative Policy Optimization across 24 standard RoboCasa tasks. Using only publicly released RoboCasa demonstrations, Z-1 reaches an 80.6% average success rate, a 13.2-point gain over its supervised fine-tuning baseline, and outperforms published state-of-the-art models without additional private data.

title: Get started with the Claude apps gateway for Google Cloud
blurb: Anthropic and Google Cloud released the Claude apps gateway, a self-hosted service shipped with the claude binary that sits between local Claude Code clients and Google Cloud to centralize enterprise governance. It routes logins through an OIDC identity provider for short-lived sessions, enforces RBAC model policies server-side from a single gateway.yaml, and attaches verified user identity to token-usage telemetry and spend caps.

title: Import AI 463: Self-improving robots; a 10k Chinese GPU cluster; and an elegiac essay for the human era
blurb: NVIDIA's ENPIRE is a harness that lets coding agents run autonomous experiment-and-reset loops to improve real-world robot policies.

title: AWS funnels $1B into forward deployed engineering hub
blurb: AWS is investing $1B into a forward deployed engineering organization that pairs pods of five to six human engineers with AI agents to build and deploy AI systems inside customer environments. The agentic-first model runs 45-day sprints and leaves a semantic layer and knowledge graph behind so agents keep operating after the team departs, with Cox Automotive, the NBA, and the NFL already onboard.

title: Sonnet 5 review: I ran 64 generations to find out if it's worth it
blurb: This post benchmarks Anthropic's Sonnet 5 blind against Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5, and Gemini 3 Pro across PRD quality, prototype generation, agentic task completion, and agent personality. The author built a repeatable eval harness in Claude Code, scoring outputs 70% on human vibe ratings and 30% on LLM-as-judge, then gives model-by-task recommendations for which to use for PRDs, complex prototypes, and daily agent chat.

title: Trump drops restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos and Fable models
blurb: The U.S. lifted an export-license requirement on Anthropic's Mythos and Fable models, and the company said it would begin restoring public access on July 1. Anthropic agreed to proactively detect security risks and coordinate release protocols with the government, terms it had largely pledged voluntarily before the June 12 restriction, which was eased amid pressure from Asian models like Fugu and Tulongfeng approaching Mythos-level capability.

title: Content Independence Day, one year on: building the business model for the agentic Internet
blurb: Cloudflare's report marks one year since Content Independence Day, when it began blocking AI training crawlers by default on new domains. The company reports the shift has moved faster than expected: generative AI hit 2.5 billion regular users in 3.5 years, more than 2x the smartphone adoption rate, and non-human traffic now exceeds 50% of the internet. Users spend just 15 minutes on the open web for every hour searching, as AI-driven answers replace multi-site browsing.

title: Cursor releases iPhone and iPad app following recent acquisition by SpaceX
blurb: Cursor released its first iOS app for iPhone and iPad, letting developers launch and manage cloud coding agents from mobile following its acquisition this month by SpaceX, which includes xAI. The app supports voice conversations with agents, diff inspection, pull request merges, and Remote Control handoffs between local and cloud environments, with 75% off Composer 2.5 runs through July 5.

title: Anthropic's new Sonnet 5 model is better at the tasks that are running up enterprise bills
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a model tuned for agentic workloads that delivers performance comparable to recent Opus models at lower prices. Starting September 1, Sonnet 5 will cost $3 per million input tokens and $15 per million output tokens, undercutting Opus 4.8's $4 and $25, and uses a new tokenizer to cut costs for the heavy agentic users driving enterprise token bills.

title: Prime Day shows how AI is changing shopping, testing Amazon’s bet against ChatGPT and others
blurb: Shoppers arriving from AI chatbots were the most likely to complete a purchase during Amazon's four-day Prime Day event, which drove a record $26.4B across U.S. retail, with Adobe reporting AI-referred visitors converted 40% more often than those from search, email, or social. The trend pressures Amazon's strategy of blocking outside assistants like ChatGPT and Perplexity while pushing its own Rufus tool and buying ads inside ChatGPT.

title: Introspective Coupling: Self-Explanation Training Tracks Behavioral Change Despite Fixed Supervision
blurb: This paper studies when training language models to explain their predictions produces faithful introspection rather than imitation, using counterfactual behavior on modified inputs as supervision. The authors find that models trained on fixed explanations from earlier checkpoints or other model families often explain their own current behavior more faithfully than their training targets, and this "introspective coupling" tracks behavior shifts across tasks like sycophancy and refusal without updated supervision.

title: QVal: Cheaply Evaluating Dense Supervision Signals for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
blurb: QVal is a training-free testbed that evaluates dense supervision signals for long-horizon LLM agents by measuring how well each method's scores align with the Q-values of a strong reference policy. Benchmarking 21 methods across four environments and six model backbones, the authors found that simple prompting baselines consistently outperform recent dense supervision methods, with performance clustering by methodological family.

title: Surrogate Fidelity: When Can Open LLMs Explain Closed Ones?
blurb: This paper evaluates when open language models can serve as interpretability surrogates for closed models that expose only output log-probabilities. Testing eleven models across Llama, Qwen, GPT, and Gemini, the authors find that prediction agreement overstates attribution fidelity: models that agree on the answer often disagree on why, so mechanistic insight does not reliably transfer to closed targets.

title: Securing the AI Agent: A Unified Framework for Multi-Layer Agent Red Teaming
blurb: AI-Infra-Guard is an open-source framework that organizes AI agent red teaming by matching a detection paradigm to each layer of the attack surface: infrastructure, protocol/tool, agent behavior, and model. It spans deterministic rule matching over 75+ components and 1,400+ vulnerability rules, LLM-driven auditing of MCP servers and agent-skill supply chains, and a jailbreak harness with 26+ attack operators across sixteen datasets.

title: An Empirical Study of Security Calibration in Large Language Models for Code
blurb: This paper presents the first large-scale empirical study of security calibration in LLM-generated code, evaluating GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.0-Flash, and Qwen3-Coder-Next on self-contained and repository-level benchmarks. The authors find overconfidence is prevalent, that models estimate security outcomes more reliably than functional correctness, and that calibration-guided repair yields only limited gains while frequently introducing functional regressions.

title: Security--Fidelity Tradeoffs: The Hidden Cost of Prompt Injection Defense
blurb: This paper identifies a security-fidelity tradeoff in defending LLMs against indirect prompt injection: defenses block injected instructions mainly by suppressing untrusted text, which corrupts tasks like translation and document editing that must preserve it. The authors introduce SecFid, a benchmark that distinguishes executing, processing, and ignoring an injection, and show across 1,168 examples and 48 configurations that no model hits both goals, with the most secure defenses reaching 99.3% security at only 71.0%-73.9% fidelity.

title: Micro-Agent: Beat Frontier Models with Collaboration Inside Model API
blurb: vLLM Semantic Router introduces a looper runtime that turns a single OpenAI-compatible model call into a bounded collaboration inside the serving layer, fanning out to workers, collecting a quorum, verifying disagreement, and synthesizing one final answer. Rather than living inside a commercial endpoint or a bespoke agent graph, this orchestration becomes an open serving primitive, letting a stable model identity outperform frontier models without changing any weights.

title: Google is rationing Gemini access to Meta because it cannot provide enough compute
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models because it cannot supply the computing capacity Meta wanted, the Financial Times reported, with several Google clients affected and Meta hit hardest. Meta has told staff to use AI tokens more efficiently and is shifting content-moderation and safety workloads to Muse Spark, a new internal model built under its Superintelligence Labs division.

title: 'Phantom Squatting': An Emerging AI-Driven Supply Chain Threat
blurb: Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 documented "phantom squatting," an attack vector where cybercriminals register the nonexistent brand domains that LLMs routinely hallucinate to intercept AI-generated traffic. Across 685,339 URL queries covering 913 brands, two LLMs produced roughly 250,000 hallucinated domains, and one attacker used an AI coding assistant to build a full phishing kit targeting a flagged phantom domain.

title: Create fully native and editable presentations with Gemini in Google Slides
blurb: Google added Gemini-powered presentation generation to Google Slides, letting users build a full multi-slide deck from a single prompt. The feature grounds decks in existing Google Drive files, matches the style of a reference presentation, and produces fully editable slides, with Gemini suggesting relevant files, emails, and chats to enrich the content.

title: Attend, Transform, or Silence: Operator-Level Visual Skipping for Efficient Multimodal LLM Inference
blurb: This paper introduces an operator-level visual-token skipping framework for multimodal LLMs that preserves the full visual-token sequence while selectively bypassing redundant attention, FFN, or both. Across three MLLM architectures and 10 VQA benchmarks, the method cut 33.7% of TFLOPs on Qwen3-VL while retaining 99.5% of the original model's performance.

title: How Far Do On-Prem Open LLMs Get on Text-to-SQL? A Cross-Family Size x Technique Frontier on BIRD
blurb: This benchmark tests how well on-premises open-weight LLMs handle Text-to-SQL on the BIRD development split, evaluating Qwen2.5-Coder, CodeLlama-Instruct, and Llama-3.x across sizes under a matched protocol with McNemar significance testing. Model generation matters more than size and self-correction is a near-free accuracy win, while schema linking shows no benefit even at 96.5% gold-table recall and self-consistency adds just 0.13 points for roughly 5x the tokens.

title: Security researchers tricked LLMs into giving them cocaine recipes by abusing role models for prompt injection
blurb: Security researchers showed that LLMs can be jailbroken into producing prohibited instructions, including cocaine synthesis recipes, by exploiting role-play prompts that override safety guardrails. The team frames the underlying problem as unwinnable in its current form, comparing prompt-injection defense to Whac-a-Mole, since each patched exploit is quickly replaced by a new variation of the same role-model abuse.

title: Venice AI becomes a unicorn with $65M Series A as its privacy-first AI platform takes off
blurb: Venice AI raised a $65M Series A at a $1B valuation, its first external round, led by Dragonfly with participation from Coinbase Ventures and North Island Ventures. The privacy-focused platform offers access to over 200 AI models, encrypts all queries client-side without storing user data, and reports profitability on annualized run-rate revenue above $70M.

title: Cursor releases its iOS app for vibe coding on the go
blurb: Cursor released a native iOS app in public beta that turns the phone into a control surface for its coding agents, letting developers launch agents, select frontier models, inspect diffs, and merge pull requests from a phone. Available to users on paid plans and requiring iOS 26.0 or later, the app supports voice input, Remote Control for agents running on a local machine, and cloud agents that run in isolated VMs.

title: US Lifts Export Restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5
blurb: The Trump administration lifted foreign access restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5 model after the company resolved government safety concerns. The reversal signals Washington's evolving approach to regulating frontier AI exports, weighing national security against maintaining US competitiveness in global markets.

title: Radial Suppression Accelerates Algorithmic Generalization: A Geometric Analysis of Delayed Generalization
blurb: This paper studies why neural networks memorize algorithmic training data long before they generalize, attributing the delay to radial inflation of hidden representations under cross-entropy optimization. The authors introduce a single-hyperparameter norm penalty that constrains activations to a sqrt(d)-radius hypersphere, accelerating grokking up to 6x across MLPs and Transformers and halving training steps for a 10M-parameter nanoGPT on 3-digit addition.

title: AI-Generated PowerShell Malware: An Experimental Framework and Dataset
blurb: This paper introduces an experimental framework for evaluating LLM-generated PowerShell malware, including a sandbox for dynamic analysis and a manually curated, natural-language-annotated dataset of real-world samples. Testing permissive open-weight models adapted to malware generation, the authors found a median Jaccard index of 84.5% between real and AI-generated malware in triggered OS events, with 48.4% of instances achieving complete overlap.

title: Large Databases Need Small, Open-Weight Language Models
blurb: This paper argues that quantized, open-weight language models running locally on 16GB of VRAM can match or exceed closed-source APIs for LM-enhanced database operators, at lower latency and a fraction of the cost. Integrating these local models into the BlendSQL v0.1.0 framework yielded a 390x reduction in overall cost and a 3.8x reduction in latency compared to a proprietary LM API.

title: Mandol: An Agglomerative Agent Memory System for Long-Term Conversations
blurb: Mandol is an agent memory system that consolidates fragmented vector and graph storage into a unified architecture, organizing conversations into a hierarchical model of raw memories and agglomerated abstract memories represented as structured semantic graphs. On the LoCoMo and LongMemEval benchmarks, it achieves the best overall accuracy among representative memory systems while delivering a 5.4x retrieval speedup and 4.8x insertion speedup under 10 QPS concurrent load.

title: Using Large Language Models as Low-Cost Statistical Estimators for Human-Response Data
blurb: This paper shows that pretrained large language models can act as low-cost statistical estimators for human-response data, proving that a well-calibrated model achieves Bayes-optimal risk for any inference depending on the data only through its conditional mean. The authors decompose the LLM's estimation error into representation bias and optimization error, bound the bias via the Pinsker inequality, and provide finite-sample concentration bounds plus a calibration protocol for replacing expensive human-subject experiments.

title: Making AI search smarter
blurb: Cloudflare announced new bot controls and the start of a new economic model for the web, beginning with search, to let sites control who accesses their content and get compensated for it. The move responds to AI answer engines that summarize pages without sending traffic, with a 2025 Pew study finding users clicked a traditional link just 8% of the time when Google showed an AI summary.

title: Together AI raises $800M to grow its AI-optimized public cloud
blurb: Together AI raised $800M in a Series C led by Aramco Ventures, with Nvidia, Vista Equity Partners, and General Catalyst participating, bringing its valuation to $8.3B. The company runs a cloud platform for open-source models whose ATLAS engine uses adaptive speculative decoding to speed up some inference workloads by up to 400%.

title: Sources: SpaceX showed investors a handset-like device prototype with AI tech from xAI, a proprietary OS, a Snapdragon chip, and a design slimmer than an iPhone (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: SpaceX showed investors a prototype of a handset-like device that runs AI technology from xAI on a proprietary operating system and a Qualcomm Snapdragon chip, according to sources cited by the Wall Street Journal. The device is reportedly slimmer than an iPhone, signaling a hardware push that would put SpaceX and xAI in direct competition with Apple and Samsung.

title: What the Saga Over Anthropic’s Mythos Tells Us About the Cyber Risks From AI
blurb: Anthropic said its Mythos AI model is so effective at finding software vulnerabilities that it has withheld general release, limiting access to roughly 200 partner organizations as of early June. The company warned that in the wrong hands the tool could help attackers steal data or disrupt critical infrastructure more easily.

title: Explicit Fuzzy Logic in the Feed-Forward Layer: Self-Forgetting Quantifiers Discover Legible Grammatical-Licensing Detectors
blurb: This paper replaces a transformer's feed-forward layer with fuzzy set operations, where each hidden unit computes explicit logic like intersection and bounded negation ("A but not B") on sigmoid-bounded memberships. Adding a small block of self-forgetting sequence quantifiers ties the GELU baseline's perplexity at 125M parameters on OpenWebText, and the units read as grammatical licensing detectors that fire on a licensor and predict the licensed word.

title: Revocable Learned State via Process Sidecars
blurb: This paper introduces process sidecars, a two-coefficient edit family for revoking a language model's learned memory after later safety training has already transported the memory direction. The authors prove the exact sidecar recovers the safety-only oracle to second order and show that any scalar task-arithmetic edit leaves first-order error, with the 2D edit improving held-out refusal closure across three models.

title: Cloudflare’s new policy pushes AI companies to pay for publishers’ content
blurb: Cloudflare will block "mixed-use" web crawlers from ad-hosting pages by default starting September 15, 2026, unless site owners opt out, in a push to make AI companies separate search crawling from AI training and agent use. The policy applies to new customers, new sites, and all existing free customers, and specifically calls out Google for accessing roughly 2x more content than other AI firms.

title: Red teamers turned Claude Desktop into a double agent to do their evil bidding
blurb: Pentera Labs red teamers compromised a developer's Claude Desktop app and escalated the access into full remote code execution on the victim's machine. The attack chain requires a compromised email inbox plus Claude Desktop installed, then abuses the app's cross-device sync to reach other sessions and its local code-execution features to run commands the victim never approved.

title: Freeform Preference Learning for Robotic Manipulation
