blurb: This paper introduces Freeform Preference Learning (FPL), a method that trains robot manipulation policies from natural-language preference axes like speed, safety, or placement quality rather than single overall trajectory comparisons. Across four real-world and two simulated long-horizon tasks, FPL beat sparse-reward and binary-preference methods by 38 percentage points while learning dense progress signals without subtask segmentation and letting users steer behavior at test time without retraining.

title: Certified Speculative Execution for Untrusted AI Agents
blurb: Certificate-Gated Prefix Acceptance (CGPA) is a certified speculative-execution contract that lets untrusted AI agents accelerate hard-constrained decision systems without forfeiting a trusted solver's feasibility guarantees. A verifier rejects constraint-violating transitions while a conformally calibrated value boundary accepts the longest safe prefix, driving six frozen LLMs to zero applied violations and turning a frozen 8B model into a 2.96x speedup at 2.1% regret.

title: ScarfBench: Benchmarking AI Agents for Enterprise Java Framework Migration
blurb: IBM Research introduced ScarfBench, an open benchmark that evaluates AI coding agents on cross-framework migration across Spring, Jakarta EE, and Quarkus rather than comparing generated code to reference implementations. Migrated applications must build, deploy, and pass behavioral validation, and frontier agents that excel on traditional benchmarks show sharply varying success rates, with whole-application migrations remaining especially difficult.

title: Designing GPU-Accelerated Query Engines with NVIDIA GQE
blurb: NVIDIA published a walkthrough of GQE, a reference architecture for executing SQL queries at high performance over large datasets on GPUs using cuDF and CUDA-X libraries like nvCOMP and nvSHMEM. The post covers techniques for efficient CPU-GPU data movement, compression, partition pruning, and overlapping transfer with computation, and details a three-layer design that consumes open-source Substrait query plans exported from existing databases.

title: Conversational analytics in BigQuery brings trusted agentic reasoning to everyone
blurb: Google made Conversational Analytics in BigQuery generally available, letting business and technical teams query data, run multi-step analyses, and generate visual reports in natural language where the data lives. Built on Gemini models, it connects to Iceberg tables and cross-cloud sources like Databricks Unity, AWS Glue, SAP, and Salesforce, and publishes agents to Gemini Enterprise, Data Studio, or custom apps via the Conversational Analytics API.

title: Anthropic reactivates Fable, Mythos after securing government approval
blurb: Anthropic reactivated its Fable and Mythos frontier models after obtaining government approval to bring them back online. The company restored access despite unresolved vetting issues, leaving questions about the models' safety review process open even as customers regain use of the systems.

title: Scaling LLM Inference: Multi-Node KV Cache Offloading with GKE & Managed Lustre
blurb: Google detailed an architecture that offloads LLM KV caches to Managed Lustre, a dedicated external parallel filesystem, using the llm-d stack on GKE to bypass host-level CPU RAM and SSD capacity limits. On a six-node A3 Mega cluster running Llama-3.3-70B, the approach cut GPU-hour requirements by nearly 60% and delivered over 50% TCO savings at a 95% cache hit rate.

title: Mag 7 value shrinks by $2.3 trillion amid AI spending jitters — but investors are still backing chipmakers
blurb: The Magnificent 7 has lost roughly $2.3 trillion in market value this month as investors scrutinize the hundreds of billions in AI infrastructure spending by Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta, with the CNBC Mag 7 Index down 10% in June. Semiconductor stocks have moved the other way, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index up around 6% this month and more than 90% on the year against a 3.4% decline for the Mag 7.

title: Inference chip startup Etched launches with $800M in funding
blurb: Etched launched with $800M raised across multiple rounds, most recently at a $5B valuation, to build inference-only chips fabricated on TSMC's N4P process. By dropping training circuitry and adding LVI technology that runs a trillion-parameter model at over 80% peak FLOPs without thermal throttling, the company claims FLOP density several times higher than existing AI processors.

title: AWS launches forward-deployed engineering team to speed enterprise agentic AI adoption
blurb: AWS launched a Forward Deployed Engineering department, backed by a $1B investment, that embeds small teams of engineers and AI agents inside enterprise customers to build and customize agentic AI systems. The teams operate on a "45/45/45" metric, aiming to ideate in 45 minutes, validate in 45 hours, and ship in 45 days, then leave the tools behind for the customer.

title: Gemini Spark updates: macOS launch, connected apps and more
blurb: Google released Gemini Spark for macOS in beta to Google AI Ultra subscribers in the US, letting the assistant automate tasks across desktop files and Google Workspace with permissioned file access. The update also adds connected-app integrations for Canva, Dropbox, Instacart, OpenTable, and Zillow Rentals, plus support for custom Model Context Protocol (MCP) connections.

title: Anthropic restores AI models Fable, Mythos after the U.S. lifts export controls
blurb: Anthropic restored access to Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5 after the U.S. government lifted the export controls that forced their suspension last month. Fable 5 returns globally on July 1, while Mythos 5, a less-restricted variant of the same model, is being reintroduced only to approved U.S. organizations following a government review of the jailbreak that let it generate exploit code.

title: How the Senate’s AI AGENT Act could reshape enterprise AI governance
blurb: Senator Mark Warner released a discussion draft of the AI AGENT Act, which would require providers of "custodial user agents" to register with the FTC before accessing large online platforms' interfaces. Its requirement to link each agent to an authorizing user would create continuous traceability obligations, forcing CIOs and CISOs to rethink how they track agent activity and assign responsibility for automated decisions.

title: Neocloud Together AI raises $800M, leaps to $8.3B valuation
blurb: Together AI raised an $800M Series C at an $8.3B valuation, led by Aramco Ventures with participation from Nvidia, Vista Equity Partners, and General Catalyst. The neocloud, which rents Nvidia GPU clusters for running open source models, reports over $1.15B in annual bookings and names Cursor, Cognition, and Decagon among its customers.

title: TikTok launches Agentic Hub
blurb: TikTok launched Agentic Hub, a centralized collection of first-party and third-party AI skills that advertisers can use for campaign creation, creative generation, performance analysis, and catalog management. The hub connects to TikTok's Model Context Protocol server to link its ad platform with outside tools, with skills already published by HubSpot, Wix, Constant Contact, and Mobvista.

title: When LLMs Read Tables Carelessly: Measuring and Reducing Data Referencing Errors
blurb: This paper presents the first systematic evaluation of data referencing errors (DREs), cases where LLMs incorrectly cite or omit table values despite understanding the table's structure. The errors appear across all tested models from 1.7B to 20B parameters, and using data referencing as a critic improves answer accuracy by up to 12.0%, with a lightweight 4B critic hitting 78.2% F1 on detecting both in-distribution and out-of-distribution DREs.

title: AdaJEPA: An Adaptive Latent World Model
blurb: AdaJEPA is an adaptive latent world model that performs test-time adaptation inside the closed loop of model predictive control, recalibrating itself when predictions drift under distribution shift. After executing an action chunk, it uses the observed next-state transition as a self-supervised signal to update the model and replan, improving planning success on goal-reaching tasks with as few as one gradient step per replanning step.

title: GR2 Technical Report
blurb: GR2 (Generative Reasoning Re-Ranker) is an end-to-end framework for the re-ranking stage of industrial recommendation systems, combining mid-training on semantic IDs, reasoning traces distilled from a stronger teacher, and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. It delivers +18.7% R@1 and +9.6% N@3 over legacy baselines on industrial traffic, and the authors show that conditional rewards are essential because LLMs otherwise hack them by preserving incoming order or exploiting position bias.

title: A Lifecycle and Application-Stack Survey of Large Language Model Vulnerabilities: Attacks, Risks, Defenses, and Open Problems
blurb: This paper surveys large language model vulnerabilities through a lifecycle and application-stack lens, arguing that risks now stem from the full system rather than model weights alone. It organizes attacks across eight stages, from data collection to tool/agent execution, and maps them to objectives like confidentiality, integrity, and agency-control. The authors emphasize where trust boundaries fail and why point defenses rarely compose, closing with a research agenda for secure LLM systems.

title: Understanding and Evaluating Claw-like Agent Security Through a Computer-Systems Lens
blurb: This paper introduces SafeClawArena, a benchmark of 406 adversarial tasks across four attack surfaces that evaluates always-on "Claw-like" agents as agentic computer systems, treating the gateway runtime as an OS, Skills as installed apps, and Plugins as privileged extensions. Attack success rates reach 70% and malicious Plugins succeed 100% of the time regardless of the LLM, though SeClaw cuts GPT-5.4's rate to 22% and Claude-Opus-4.6 already sits near that floor everywhere.

title: OpenAI Leans Toward Holding Up I.P.O. Until Next Year
blurb: OpenAI is leaning toward delaying its IPO until next year, as advisers urge Sam Altman to slow down following volatility in SpaceX's stock. The company is weighing the move while grappling with financial challenges, and the cautious timeline reflects concerns about taking a high-burn AI business public into an uncertain market.

title: Qualcomm's proposed solution to catch up in AI infra: Bury the compute under the DRAM
blurb: Qualcomm introduced high-bandwidth compute (HBC), a near-memory architecture that stacks DRAM layers directly on top of its XPUs into a single unified module, launching next year in its AI250-series Dragonfly rack systems. The company claims 768GB of capacity and up to 133 TB/s of "effective" memory bandwidth per card, though it declined to explain how its LPDDR interface matches what Nvidia achieves with eight HBM3e stacks.

title: Build agents even faster with Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform’s fully-managed, remote MCP server
blurb: Google released a fully-managed, remote MCP server for its Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform that connects external agents in IDEs like Claude Code or Antigravity CLI to Google Cloud resources. Through the standardized MCP interface, agents can call Model Garden models, pull shared prompt templates, and manage Notebooks, while native Cloud IAM Deny policies restrict access to authorized resources.

title: Synthesize the big picture and analyze trends with BigQuery's AI.AGG function
blurb: Google released the BigQuery AI.AGG() function in preview, letting users summarize or synthesize unstructured and multimodal data across millions of rows using natural-language instructions in a single line of SQL. Unlike row-level AI functions, AI.AGG() groups and prioritizes records at scale, enabling tasks like surfacing top feature requests in negative reviews or finding hidden inefficiencies in system logs that never throw a FATAL error.

title: Wayve launches $85M employee tender offer at $8.5B valuation
blurb: Wayve launched an $85M tender offer letting employees sell vested equity at the company's $8.5B valuation, its second liquidity event after a similar offering alongside its May 2024 Series C. The self-driving startup, which raised a $1.2B Series D in February, is targeting Uber robotaxi pilots later this year and plans to integrate its software into Nissan's next-generation driver-assist systems.

title: The Lab Mistake That Might Revolutionize Computing
blurb: IEEE Spectrum profiles a neuromorphic device, discovered through a lab error, that mimics the behavior of a biological neuron using far fewer CMOS transistors than conventional designs require. The approach targets the energy gap between GPUs, which draw up to 1,000 watts each, and the human brain, which is roughly a million times more efficient at comparable tasks.

title: Anthropic launches AI drug discovery program, joining tech giants in betting on healthcare
blurb: Anthropic launched an internal drug discovery program focused on treatments for neglected diseases that traditional biopharma companies overlook, running alongside its new Claude Science product for life sciences companies. Life sciences head Eric Kauderer-Abrams framed the in-house effort as a way to build tighter feedback loops with the drugmakers Anthropic is courting, joining Alphabet, Apple, and Amazon in targeting healthcare.

title: Meta wants to rent out its spare AI compute, and Wall Street likes the idea
blurb: Meta is building a cloud business called Meta Compute to rent out surplus AI capacity, according to a Bloomberg report, pitting it against AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure. The plans are early and could take the form of hosted model access like Amazon Bedrock or raw compute sales, and Meta shares jumped more than 10% on the news after a year of investor anxiety over its $115B to $135B 2026 capex guidance.

title: ‘What we’re seeing right now is just rapid escalation in AI token spend’: Accenture tells staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks amid surging costs
blurb: Accenture told some staff to stop using AI for basic tasks like converting PDFs to slides, citing a rapid escalation in token spend that has made costs unpredictable. The consultancy's agentic AI strategy lead said C-level executives are still questioning whether the spending delivers value, and framed the problem as one every AI-using enterprise will face, opening a services opportunity for Accenture to advise clients on it.

title: US government allows Anthropic to redeploy its Mythos and Fable AI models
blurb: The US Commerce Department lifted its export ban on Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models, letting the company restore customer access starting July 1. The reversal came after Anthropic agreed to proactively detect and address security risks, following earlier warnings from Amazon and others that the models were vulnerable to jailbreaks.

title: Claude Sonnet 5 on GitLab: More reliable, more efficient
blurb: Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 5 is now available on the GitLab Duo Agent Platform across all tiers and deployment models through GitLab's AI Gateway. It is the first model to complete every task in GitLab's evaluation suite, up from 93.8% for Sonnet 4.6, while resolving 8.8% more issues to return higher-quality, reviewable code.

title: Breaking Failure Cascades: Step-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Medical Multimodal Reasoning
blurb: This paper introduces Medical Reasoning-aware Policy Optimization (MRPO), a reinforcement learning method that assigns exponentially larger penalties to tokens in early invalid reasoning steps when the final answer is wrong, targeting the cascading errors that drive most failures in medical visual question answering. MRPO cut early-stage reasoning failures from 64% to 13% and, on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, outperformed HuatuoGPT-Vision-34B by 2.79 points despite using a much smaller backbone.

title: SemRF: A Semantic Reference Frame for Residual-Stream Dynamics in Language Models
blurb: SemRF is a formalism that fixes semantic anchors so residual-stream analysis can distinguish real layer-by-layer computation from measurement drift caused by mismatched embedding and unembedding coordinates. With the frame fixed, the authors model computation as a depthwise semantic trajectory and show that lower-curvature, lower-action paths correspond to fewer semantic degrees of freedom, giving a conditional link to parameter efficiency.

title: Random Reshuffling Dominates Stochastic Gradient Descent
blurb: This paper proves that Random Reshuffling, the common practice of sampling data without replacement each epoch, provably dominates standard Stochastic Gradient Descent for smooth convex optimization under any reasonable stepsize after any finite number of epochs. The result removes two longstanding restrictions in prior theory, which held only for stepsizes below a 1/n threshold and left RR's guarantees strictly worse than SGD's when the epoch count was small.

title: Review Residuals: Update-Conditioned Residual Gating for Transformers
blurb: This paper introduces Review Residuals, which scale each transformer sublayer update by a learned, input-dependent gate conditioned on both the current state and the proposed update rather than committing every update with a fixed coefficient of one. Trained from scratch across five sizes, the additive form trains stably at all depths and shows no benefit at small scale but significantly beats parameter-matched Highway and standard residuals at 590M and 1B, with the advantage growing as models get larger.

title: The Decomposition Is the Fingerprint: Per-Component Identity for Agent Skills
blurb: This paper introduces a locality-sensitive fingerprint for AI agent skills that embeds each component and projects it to a fixed 120-byte signature via multi-bank SimHash, compared in constant time by Hamming distance. Keeping the fingerprint as a per-component triple of prompt, code, and tools recovers skill-family identity through paraphrase, renaming, and refactoring, reaching 0.974 AUC while using 77x fewer bits than the embedding it approximates.

title: MESA: Prioritizing Vulnerable Communication Channels for Securing Multi-Agent Systems
blurb: MESA is a label-free framework that ranks which communication edges in a multi-agent system are most security-critical, without requiring any observed attack traces. It combines six graph-theoretic metrics with ablation and masking probes, and across three scenarios, eight topologies, and five open-source LLMs its rankings correlated with per-edge attack success at a mean Spearman ρ of 0.60, letting defenders monitoring the top 10% of edges intercept roughly 3x the attacks of random allocation.

title: AdaTrans: Automated C to Rust Transformation via Error-Adaptive Repair
blurb: AdaTrans is a framework for automatically translating C code to Rust that maps compiler errors to specific repairs using strategy-driven RAG, adapts its behavior based on error type, and validates for both compilability and functional equivalence. On 104 algorithmic problems, it reached a 95.51% mean compilation pass rate and 81.09% solve rate while holding the unsafe file rate to 1.19%.

title: MOA: A Profiling-Guided LLM Framework for Memory-Optimization Automation at Codebase Scale
blurb: MOA is an LLM-driven framework that automatically detects and repairs recurring memory inefficiencies across production-scale codebases using three agents: an Analyzer that mines anti-patterns from profiling data, a Checker Generator that synthesizes static analyzers, and a Patcher that generates fixes. On OpenHarmony's 100M+ lines of C/C++, it produced 769 patches at a 92.5% expert acceptance rate, averaging 42.2% heap reduction and 10.6% smaller binaries.

title: Towards Knowledge Alignment in Code LLMs: Contrastive Unlearning for Evolving APIs
blurb: This paper introduces CURE, a contrastive unlearning method that adapts code LLMs to evolving software libraries by jointly discouraging deprecated API calls and encouraging their correct replacements. On a recent deprecated-API benchmark, CURE reduced outdated API usage, improved correct replacement rates, and outperformed two state-of-the-art baselines while preserving general code generation performance.

title: CLIP: Lightweight Cosine-Law-Based Inverted-List Pruning for IVF-Based Vector Search
blurb: This paper proposes CLIP, a cosine-law-based pruning technique for IVF vector search that supports both inter- and intra-cluster pruning, eliminating irrelevant clusters in O(1) time and filtering vector batches in logarithmic time with negligible overhead. Its variants achieve up to 78% pruning and 69% higher efficiency over static IVF baselines, while the LSM-inspired LSM-IVF design improves throughput by up to 141% over dynamic IVF baselines.

title: Working With AI: A concrete example
blurb: Carson Gross, creator of htmx and hyperscript, walks through a single debugging session with Claude to illustrate what he calls the Sorcerer's Apprentice problem. Claude correctly found the root cause of a parser regression in hyperscript's `as JSON` binding, but Gross argues that leaning on AI without understanding the underlying system leaves developers unable to fix the issues it introduces. The piece is a concrete case study in AI's strengths and its risk of dulling developer judgment.

title: Herdr: Agent multiplexer that lives in your terminal
blurb: Herdr is an open-source terminal multiplexer for AI coding agents, organizing them into workspaces, tabs, and panes with mouse-native click, drag, and split controls. Agents keep running after the client detaches and can be reattached from another terminal, and each pane shows the agent's real terminal output rather than a GUI reinterpretation.

title: South Korea to spend $1T on more memory chip production and humanoid robots
blurb: South Korea's government and top tech firms committed $1 trillion to memory chip fabs, AI data centers, and humanoid robot deployment by 2028. Samsung and SK Hynix account for $585 billion of that to build new plants and double the country's DRAM output within five years, amid labor union pushback over robots entering factories.

title: Meta Is Planning Cloud Business to Take on Amazon, Google
blurb: Meta is developing a cloud infrastructure business that would sell access to its AI computing power and models, according to Bloomberg. The move would put Meta in direct competition with Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud, marking a shift from consuming compute for its own products to selling it to external customers.

title: Morgan Stanley cut its riskiest reconciliation job in half — by making its agents less autonomous
blurb: Morgan Stanley deployed agentic AI in P&L reconciliation, cutting the time to close a single book from up to six hours to two or three, saving roughly 1,500 controller-hours per week. The system, called FIXR, got there by making agents less autonomous: humans stay fully in the loop, and their decisions are iteratively converted into repeatable rules the system can later apply on its own, auto-clearing familiar breaks and flagging uncertain ones for review.

title: Harnessing Textual Refusal Directions for Multimodal Safety
blurb: This paper shows that textual refusal directions extracted from an LLM backbone generalize across image and video modalities, enabling multimodal safety without collecting unsafe multimodal training data. The authors introduce MARS, a training-free method that re-centers activations to correct modality misalignment and adaptively scales steering, achieving consistent safety gains across five multimodal LLMs while preserving utility.

title: CoMet: Context and Multiplicity Decomposition for Multimodal Uncertainty Estimation
blurb: CoMet is a post-hoc method for estimating uncertainty in multimodal large language models by decomposing it into a context term, which captures ambiguity from the task or prompt, and a multiplicity term, which counts how many plausible answers remain compatible with the input. The lightweight module estimates both without autoregressive generation or repeated sampling, improving over baselines on hallucination detection and open-ended and multiple-choice visual question answering benchmarks.

title: Experience Graphs: The Data Foundation for Self-Improving Agents
blurb: This paper introduces Trellis, a data foundation that treats an agent's exploration history, called an experience graph, as first-class queryable database state rather than disposable JSON checkpoints and session logs. Recasting frontier selection as a query and cross-session reuse as vector-seeded graph retrieval, the design reached target speedups roughly 10x faster at 52% lower token cost in KernelEvolve, Meta's production accelerator-kernel optimizer.

title: Schneider Electric acquires Cognite for $3.1B in industrial AI push
blurb: Schneider Electric acquired industrial AI company Cognite for $3.1B in an all-cash deal to strengthen its AI software and industrial data business. Cognite's platform pairs a unified data model and knowledge graph with agentic AI through two products, Data Fusion and Atlas AI, which Schneider plans to integrate with its Aveva CONNECT platform.

title: Optimizing a Neural Reconstruction Pipeline Using NVIDIA Nsight Developer Tools
blurb: This post walks through optimizing NVIDIA Omniverse NuRec, a neural reconstruction pipeline that turns multisensor camera and lidar data into simulation-ready 3D scenes, using NVIDIA Nsight Developer Tools to profile its PyTorch training loops and custom CUDA kernels. The team cut reconstruction times from several hours toward a real-time target where a 30-second capture reconstructs in roughly 30 seconds.

title: Bringing speed and strong cost performance to the market with Gemini Omni Flash and Nano Banana 2 Lite
blurb: Google added two models to its Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform: Nano Banana 2 Lite (Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite Image), now generally available as the fastest and most cost-efficient model in the Nano Banana family, and Gemini Omni Flash, in public preview for video generation and conversational editing. Omni Flash supports character and product swaps, style transfers, and relighting, and natively generates audio with every video output.

title: AWS puts $1 billion into new AI unit to embed engineers with customers, joining growing wave
blurb: AWS announced a $1B investment in a new Forward Deployed Engineering unit that embeds engineers directly inside customers' facilities to build and roll out AI systems. The unit will be seeded with thousands of FDEs working alongside AI agents, making AWS the first hyperscaler to follow the OpenAI and Anthropic embedded-deployment companies launched earlier this year.

title: Powering AI agents: CoreWeave’s validation of Nvidia Vera Rubin signals new chapter for rack-scale computing
blurb: CoreWeave and Nvidia announced the first bring-up and validation of the Nvidia Vera Rubin NVL72 rack-scale system on CoreWeave Cloud. Each rack packs 72 Rubin GPUs, 36 Vera CPUs, and 260 terabytes per second of NVLink 6 bandwidth, targeting agentic workloads that reason continuously and scale unpredictably in production rather than one-off inference.

title: Apify lets AI Agents pay via Coinbase x402 for web tools
blurb: Apify partnered with Coinbase to let autonomous AI agents discover, pay for, and run its web automation Actors through x402, the payment protocol built on the HTTP 402 status code, without an account, subscription, or API key. Agents settle USDC micropayments from a Base wallet on a pay-per-result basis, and roughly 20,000 Pay Per Event Actors are now callable, where about $1 covers 350 Google Maps listings.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Science, a dedicated app for scientists
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Science, a desktop workspace for researchers that builds on Claude for Life Sciences and comes pre-configured with over 60 scientific databases. Running on Opus 4.8, the app analyzes literature, runs multistep analyses, and natively renders scientific artifacts like 3D protein structures and genome tracks, each shipping with the code that generated it and a traceable result history.

title: SpaceX Showed Investors Prototype of Elon Musk’s New AI Device
blurb: SpaceX showed investors a prototype of a new AI device, a sleek handset-like unit designed to integrate xAI technology. The demo positions the device as a hardware channel for Musk's AI ambitions, extending xAI's reach beyond software and into a consumer form factor that could compete with existing smartphones.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Science, and Google and OpenAI are already racing to match it
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Science, a single workspace that integrates researchers' existing databases, notebooks, and terminals and routes heavy jobs like protein folding to a lab's HPC clusters or Modal. Every result carries a trail back to the code and environment that produced it, and Google and OpenAI both shipped near-identical science tools within the past two months.

title: AxDafny: Agentic Verified Code Generation in Dafny
blurb: AxDafny is a verifier-guided repair framework for Dafny that iteratively generates implementations, invariants, assertions, and termination arguments to produce code that passes formal verification. It reaches 92.7% verification success on DafnyBench, beating the strongest prior proof-hint baseline by 6.5 points, and ships with LCB-Pro-Dafny, a 250-problem competition-style benchmark with formal specifications and a verifier-based evaluation harness.

title: Robust Text Watermarking for Large Language Models via Dual Semantic Embeddings
blurb: This paper introduces Dual-Embedding Watermarking (DEW), a semantic watermarking scheme for LLMs that combines contextual and token-level embeddings to survive paraphrasing and translation. DEW projects embedding vectors through pseudo-random matrices seeded with a secret key, and experiments across multiple models show it improves post-paraphrase detection and remains detectable after translation while keeping text quality competitive.

title: EnclaveX: End-to-End Confidential AI with CPU/GPU TEEs
blurb: EnclaveX presents an end-to-end confidential AI workflow that combines CPU and GPU trusted execution environments, pairing Intel TDX and AMD SEV-SNP at the VM level with NVIDIA H200 GPU TEEs at the application level. The paper measures performance overhead on industry benchmarks and flags gaps such as Kubernetes administrators' ability to access confidential VM contents.

title: Rehearsed Multi-Agent Live Product Demonstrations with Real-Time Voice Question Answering
blurb: Rhetor is a multi-agent system that takes a running web application and its source repository and produces a rehearsed live demo with synchronized narration and real-time voice question answering. It merges UI exploration with source-code analysis into tiered features, constrains a scripter to observed UI elements via semantic locators, and runs a pre-presentation rehearsal loop that converged to a 0.92 locator-firing rate on a 53-action workload.

title: How Cursor deploys AI inside the enterprise
blurb: Cursor VP of Forward Deployed Engineering Pauline Brunet describes how her team embeds inside enterprise customers to deploy agents across the entire software development lifecycle, an approach she frames as building an "AI software factory." The interview covers why forward deployed engineering has become a central enterprise AI role, the challenge of expanding agent adoption beyond individual enthusiasts to whole organizations, and what engineers need to demonstrate to move into the work.

title: DEEPMED Search: An Open-Source Agentic Platform for Medical Deep Research with Introspective Verification
blurb: DEEPMED Search is an open-source agentic platform for medical deep research that routes sub-queries to PubMed, web search, or local graph-based knowledge bases based on information density. It adds an introspective verification module, powered by a causal-consistent multi-agent debate framework, that validates retrieved evidence against diagnostic logic before synthesis to produce structured, citation-backed reports.

title: Anthropic releases Claude Sonnet 5 with improved agentic capabilities
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, the successor to Sonnet 4.6, which improves reasoning, coding, and agentic performance while closing much of the gap with the pricier Opus 4.8. Sonnet 5 scores 63.2% on agentic coding, up from 4.6's 58.1% but still behind Opus 4.8's 69.2%, and ships as the default model across Free and Pro plans at $2 per million input tokens.

title: How the AI bubble could pop and take down the global economy, according to the BIS
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements warned that a burst AI investment bubble could spill over into the broader financial system and drag down the global economy. The central bank body pointed to concentrated AI-driven equity valuations and heavy capital spending as risks that could amplify a downturn if sentiment reverses.

title: The DeepMind trio who built a poker AI are now making money for quant hedge funds
blurb: EquiLibre Technologies, a Prague-based AI lab founded by three former DeepMind researchers behind a poker-beating AI, raised a Series A led by Creandum at a $500M valuation. The startup applies reinforcement learning to trading and, in partnership with Tower Research Capital, has run its agents across billions in daily S&P 500 and Nasdaq volume with zero negative months since inception.

title: Warp CEO Zach Lloyd on why software factories are the next phase of coding
blurb: Warp CEO Zach Lloyd argues that within a year most significant software projects will run on automated "software factories" that continuously triage, implement, review, verify, and monitor code changes. His answer is Oz, a new orchestration platform that connects multiple models and coding harnesses across local environments and cloud sandboxes. Lloyd frames the shift as engineers moving from working interactively with individual agents to supervising an automated development loop.

title: Google unveils Nano Banana 2 Lite aka Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite for low cost, 4-second fast enterprise image generations
blurb: Google released Nano Banana 2 Lite, designated Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite Image on its API, which generates 1K-resolution images in under four seconds at a flat $0.034 per 1,000 images. It is available now to enterprise developers through Google AI Studio, the Gemini API, and the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, and ships alongside a public preview of Gemini Omni Flash for conversational video generation and editing.

title: Anthropic finally brings back Claude Fable 5, but you’ll have to live with a temporary usage limit
blurb: Anthropic will restore global access to Claude Fable 5 on July 1, weeks after a US government directive forced it to suspend the model rather than risk serving blocked foreign nationals. Through July 7, Fable 5 usage counts toward up to 50% of the weekly limit on Pro, Max, Team, and select Enterprise plans, and a new government-coordinated safety classifier blocks the flagged jailbreak in over 99% of cases.

title: Anthropic upgrades Claude with new Sonnet 5 model, details here
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, its most agentic mid-sized model, replacing February's Sonnet 4.6 as the default for Free and Pro users. Anthropic says its performance approaches Opus 4.8 on reasoning, tool use, and coding, with introductory pricing of $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens through August 31.

title: Anthropic just released a brand-new Claude Science app for Mac
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Science, a public beta desktop app for macOS and Linux that runs full scientific analyses, connects to 60+ databases, and saves complete provenance on every result. The app ships with specialists for genomics, proteomics, structural biology, and cheminformatics, and uses NVIDIA's BioNeMo Agent Toolkit to reach models like Evo 2, Boltz-2, and OpenFold3.

title: Sonnet 5 Closes Most of the Gap to Opus 4.8 on Agent Work
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, pitched as most of Opus 4.8's capability at well under half the cost. It comes within six points of Opus 4.8 on agentic coding (63.2% vs. 69.2%) and edges slightly ahead on knowledge work, making it the better default for high-volume, always-on agents while Opus 4.8 still owns the hardest coding and offensive-security tasks. Two catches move the math: a new tokenizer and a $2/$10 introductory rate that resets to $3/$15 on September 1, 2026.

title: Chamath Palihapitiya Takes CEO Role at 8090 Labs After $135M Raise
blurb: Chamath Palihapitiya's enterprise AI coding startup 8090 Labs closed a $135M Series A led by Salesforce Ventures, and he will run the company as CEO in his first full-time operating role since leaving Facebook. Founded in January 2024, 8090 sells Software Factory, an AI coding agent built for corporate engineering teams to ship production-grade software with the audit trails and controls enterprises require.

title: Give Your AI a Memory Layer That Survives Sessions
blurb: This deep dive builds a complete local memory layer for AI agents from an empty folder using Mem0 and Qdrant, addressing the problem that a new session wipes prior context regardless of context window size. It tests the stack against contradictory information that most demos skip, and works through the token math, storage costs, and access controls a production memory store needs before handling customer data.

title: DATADOG rachète ADAPTIVE ML pour renforcer son laboratoire d’IA
blurb: Datadog acquired Adaptive ML, a startup specializing in Reinforcement Learning Operations (RLOps), to strengthen its internal AI research lab. The deal is primarily a talent and expertise play in model post-training, letting Datadog turn its operational data into specialized models for observability and cybersecurity; Adaptive ML had raised $20M in 2024, and terms were not disclosed.

title: LUNA: Learning Universal 3D Human Animation Beyond Skinning
blurb: LUNA is an LBS-free neural animation model that maps 2D controls like images, keypoints, and sketches directly into 3D Gaussian deformations, bypassing explicit body fitting through a transformer motion regressor that separates global rigid motion from local dynamics. The authors report competitive visual fidelity against LBS-based methods along with zero-shot cross-identity generalization, making it the first end-to-end 3D animatable model to support implicit 2D driving.

title: Signed-Permutation Coordinate Transport for RMSNorm Transformers
blurb: This paper shows that the residual-stream gauge that lets you move coordinate-indexed objects like steering vectors and sparse autoencoders across checkpoints is architecture-dependent, with RMSNorm models carrying a signed-permutation gauge that permutation-only alignment fails to capture. The authors introduce sign-marginalized Hungarian matching and coordinate-preserving transport, recovering 91.1% of cross-run coordinates at 1500 steps versus 60.3% for endpoint matching and cutting TinyLlama SAE reconstruction error from 1.08 to 0.004.

title: CSO-LLM: Class Subspace Orthogonalization for Post-Training Backdoor Detection and Trigger Inversion in LLMs
blurb: This paper introduces CSO-LLM, a framework for detecting post-training backdoors and inverting triggers in LLMs treated as classifiers, addressing the discrete input space and lack of comprehensive token blacklists that make existing image-based methods hard to port. Its core technique, class subspace orthogonalization, boosts detector sensitivity and specificity while implicitly penalizing candidate triggers that push signals toward an attack's target class, achieving strong detection and accurate trigger recovery across several LLM architectures.

title: Curvature-Guided Module Localization for Low-Rank Detoxification of Backdoored Large Language Models
blurb: This paper introduces a post hoc framework for removing backdoors from poisoned LLMs without full retraining, using activation patching and Fisher/K-FAC curvature analysis to localize the modules that propagate trigger-induced behavior before applying targeted low-rank repair. Tested on poisoned Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct variants with triggers at the start, middle, and end of prompts, the method suppressed trigger-conditioned malicious responses while preserving benign behavior.

title: Governance Gaps in Agent Interoperability Protocols: What MCP, A2A, and ACP Cannot Express
blurb: This paper presents a gap analysis of five agent interoperability protocols (MCP, A2A, ACP, ANP, and ERC-8004) against a six-dimension governance taxonomy covering membership, deliberation, voting, dissent preservation, human escalation, and audit. Voting and dissent preservation are absent across all five protocols, and deliberation is absent or partial. The authors argue that governing agent communities is a missing architectural layer above current standards, not a missing feature within them.

title: FeatX: Editing Software by Editing Features for Repository-Level Code Evolution
blurb: FeatX is a feature-oriented tool that lets developers evolve a repository by editing an extracted epic-feature hierarchy, then uses a three-stage Evolution Agent to translate those feature edits into code patches. Across a user study and replay experiments on 38 real-world commits, it cut cognitive load versus vanilla ChatGPT and improved function-level modification localization F1 by 42.6% over strong LLM baselines, at $0.07 total cost.

title: EvalSafetyGap: A Hybrid Survey and Conceptual Framework for LLM Evaluation-Safety Failures
blurb: This paper argues that LLM evaluation and AI safety share a measurement problem: benchmark, reward-model, and safety scores can climb while the latent properties they track stay unverified. It introduces EvalSafetyGap, a framework built on Goodhart's Law plus an Instability Decomposition and Alignment Trilemma, and runs a ten-model audit measuring capability, behavioral safety, and governance separately. The audit finds capability and adversarial robustness statistically unrelated (Pearson r = +0.232, p = 0.520), with the open-closed safety gap driven mainly by disclosure rather than behavioral robustness.

title: DA-Studio: An Agentic System for End-to-End Data Analysis
blurb: DA-Studio is a web-based agentic system for end-to-end data analysis that autonomously plans multi-step workflows, runs generated code in a sandboxed workspace, and keeps every action trace and intermediate artifact inspectable. It builds executable analysis steps from raw files and natural-language requests through iterative action generation and feedback, and supports streamed traces, artifact preview, code editing and rerunning, and report export.

title: Making Multimodal LLMs Reliable Chart Data Extractors: A Benchmark and Training Framework
blurb: This paper introduces a benchmark of real-world charts without data labels to test whether multimodal LLMs can reverse-engineer accurate data tables, finding that current models reconstruct table structure reliably but fail at precise value recovery. The authors propose a training framework based on progressive, human-like chart reading that reaches state-of-the-art numerical accuracy with a 7B-parameter model.

title: Private AI: Venice.ai, led by crypto vet Erik Voorhees and Seattle’s Jesse Proudman, raises $65M
blurb: Venice.ai, a privacy-focused AI startup that doesn't log user prompts, raised $65M at a $1B valuation in a Dragonfly-led round.

title: How to Govern Autonomous Agents in Enterprise AI Factories
blurb: NVIDIA published its Secure Agent Workspace Reference Design, an architecture that moves agent execution off the user's laptop, browser, or IDE and into a managed workspace where identity, credentials, network access, runtime policy, and audit are enforced consistently. The post details a phased rollout that treats a company-managed VM as the isolation boundary, making agent activity observable, bounded, and revocable before deeper runtime controls are added. The goal is governed, always-on agents that the entire employee base can use without exposing sensitive enterprise systems.

title: Attackers Hijack Exposed AI Endpoints to Power Offensive Ops
blurb: Zenity researchers documented three campaigns between March and May in which attackers hijacked exposed self-hosted LLM endpoints, including Ollama and LiteLLM instances, to run offensive AI operations. The technique requires no software exploit or authentication, just knowledge of an internet-facing inference endpoint, which operators used to power autonomous pen-testing frameworks like Strix and HexStrike AI and a jailbroken Codex agent.

title: Changing AI math could reduce the hardware burden, researchers show
blurb: Researchers introduced SEMQ, an abstraction layer that separates semantic meaning from the underlying vector embeddings used in AI systems. By decoupling the two, the approach aims to change the math behind model operations and cut the hardware burden required to run them, potentially lowering the compute and memory demands of large-scale inference.

title: South Korea’s hot new sensation is 3S+1F – a quadrillion-Won AI plan, not a band
blurb: South Korea unveiled a national AI strategy dubbed "3S+1F" backed by roughly one quadrillion Won in planned spending to build domestic compute, models, and chip capacity. The plan organizes investment around three "S" pillars and one "F" pillar covering sovereign infrastructure, talent, and funding as Seoul works to keep pace with US and Chinese AI efforts.

title: How Schrödinger sped up molecular discovery by 4x with Alphaevolve
blurb: Schrödinger used AlphaEvolve, Google DeepMind's evolutionary coding agent, to speed up its machine-learned force field training pipeline by 4x. The agent targeted the Ewald summation, a performance bottleneck in Schrödinger's PyTorch code that lacked a vectorized implementation and relied on slow for-loops. AlphaEvolve iteratively evolved a batched version using parallel matrix multiplication, raising throughput while passing a full regression and correctness test suite.

title: Why Agent Loops Fail in Production (and the Database Patterns That Fix Them)
blurb: This CockroachLabs post argues that agent loops fail in production because of the data layer, not the model, mapping each common failure to a database pattern that prevents it. It provides working PostgreSQL and CockroachDB code for preserving consistent state, recovering from interruptions, and maintaining an auditable record across loop iterations.

title: Venice AI, which offers access to 200+ AI models while allowing users to retain their privacy, raised a $65M Series A led by Dragonfly at a $1B valuation (Ram Iyer/TechCrunch)
blurb: Venice AI raised a $65M Series A led by Dragonfly at a $1B valuation, positioning its privacy-focused platform that offers access to more than 200 AI models. The service lets users tap a broad model catalog without surrendering their data, differentiating it from mainstream assistants that retain user activity.

title: Mastering Agentic Techniques: AI Agent Reinforcement Learning
blurb: NVIDIA published a guide on using reinforcement learning to build specialized, long-running agents, covering when RL beats prompting, RAG, or added tools and how to run a first verifiable RL training loop. It walks through defining success criteria as verifiers that score outputs via tests, tool execution, schema validation, or LLM-as-judge review, using open Nemotron models with NeMo RL and NeMo Gym.

title: AI Data Centers Have Been Great for the Steel Industry. Now, a Power Crisis Looms.
blurb: AI data center construction has driven strong demand for steel, but steelmakers now warn they are competing with those same data centers for electricity. The power crunch threatens to raise operating costs and constrain output at energy-intensive mills, turning a major source of new orders into a direct competitor for grid capacity.

title: Google's Gemini Omni Flash hits the API, turning enterprise video production into a conversation
blurb: Google released Gemini Omni Flash to developers and enterprise customers through its API, the first model in its new "Omni" family after debuting to consumers at I/O 2026. The model takes text, images, and video and returns a finished clip with synced audio, and supports conversational editing that lets users relight, reframe, or re-wardrobe a shot without regenerating from scratch.

title: Stathera nabs $55M to make vacuum-sealed silicon oscillators for AI chips
blurb: Stathera raised $55M in a Series A led by Maverick Silicon, with participation from MediaTek's venture arm and Celesta Capital, to build vacuum-sealed silicon oscillators for AI chips. The Montreal company merges a processor's clock and timekeeping oscillator into one silicon device fabbed alongside chip wafers, cutting oscillator footprint by up to 85% while lowering power draw.

title: Tim Cook and EU tech chief hold ‘constructive’ virtual meeting over Siri AI standoff
blurb: Tim Cook held a virtual meeting with EU Executive Vice-President Henna Virkkunen over Apple's decision to withhold Siri AI from the European Union, which both sides described as constructive. Apple blames the Digital Markets Act and a rejected 18-month transition plan for the delay, while the European Commission maintains that nothing in the DMA prevents Apple from shipping the feature.

title: Anthropic may impose KYC restrictions for Fable 5 access
blurb: Anthropic appears set to bring back Claude Fable 5, with fresh strings in the latest iOS build pointing to a credit-gated return that may require know-your-customer identity verification to receive credits. The KYC gate would let Anthropic enforce a likely US-only restoration tied to an export control directive that pulled the model globally earlier this month, restoring access for vetted domestic users.

title: Google Makes Gemini's Personalized Image Generation Free in the US
blurb: Google made Gemini's personalized image generation free for eligible US users on Monday, ending a requirement that had limited the Nano Banana-powered feature to Plus, Pro, and Ultra subscribers. The tool builds images from a user's inferred interests and can insert their real likeness by pulling from connected Google Photos, Gmail, YouTube, and Search rather than a typed description.

title: Google's new Nano Banana 2 Lite image model is its fastest and cheapest yet
blurb: Google DeepMind released Nano Banana 2 Lite, part of the Gemini 3.1 family, which generates images in about 4 seconds versus 20 for standard Nano Banana. It costs roughly $0.034 per 1K images, half the rate of Nano Banana 2, though Google notes it struggles more with text, infographic data, and character consistency.

title: PolicyGuard: From Organizational Policies to Neuro-SymbolicCompliance Review Engines
blurb: PolicyGuard is a neuro-symbolic framework that converts organizational policies into an executable review engine of typed relational logic rules and atom-level extraction questions. LLMs answer local questions from retrieved document evidence while a symbolic evaluator applies the formal rules to flag non-compliance, an approach the authors evaluate on company-specific NDA contract review to make decisions more inspectable, maintainable, and testable.

title: MECoBench: A Systematic Study of Multimodal Agent Collaboration in Embodied Environments
blurb: MECoBench is a benchmark for evaluating how multimodal large language models collaborate as embodied agents in visually grounded environments, spanning diverse real-world tasks, two cooperation structures, and three collaboration modes. Across experiments, the authors find that collaboration generally improves task completion when coordination costs stay manageable, that communication drives most of the gains, and that the best mode depends on team size and model capability.

title: Automated Background Swapping for Robustness against Spurious Backgrounds
blurb: This paper introduces AutoBackSwap, a data augmentation method that reduces image classifiers' reliance on spurious background correlations by disentangling foreground from background, inpainting synthetic backgrounds, and recombining them into new training samples. Patch-wise labeling of a few hundred samples suffices to train the secondary network, and the method outperforms prior work even when no training example breaks the spurious correlation.

title: FlexViT: A Flexible FPGA-based Accelerator for Edge Vision Transformers
blurb: FlexViT is a reconfigurable FPGA accelerator for Vision Transformer inference on resource-constrained edge devices, mapping both fully connected and convolutional layers onto a unified INT8 GEMM engine via runtime im2col. It uses a dual-mode dataflow that switches between input and weight reuse at runtime, achieving up to 2.74x speedup on accelerator-executed layers and 1.40x end-to-end over CPU-only execution on a PYNQ-Z2 board.

title: Accelerating Conformal Prediction via Approximate Leave-One-Out
blurb: This paper accelerates conformal prediction by replacing exact leave-one-out refits with approximate leave-one-out (ALO) estimators, and proves the resulting intervals retain asymptotic coverage and efficiency. Simulations show the ALO-based methods match the coverage and efficiency of exact approaches like Jackknife+ while significantly cutting runtime.

title: Low-dimensional topology of deep neural networks
blurb: This paper studies feedforward networks, ResNets, and transformers by restricting each layer to a width of three, tracking how low-dimensional topological invariants like linking number change across layers. The authors prove that ResNet skip connections and transformer attention are equally powerful at altering linking numbers, both strictly exceed monotonic feedforward networks, and that swapping in a nonmonotonic activation lifts a feedforward network into the same expressivity class.

title: Off the Rails: Hijacking the Scoring Head in Generative End-to-End Driving Planners with Safety-Violating Adversarial Perturbations
blurb: This paper introduces Derail, an adversarial framework that attacks the scoring head in generative end-to-end autonomous driving planners, the single learned component that selects a final trajectory from candidate paths. Across multiple diffusion-based and vocabulary-based planners, Derail flips selection from safe to unsafe trajectories with score drops of 39-80% and collision rates up to 50%, outperforming generic loss-maximization and feature-divergence attacks.

title: Do Machines Struggle Where Humans Do? LLM and Human Comprehension of Obfuscated Code
blurb: This paper evaluates whether large language models share the comprehension failures that code obfuscation induces in human programmers, testing several LLMs across five obfuscation tiers using the Block Model. Reasoning-tuned models aligned significantly with human difficulty patterns while instruction and coder-tuned models showed near-zero correlation, and performance under control-flow flattening degraded in proportion to state-space complexity.

title: Falsification, Not Exposure: An Internally Preregistered Placebo-Controlled Decomposition of Self-Repair Feedback in Frozen Small Code Models
blurb: This paper tests whether self-repair feedback in small frozen code models works by re-exposing the model to its failing code or by supplying an external, executable counterexample. Across 290 dead task cells from HumanEval+/MBPP+ with three 0.5B-1.5B models, code-plus-facts recovered +18 unlocks over bare-code retry while instruction-only prompts showed no distinguishable effect, indicating the gains come from external criticism rather than self-critique.

title: The Human Creativity Benchmark
blurb: This paper introduces the Human Creativity Benchmark (HCB), which evaluates creative AI by separating two signals professionals produce: convergence around shared best practices and divergence reflecting legitimate differences in taste. Across 15,000 professional judgments spanning five creative domains and three workflow phases, convergence concentrated on verifiable dimensions like technical correctness and visual hierarchy, while divergence concentrated on taste-driven dimensions like aesthetic direction, and no model excelled uniformly across all phases.

title: Legible Shared Autonomy: Implicit Communication of Robot Belief through Motion
blurb: This paper introduces legible shared autonomy, a method that makes a robot arm's inferred goal visible through its motion rather than moving along ambiguous shortest paths toward an assumed target. The system modulates communication strength via confidence-aware authority allocation, acting assertively when certain and ceding control when uncertain, and user studies with a six-degree-of-freedom arm showed improved understanding of robot beliefs and reduced control effort.

title: The Gemini app is bringing personalized image creation to more users.
blurb: Google expanded personalized image generation in the Gemini app to all eligible U.S. users for free, connecting its Personal Intelligence feature with the Nano Banana model and Google Photos. The opt-in integration pulls context and actual photos from connected Google apps like Gmail and Photos, letting users generate tailored images from simple prompts like "create an illustration of me and my favorite things" without manual uploads.

title: Amplifying Membership Signal Through Chained Regeneration
blurb: This paper introduces MADreMIA, a model-agnostic framework that strengthens membership inference and dataset inference attacks by chaining generations iteratively, where each output feeds the next input rather than relying on weak one-shot signals. Memorized training samples degrade more slowly and stay more coherent across these regeneration trajectories, and the authors report gains at low false-positive rates across diffusion, language, and autoregressive image models, with early results for audio.

title: Making Sense of Touch from the Child's View for Contrastive Learning
blurb: This paper introduces a structured coding system for baby-centric touch events, producing a dataset of 264k two-second clips coded by that system to study whether touch drives infants' learning of visual concepts. The authors pretrain developmentally grounded models on the dataset via contrastive learning, surfacing early evidence for how much babies rely on touch for visual learning.

title: Alex Karp calls US reliance on AI labs for military tech "effing insane", pans AI fees for businesses, and touts Palantir's Nvidia Nemotron deal for US agencies (Ty Roush/Forbes)
blurb: Palantir CEO Alex Karp called US dependence on private AI labs for military technology "effing insane" and criticized the fees these companies charge businesses. He also promoted Palantir's new deal to integrate Nvidia's Nemotron models into AI systems used by US government agencies.

title: Tidal AI Policy
blurb: Tidal will badge fully AI-generated tracks with an "AI" label and block them from earning royalties or direct-to-fan sales, with monetization cut off immediately and the full policy taking effect July 15. The streamer will also use automated tools to remove tracks that impersonate artists, joining Spotify, Apple Music, and Deezer in setting rules for AI music.

title: Inside Genebench-Pro
blurb: OpenAI shared a look inside Genebench-Pro, a benchmark for evaluating how well frontier models handle genomics and gene-related reasoning tasks. The post walks through case studies of model performance across real-world biological problems, detailing where current systems succeed and where they still fall short on specialized domain knowledge.

title: Google’s Gemini Omni Flash and Nano Banana 2 Lite support slick media content creation at lower costs
blurb: Google added Gemini Omni Flash and Nano Banana 2 Lite to its Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, two media-focused models built for cheaper image and video generation. Omni Flash enters public preview at 10 cents per second of video output and supports conversational editing, letting users swap characters, adjust camera angles, and relight scenes with natural language, with WPP already integrating it into its Open platform.

title: L’Autriche invite Bruxelles à explorer une implantation d’Anthropic dans l’Union européenne
blurb: Austria's State Secretary for Digitalization, Alexander Pröll, asked the European Commission to study establishing a strategic Anthropic presence inside the European Union. The request, sent in a letter to Commissioner Henna Virkkunen, reflects growing concern that US restrictions on the most advanced AI models could leave Europe without reliable access to frontier capabilities.

title: Real-Time Source-Free Object Detection
blurb: RT-SFOD is a source-free object detection method built on the NMS-free YOLOv10 dual-head detector that adapts to domain shifts under tight latency and memory budgets. It combines Dual-Head Pseudo-Label Fusion and a Multi-scale Adaptive Representation Diversification loss, both training-time only, to deliver 1.4 to 3.5% mAP gains, 1.3x higher throughput, and roughly 2x fewer parameters than prior state-of-the-art SFOD methods.

title: Probing Memorization of Tabular In-Context Learning
blurb: This paper introduces ICLMEM, a probing framework that separates context-based predictions from parametric memorization in large tabular models that use in-context learning. Testing a leading real-world-trained model, the authors detected moderate memorization in 8 of 10 tasks (AUC up to 0.67), strongest on low-cardinality and binary tasks, though the signals largely vanished under realistic training conditions.

title: ScratchWorld: Evaluating If World Models Compute Executable Consequences
blurb: ScratchWorld is an offline benchmark that treats Scratch projects as executable worlds, using a pinned VM to produce replay-verified transitions, hidden variables, and counterfactual outcomes for testing next-state, causal, and long-horizon prediction. Across a 659-example release, seven language and reasoning models reach at most 13.8% on its value-aware changed-field F1 metric, while simply copying the input state inflates implied full-state accuracy to 98%.

title: Failure-Based Testing for Deep Reinforcement Learning Agents
blurb: This paper introduces Prior Random Testing (PRT), a black-box method for uncovering critical failures in deep reinforcement learning agents that reward-guided testing misses once agents are well-trained. PRT uses task difficulty as a cue to prioritize failure-prone regions of the input domain, and across four benchmarks it cut the cost of finding the first failure by over 50% versus random testing while preserving test-case diversity.

title: Loc2Repair: A Framework for Evaluating the Impact of File-Level Issue Localization in Repo-Level LLM Repair
blurb: Loc2Repair is a modular evaluation framework that decouples file-level issue localization from patch synthesis in repository-grounded LLM repair, letting researchers pair different localizers and repair backbones under matched conditions. On SWE-bench Verified, explicit localization lifted pooled resolved rate from 44.7% to 48.9-49.1% with predicted localizers and 52.4% with gold file sets, while also cutting mean elapsed time.

title: Palantir's Karp bashes OpenAI, Anthropic token model: 'Something has gone completely wrong'
blurb: Palantir CEO Alex Karp criticized the token-based pricing used by OpenAI and Anthropic, telling CNBC that "something has gone completely wrong" as enterprises tire of rising costs and "tokenmaxxing." He pointed enterprises toward open-weight and proprietary models for cheaper control over their compute and data, and cited China's fast-improving labs as a reason U.S. dominance can't be assumed.

title: TwelveLabs raises $100M to bring superintelligence to AI video models
blurb: TwelveLabs raised $100M in a Series B co-led by NEA and NAVER Ventures, bringing its total funding past $207M to expand its video-understanding foundation models. The company builds multimodal models like the Marengo and Pegasus families that natively parse video into structured data, maintaining memory that persists across queries so models can reason over scenes, entities, and time segments.

title: Matrix Orthogonalization Improves Memory in Recurrent Models
blurb: This work orthogonalizes the mLSTM memory matrix during reads to improve noisy associative recall in recurrent models, borrowing the equalization idea from the Muon optimizer.

title: DigitalOcean Evaluations: Production Model and Router Testing for the Inference Stack
blurb: DigitalOcean added Evaluations to its Inference Engine, letting teams validate any catalog, fine-tuned, BYOM, or router configuration on their own data before production. The feature runs LLM-as-a-Judge scoring with per-item rationale across six pre-built metrics, tracks latency, token, and cost per run, and supports custom rubrics, reusable presets, and MCP-triggered evaluations for CI pipelines.

title: Anthropic says Trump admin has lifted export controls on Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5
blurb: Anthropic said the Commerce Department has lifted the export controls that forced it to pull Fable 5 and Mythos 5 in mid-June, when the government demanded it block all foreign-national access, including its own employees abroad. Fable 5 returns to Claude, Claude.AI, and Claude Code this week, with Mythos 5 restored for select U.S. organizations under approved safeguards. The standoff landed as cheaper Chinese open-source models closed the gap, and executives warned that every week of restricted U.S. rollout handed those developers time to catch up.

title: U.S. Lifts Restrictions on Anthropic’s Most Powerful A.I. Models
blurb: The U.S. lifted its restrictions on Anthropic's most powerful AI models, letting the company bring its top systems back online and ending a months-long standoff with the Trump administration. The reversal removes a regulatory overhang that had capped what one of the frontier labs could ship, and it signals that direct confrontation with Washington is no longer the default cost of operating at the frontier. For founders building on Anthropic, the practical read is fewer capability gaps and less platform risk from a supplier whose roadmap was, until now, partly hostage to a political fight.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 at a steep discount to its top model as the company races toward a blockbuster IPO
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a near-flagship model priced well below its top-tier Opus, and made it the default for Free and Pro users. It scores 63.2% on SWE-bench Pro against Opus 4.8's 69.2%, matches Opus on tool-assisted reasoning, and even edges it out on GDPval knowledge work, all while costing roughly 40% less per token. The move reads as IPO positioning: pushing frontier-grade agentic capability down to mid-tier pricing builds the broad developer adoption that looks good in an S-1, just as the public market prepares to test AI's private valuations.

title: Realta Fusion generates electricity directly from a fusion reaction, an apparent first
blurb: Realta Fusion says it powered a lightbulb using electricity harvested directly from its WHAM demonstration device, which it believes is a private-company first. Instead of using fusion heat to make steam and spin a turbine, the Wisconsin startup captured charged alpha particles at roughly 90% efficiency, compared with about 33% for the steam turbines in today's fission plants. That efficiency matters because the central hurdle in fusion is building a reactor that produces more energy than it consumes, and Realta estimates recirculating this power could lift a commercial plant's total output by 20% to 30%.

title: Anthropic Reaches Deal With Trump Administration to Restore Access to Fable AI Model
blurb: Anthropic reached a deal with the Trump administration to restore access to its Fable model, ending a 2½-week shutdown triggered by security concerns. The outage rippled through the AI industry, cutting off teams that had wired Fable into production and exposing how much downstream software now depends on a single frontier model staying online. The episode is a reminder that government intervention is now a real operational risk in the model supply chain, and the founders least exposed are the ones who kept a fallback provider ready to switch to.

title: Sonnet 5 review: I ran 64 generations to find out if it's worth it
blurb: Tired of one-off vibe checks, the author built a repeatable eval harness in under 45 minutes with Claude Code, then ran Sonnet 5 blind against Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5, and Gemini 3 Pro across PRD quality, prototype generation, and agentic tasks. Scoring blended human gut-feel ratings at 70% with LLM-as-judge at 30%, since neither alone holds up. The payoff is a per-task recommendation: one model for PRDs, another for complex prototypes, a third for daily agent chat.

title: Trump drops restrictions on Anthropic’s Mythos and Fable models
blurb: The U.S. lifted the export-license requirement on Anthropic's Mythos and Fable models, and the lab will begin restoring public access on July 1. The restriction, added June 12, had forced Anthropic to cut off access entirely after compliance proved impractical at scale, in exchange for commitments the lab had mostly pledged voluntarily months earlier. The reversal tracks competitive pressure as much as security, with Asian models like Fugu and Tulongfeng closing on Mythos-level capability. For founders, the takeaway is the lack of a rulebook: model access now hinges on case-by-case White House approval, leaving no clear signal on what governs future releases.

title: Apple Seeks to Buy Chinese-Made Memory Chips With Lobbying Push
blurb: Apple is negotiating to buy memory chips from two Chinese semiconductor makers on the Pentagon's blacklist, a move to blunt a global memory shortage that has already pushed prices up across its product line. The lobbying push shows how severe the DRAM and NAND crunch has become when the most supply-chain-disciplined company in tech is willing to court blacklisted suppliers and the political blowback that follows. For any founder building hardware, the takeaway is that memory scarcity is now a first-order cost problem, not a procurement footnote.

title: Crunchbase Data: Q2 Brought The Most Billion-Dollar Startup Exits Since 2021
blurb: Big startup exits are back, but the story is size, not count. Q2 2026 brought the most billion-dollar venture-backed exits since the 2021 peak, led by SpaceX's record IPO, which hit a $2.1 trillion first-day market cap and raised $75 billion, followed days later by its $60 billion acquisition of Cursor, the priciest purchase of a private startup ever. Deal counts are still well below the 2021 boom, but returns blow past it, and with Anthropic and OpenAI filing confidentially for IPOs that could test the trillion-dollar mark, the size trend has room to run.

title: Google is rationing Gemini access to Meta because it cannot provide enough compute
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models because it cannot supply the compute Meta wanted, forcing Meta to ration AI tokens internally and accelerate its shift to an in-house model called Muse Spark. The signal is stark: Google is spending over $180B on capex this year and still renting 110,000 Nvidia GPUs from SpaceX for $920M a month, yet cannot serve a customer as large as Meta. When the company with one of the world's biggest infrastructure pools is rationing access, the AI buildout has clearly fallen behind consumption, and depending on a competitor's models is now a liability worth billions to escape.

title: Rocket Lab buys its way into the satellite big league with $8B Iridium deal
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to buy satellite operator Iridium for $8B, moving from launch provider into owning an orbital constellation and a recurring connectivity business. The deal vaults it from rocket vendor to vertically integrated space company, putting it in the same league as SpaceX rather than competing for launch contracts underneath it. For a sector where launch is a thin-margin commodity, the strategic read is that the money is in owning what flies, not just getting it there.

title: Robinhood Rolls Out Perpetual Futures Tied to Commodities, ETFs
blurb: Robinhood is rolling out perpetual futures tied to commodities, ETFs, and currencies in Europe, extending the round-the-clock trading model that started in crypto to mainstream asset classes. Perpetuals never expire and settle continuously, so Robinhood can offer leveraged, 24/7 exposure without the rollover friction of traditional futures. The move pushes retail brokerages toward always-on markets and puts pressure on incumbents whose products still stop trading at the closing bell.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix to Spend $520 Billion on Chip Plants in South Korea
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix said they will spend $520B building chip plants in South Korea, after concluding their existing investments wouldn't keep pace with demand. The two largest memory-chip makers are responding to a worldwide shortage that current capacity can't cover. For founders building on AI, the constraint is moving upstream: memory and compute supply, not model quality, is increasingly what sets the ceiling on what you can ship and what it costs.

title: Sources: SpaceX showed investors a handset-like device prototype with AI tech from xAI, a proprietary OS, a Snapdragon chip, and a design slimmer than an iPhone (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: SpaceX is building a phone. Sources say it showed investors a handset-like prototype running xAI models, a proprietary OS, and a Snapdragon chip in a body slimmer than an iPhone, pairing the device with Starlink's direct-to-cell network. The play is to own the full stack from satellite to silicon to model, cutting Apple and Google out of the loop that sits between users and the network. For founders, it signals that the next platform war may be fought over integrated hardware plus connectivity plus AI, not any one layer alone.

title: Sources: SoftBank has reopened talks for a $10B loan backed by its OpenAI stake and is offering to guarantee repayment if OpenAI collateral proves insufficient (Echo Wang/Reuters)
blurb: SoftBank has reopened talks for a $10B loan backed by its OpenAI stake, and it is offering to personally guarantee repayment if the OpenAI collateral falls short. The structure signals how illiquid these mega-stakes have become, with even a lead backer unable to borrow cleanly against paper worth tens of billions. When the largest AI investor has to cover the gap itself to unlock cash, it shows how much of the current boom's value is locked up in positions no one can easily sell.

title: Databricks’ Co-Founder Arsalan Tavakoli: Every Software Monopoly Falls in the Next 24 Months
blurb: Databricks co-founder Arsalan Tavakoli-Shiraji says any business with a monopoly today won't have one 12 to 24 months from now, as build costs, the low end, and migrations break pricing power at once. He has unusual visibility here, with Databricks running at a $6.9B revenue run-rate, growing 80%+, and AI products alone past a $1.7B run-rate. His sharper read is that enterprises are token maxing without knowing their true AI ROI, so the opening for founders is to tie AI spend to a clear business outcome rather than just standing up agents.

title: Venice AI becomes a unicorn with $65M Series A as its privacy-first AI platform takes off
blurb: Venice AI raised a $65M Series A at a $1B valuation, its first outside money, led by crypto firm Dragonfly with Coinbase Ventures joining. The pitch is privacy: it hosts uncensored open-source models and proxies queries to OpenAI and Anthropic, encrypting input client-side and storing nothing. Two years in, it is already profitable at over $70M in run-rate revenue, serving 3 million users and 1.7 million API calls a day. The bet is that demand for unrestricted, private AI grows faster than the safeguards labs keep adding.

title: Neocloud Together AI raises $800M, leaps to $8.3B valuation
blurb: Together AI raised an $800M Series C at an $8.3B valuation, led by Aramco Ventures, more than doubling the $3.3B mark it hit 16 months ago. The neocloud rents Nvidia GPU clusters and now claims over $1.15B in annual bookings, as companies route more work to cheap open-source models instead of paying frontier-token premiums. That shift has tripled open-source model usage in the past year, and the capital is flooding the picks-and-shovels layer, with Upscale AI and TensorWave each raising nine figures last month.

title: US Lifts Export Restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5
blurb: Washington lifted foreign-access restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5 after the company resolved the government's safety concerns, opening the frontier model to overseas markets. The reversal shows the emerging playbook for regulating frontier AI: export controls become a lever labs negotiate against, and clearing them is now a distribution milestone as much as a compliance one. For founders building on the frontier labs, model availability in a given country increasingly depends on Washington's terms, not just the vendor's roadmap.

title: Apple takes Epic fight over app store fees to the Supreme Court
blurb: Apple asked the Supreme Court to overturn a contempt finding that bars it from charging commissions when developers route iPhone users to outside payment methods. The 9th Circuit ruled Apple violated the spirit of an anti-steering order by imposing a 27% fee on link-outs, high enough to have a prohibitive effect on developers. The case now hinges on whether courts can find contempt based on an injunction's spirit or only its explicit language, a question that reaches well beyond App Store economics into how tightly any regulated company can be held to court orders.

title: Cloudflare’s new policy pushes AI companies to pay for publishers’ content
blurb: Cloudflare will block "mixed-use" AI crawlers by default starting September 15, 2026, forcing model providers to separate search bots from those used for agents and training or lose access to any page hosting ads. The change hits new customers and all existing free sites automatically, so publishers who never touched their settings suddenly gain leverage over how their content gets used. With bots now the majority of internet traffic, Cloudflare is betting that gating training data at the network layer pushes AI companies toward paying for content rather than scraping it for free.

title: Together AI raises $800M to grow its AI-optimized public cloud
blurb: Together AI raised $800M in a Series C led by Aramco Ventures, with Nvidia, Vista, and General Catalyst joining, valuing the open-model cloud at $8.3B. The bet is that inference gets won on price and speed, not just raw GPU access. Its ATLAS engine uses adaptive speculative decoding to claim up to 400% faster workloads, and its Batch Inference tier cuts cost up to 50% for jobs that don't need instant answers. For founders building on open-source models, the infrastructure layer is consolidating around vendors that optimize the whole stack, not just rent chips.

title: Record chip rally adds $2 trillion in combined value to Micron, Intel and AMD in second quarter
