blurb: The AI chip rally in Q2 added roughly $2 trillion in combined market cap to Micron, Intel, and AMD, pushing all three into the 10th, 11th, and 12th spots among US tech companies. The money is rotating out of Nvidia and the hyperscalers into the rest of the stack, as investors bet the AI capex buildout lifts memory, CPUs, networking, and chip designers, not just GPUs. Nvidia gained only 15% while Micron tripled and reported gross margins jumping to 85% from 39% a year earlier on surging memory prices.

title: Mag 7 value shrinks by $2.3 trillion amid AI spending jitters — but investors are still backing chipmakers
blurb: Roughly $2.3 trillion has been wiped off the Magnificent 7 this month as investors scrutinize the hundreds of billions Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta are pouring into chips and data centers, some of it funded by debt. The market is re-rating these names from asset-light cash machines into balance-sheet-heavy AI builders, and the payoff thesis rides on next month's Q2 earnings. The money isn't leaving the trade, just moving down the stack: the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index is up 6% in June and more than 90% this year while the Mag 7 slid.

title: Digital Realty falls 5% after taking $3.5 billion stake in Blackstone's Virginia data centers
blurb: Digital Realty bought Blackstone's stake in three Northern Virginia data centers for $3.5 billion, a portfolio valued at $7.8 billion, and its stock fell 5% on the news. The deal lands in the world's largest data center market, where 92% of North American capacity under construction is already pre-committed and vacancy is set to stay low through 2030. Hyperscalers like Amazon, Microsoft, Meta, and Google are driving roughly $700 billion in AI infrastructure capex this year, and they are increasingly funding it through private equity, private credit, and debt rather than their own balance sheets.

title: Inference chip startup Etched launches with $800M in funding
blurb: Etched launched with $800M raised across multiple rounds, most recently at a $5B valuation backed by TSMC's venture arm alongside Geoffrey Hinton, Fei-Fei Li, and Andrej Karpathy. The bet is narrow on purpose: by dropping training circuits and optimizing only for inference on TSMC's N4P process, the chip claims several times the FLOP density of existing AI processors and can run a trillion-parameter model at 80%+ peak FLOPs without thermal throttling. The inference layer Nvidia's Rubin serves as a side job is now drawing funded, specialized challengers going after it directly.

title: Restaurants can now accept orders placed directly from ChatGPT and Claude thanks to Square's new, low-fee, no setup integration
blurb: Square launched a ChatGPT app and Claude plugin that let customers order directly from restaurants inside those AI tools, with no technical setup and no marketplace commission. That undercuts the delivery aggregators head-on, where DoorDash takes 15% to 30% and Uber Eats 20% to 30% for placement and fulfillment. Square still charges its standard online-ordering fee of about 2.9% to 3.3% plus $0.30, and pulls live catalog, pricing, and stock so agents never surface out-of-stock items. The bet is that AI chat becomes a new ordering surface, and the restaurants that show up there keep the margin the aggregators used to take.

title: Meta wants to rent out its spare AI compute, and Wall Street likes the idea
blurb: Meta is reportedly building a cloud business, under a new unit called Meta Compute, to rent out the surplus AI capacity from its $115B to $135B 2026 capex plan. Selling either model access or raw compute would put it head to head with AWS, Google Cloud and Azure, and turn idle infrastructure into revenue instead of a sunk cost. The market read it as relief, with shares jumping more than 10% after a year down close to 15%, a sign investors will reward any credible path to earning back the AI build-out.

title: Wayve launches $85M employee tender offer at $8.5B valuation
blurb: Wayve launched an $85M employee tender offer at its $8.5B valuation, letting staff cash out vested equity that new and existing investors are buying up. The offer is the second such liquidity event for the UK self-driving company and part of a growing pattern where AI startups use tenders as a retention tool, joining Decagon, ElevenLabs, Linear, and Clay in giving employees a reason to stay rather than wait years for an exit. Investors keep funding these rounds because they want more equity in high-growth companies, even at a premium.

title: Meta Is Planning Cloud Business to Take on Amazon, Google
blurb: Meta is developing plans to build a cloud infrastructure business that would sell access to AI compute and models, putting it in direct competition with AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud. The move would turn Meta's massive AI capex into a revenue line rather than a pure cost center, monetizing capacity it built for its own models. For founders, it signals a fourth major hyperscaler entering the market, with more pricing pressure and more supplier options for anyone renting GPUs.

title: ‘What we’re seeing right now is just rapid escalation in AI token spend’: Accenture tells staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks amid surging costs
blurb: Accenture has told staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks like converting PDFs to slides, after internal meetings flagged "rapid escalation in AI token spend." The shift to consumption-based pricing, combined with employees "tokenmaxxing" to prove their AI uptake, has pushed spend to an inflection point where C-level execs are openly questioning the return. Accenture's agentic AI lead says this isn't niche, every AI-using enterprise will hit it, which is why the consultancy now plans to sell clients the service of untangling their own token bills.

title: Supreme Court agrees to hear Apple appeal over Epic Games ruling
blurb: The Supreme Court agreed to hear Apple's appeal over the contempt ruling in its Epic Games fight, reviewing whether Apple can charge commissions on purchases made through external payment links. The narrow question, whether Apple willfully defied the 2021 order to allow outside payment options, decides how much the App Store's off-platform revenue is actually protected. For any founder whose app economics depend on Apple's cut, the outcome sets whether the external-payment escape hatch stays open or Apple gets to keep taxing purchases that leave its In-App Purchase system.

title: Microsoft set for new round of job cuts next week, spanning Xbox, sales and consulting
blurb: Microsoft is cutting thousands of jobs next week across Xbox, sales, and consulting, less than 2.5% of its 220,000 staff, even as it spends more than $100B this fiscal year building AI and cloud infrastructure. The timing tracks its usual post-June-30 restructuring, but the backdrop is new. Shares are down 19% in a month, near a 52-week low, as Wall Street doubts the AI payoff. U.S. tech has announced 123,653 cuts in 2026, up 66% year over year, and AI is now the most-cited reason for layoffs, linked to 87,714 so far this year.

title: SumUp launches consumer account paying users to shop small
blurb: SumUp launched a personal bank account paying 5% cashback on spending inside its own merchant network, plus no transaction fees for merchants who take payments from those consumers. The move turns SumUp's small-business base into a two-sided flywheel, using loyalty rewards rather than features to compete against Revolut, Monzo, and N26 in a crowded neobank market. It follows Wise and Klarna in bolting consumer banking onto a payments business, betting that steering shoppers toward local merchants is a defensible edge the pure neobanks can't easily copy.

title: SpaceX has an AI device prototype, and it sure sounds phone-ish
blurb: SpaceX has shown investors a prototype "handset-like" AI device, sleeker than an iPhone and reportedly built on a proprietary OS with xAI integration, though Musk calls the reporting "utterly false." The play is platform ownership: SpaceX has Tesla-grade manufacturing, chip access, and Starlink Mobile ambitions that could turn a device into a full stack outside Google's and Apple's walls. But the AI-hardware graveyard already holds Humane and Rabbit, and OpenAI's Jony Ive project keeps slipping, so wanting to sell an AI device still isn't the same as consumers wanting to buy one.

title: Finance App Network Maker Plaid Is Said to Consider US IPO
blurb: Plaid is weighing a US IPO, according to people familiar with the plans, a move that would put the fintech infrastructure layer that connects apps to bank accounts onto public markets. Plaid was last valued around $13.4B in a 2021 round and later marked down before recovering, so a listing would test what investors now pay for the plumbing beneath consumer fintech. For founders building on that plumbing, a public Plaid means more disclosure on pricing, margins, and how much of the account-connection market one vendor actually holds.

title: Venice Raises $65M Series A at $1B Valuation Led by Dragonfly
blurb: Venice, Erik Voorhees's privacy-first AI platform, raised a $65M Series A at a $1B valuation led by Dragonfly, its first outside capital since launch. The company hit profitability in Q1 and, by its own account, is now the largest firm at the intersection of AI and crypto by revenue, yet it sold equity rather than tap a treasury holding 30M of the roughly 80M VVV tokens in circulation. Most of the money goes toward owning datacenters instead of renting compute, a bet that controlling capacity ahead of a resource squeeze widens margins and funds larger token burns.

title: Meta's plan to launch a cloud business eases the biggest overhang on the stock
blurb: Meta is preparing to launch a cloud infrastructure business that sells its excess AI compute and models to outside customers, and the stock jumped more than 9% on the news. The move finally answers the question hanging over Meta's spending, with capex set to nearly double to $135B this year against a narrow, ad-dependent revenue base. Until now Meta was the only hyperscaler-scale spender without a cloud business to monetize the buildout, unlike Microsoft, Google, and Amazon, whose comparable capex feeds paying enterprise customers.

title: How Cursor deploys AI inside the enterprise
blurb: Forward deployed engineering has become one of the most prominent roles in enterprise AI, sitting between software engineering, product, and customer implementation. At Cursor, VP Pauline Brunet runs a team that goes on-site, works inside a customer's own systems and tools, and stands up highly configurable agent deployments across the entire software development lifecycle. The goal is an AI software factory for transformation and CTO organizations in financial services, telecom, and semiconductors, moving agent adoption past individual enthusiasts to something a whole company runs on.

title: Morgan Stanley cut its riskiest reconciliation job in half — by making its agents less autonomous
blurb: Morgan Stanley cut its riskiest P&L reconciliation job in half by making its agents less autonomous, not more. Controllers stay fully in the loop reviewing every mismatch, and their decisions get turned into repeatable rules the system applies on its own, dropping a single book from six hours to two or three and saving about 1,500 hours a week across 100 controllers. The lesson for founders deploying agents in accuracy-critical work is that the win comes from capturing human judgment as durable logic, not from removing the human.

title: AWS puts $1 billion into new AI unit to embed engineers with customers, joining growing wave
blurb: AWS is investing $1B in a Forward Deployed Engineering unit that embeds pods of five or six engineers, working alongside AI agents, inside customer facilities to ship AI systems in weeks. It's the first hyperscaler to formalize the FDE model that Palantir coined, following OpenAI and Anthropic, both of which stood up their own FDE ventures with banks and PE firms earlier this year. The pattern signals where AI value is actually captured: not in the model, but in the messy on-site work of getting customers to deploy it, and the labs and clouds are racing to own that layer.

title: Bending Spoons, Owner of AOL and Other Old Internet Brands, Is Going Public
blurb: Bending Spoons, the Italian company that buys aging internet brands like AOL, Vimeo, and Eventbrite, is going public this week at a potential $19 billion valuation. Its model is the opposite of the growth-at-all-costs playbook: acquire tired properties, strip costs, raise prices, and run them for cash rather than expansion. The IPO tests whether public markets will reward a rollup that treats declining software as an annuity, at a moment when most attention and capital are flowing the other way, toward AI-native growth.

title: Ashton Kutcher leaving Sound Ventures to launch new VC firm with Morgan Beller
blurb: Ashton Kutcher is leaving Sound Ventures, the firm he co-founded 11 years ago and rode into early positions in OpenAI, Anthropic, and World Labs, to start a new early-stage fund with Morgan Beller, a former NFX and a16z partner who co-led Meta's Libra. The split is really a bet on a different layer of the AI stack, with the new firm targeting infrastructure, energy, and deep tech rather than the category-leading labs. The signal for founders: the smart money is moving down the stack, toward the hard-science plumbing that powers the models everyone else is funding.

title: Google Must Pay Nearly $2 Billion to Klarna in Antitrust Case
blurb: A Swedish court ordered Google to pay Klarna nearly $2 billion, ruling that it favored its own price-comparison service over Klarna's in search results. The decision follows the EU's earlier Google Shopping case and shows that self-preferencing liability now travels down to individual competitors seeking damages, not just regulators levying fines. For any founder building on top of a platform that also runs its own competing product, the ruling is a rare data point that the courts will put a price on being buried.

title: ByteDance construye en Brasil su mayor complejo de centros de datos fuera de China
blurb: ByteDance is building its largest data center complex outside China in Brazil, a $39B project that lands as the biggest win yet in Lula's campaign to pull Chinese capital into the country. The scale signals that frontier compute buildout is going where the power, land, and political incentives are, not just where the existing cloud giants already sit. For founders, it is a reminder that the infrastructure layer under AI is globalizing fast, and Latin America is now a place where that capacity gets built rather than only consumed.

title: How the AI bubble could pop and take down the global economy, according to the BIS
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements warned that an AI bubble could burst and drag the broader economy down with it, citing the concentration of market gains and capex in a handful of tech firms. The central bankers' bank flagged that AI-driven equity valuations and surging data-center spending now sit at the center of global growth, so a correction would not stay contained to tech. For founders, the read is that a downturn in AI sentiment would tighten capital and demand well beyond the labs, even for companies with no direct AI exposure.

title: The DeepMind trio who built a poker AI are now making money for quant hedge funds
blurb: Three ex-DeepMind researchers who built a poker-beating AI have pointed the same reinforcement learning at markets, and their Prague-based lab EquiLibre is now valued at $500M after a Creandum-led Series A, the largest single check the firm has ever written. Trading with quant firm Tower Research across the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, EquiLibre's agents move billions in daily volume and claim zero negative months since inception. The wedge is that markets score themselves in dollars, making them ideal for self-learning agents, and DeepMind alumni chasing frontier problems have become one of the hottest bets in venture.

title: AOL Owner Bending Spoons Jumps 40% After $1.68 Billion IPO
blurb: Bending Spoons closed 40% above its IPO price after the Milan-based app owner and its backers raised $1.68 billion, one of Europe's largest tech listings in years. The company runs a buy-and-optimize playbook, acquiring aging consumer software like AOL, Evernote, and WeTransfer, then cutting costs and raising prices rather than chasing growth. The strong debut signals public-market appetite for profitable, cash-generative software over the growth-at-all-costs model, and validates roll-up strategies that treat mature apps as durable cash engines rather than turnaround projects.

title: Meta loses bid to dismiss US states' claims that FB, Instagram addict children
blurb: A federal judge ruled that Meta must face claims from more than 30 US states alleging Facebook and Instagram were designed to hook children, letting the case proceed toward trial. Discovery will now pull internal research on engagement mechanics into the open, the same evidence that has already forced settlements elsewhere. For any founder building consumer products around retention and habit loops, the standard being tested here is whether "engaging by design" becomes a legal liability, not just a growth metric.

title: Blue Origin pivots to redesigned launchpad after explosion in push to fly by end of 2026
blurb: Blue Origin is scrapping the launchpad where its New Glenn rocket exploded during a May hot-fire test, moving to a horizontal/vertical hybrid configuration to return to flight before the end of 2026. The rebuild matters beyond Blue Origin, because NASA's Artemis moon program, Amazon's Leo satellites, and AST SpaceMobile all depend on New Glenn for launch capacity while SpaceX extends its reusable-rocket lead. NASA says New Glenn remains "plan A" for its cargo lander and it has time into 2027 before needing a backup provider.

title: Venice AI, which offers access to 200+ AI models while allowing users to retain their privacy, raised a $65M Series A led by Dragonfly at a $1B valuation (Ram Iyer/TechCrunch)
blurb: Venice AI raised a $65M Series A led by Dragonfly at a $1B valuation, offering access to 200+ models without logging or retaining user data. The bet is that privacy becomes a paid feature as the frontier labs monetize the opposite. As enterprises and regulated buyers get warier about where their prompts go, a router that promises not to store anything carries real pricing power, and a billion-dollar mark says investors expect that demand to compound.

title: Chamath Palihapitiya Takes CEO Role at 8090 Labs After $135M Raise
blurb: Chamath Palihapitiya raised a $135M Series A for 8090 Labs, the enterprise AI coding startup he founded, and is taking the CEO seat himself. It is his first full-time operating role since he left Facebook, and the round leans heavily on his own network, led by Salesforce Ventures with All-In co-hosts David Friedberg and Jason Calacanis, David Sacks' Craft Ventures, and angels Nikesh Arora and Adam D'Angelo. The bet is enterprise-first: 8090's Software Factory targets corporate engineering teams that need audit trails and controls, not individual developers shipping prototypes.

title: Private AI: Venice.ai, led by crypto vet Erik Voorhees and Seattle’s Jesse Proudman, raises $65M
blurb: Venice.ai raised $65M in its first outside round at a $1B valuation, betting that privacy becomes the wedge as people hand AI their medical, legal, and relationship questions. Founded by ShapeShift's Erik Voorhees and Seattle's Jesse Proudman, the company logs no prompts, keeps conversations on-device, and strips out most content filters, so there's no central trove to breach or subpoena. The bet is that a data breach or subpoena at OpenAI or Anthropic eventually makes "we store nothing" a real selling point, though fewer guardrails also invites the misuse the incumbents built filters to prevent.

title: HubSpot’s Warmly deal points to the next generation of CRM
blurb: HubSpot acquired Warmly, and the deal signals CRMs are turning from customer databases into systems that spot buying intent and act on it. Warmly identifies more than half of anonymous website visitors at the individual level, handing sales teams names instead of just company logos, and it brings two AI agents that turn buying signals into meetings and engage likely buyers before they visit. It fits a year of HubSpot moves toward AI-run revenue work, including Breeze agents and outcome-based pricing, and it narrows the gap between an anonymous visit and a qualified conversation.

title: Nothing and Hopin backer Tapestry VC raises $80M Fund III to back Europe’s repeat founder wave
blurb: Salesforce's $3.6B acquisition of Fin, the AI agent formerly known as Intercom, just handed early backer Tapestry VC its clearest win and the timing to raise an $80M Fund III, nearly three times its last. Tapestry's thesis is the repeat founder who drags a traditional SaaS business into the AI era, and the fund is backed by British Business Bank, Railpen, Molten Ventures, and OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar. With Fund III it moves upmarket, writing $1M to $4M checks and taking more lead roles as Europe's serial founders start their next companies.

title: BENDING SPOONS réussit son IPO : Wall Street valide le modèle du « serial consolidator » logiciel
blurb: Bending Spoons raised $1.68B in its Nasdaq IPO at an $18.4B valuation, priced above range, and Wall Street bought the model more than the assets. Investors aren't paying for Evernote, Vimeo, WeTransfer, or AOL. They're paying for a machine that acquires, restructures, and turns profitable software properties around at scale, now proven with Q1 2026 revenue of $601M, a return to profit, and 500M active users. The SaaS slowdown feeds it: normalized valuations and pricier capital have created a deep pool of profitable, under-exploited software to consolidate.

title: Zuckerberg Urges Meta to Explore Working With Polymarket and Kalshi
blurb: Meta is now eyeing prediction markets, with Zuckerberg pushing the company to explore partnerships with Polymarket and Kalshi. The move would plug real-money event contracts into platforms with billions of users, turning the feed into a place where people bet on outcomes rather than just argue about them. For founders in the space, the signal is that prediction markets are graduating from crypto-native niche to mainstream distribution, and the labs and platforms with the audience are the ones deciding who gets the volume.

title: UK regulator wants Apple and Google to let devs steer clear of app store fees
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority is moving to force Apple and Google to let developers point users to cheaper payment options outside the app stores, sidestepping the standard 15% to 30% cut. It follows the same anti-steering logic already reshaping the EU under the DMA and the US after the Epic ruling, so the walled-garden tax is now eroding in three major markets at once. For any founder whose margins are set by platform fees, routing checkout off-platform is shifting from a compliance risk to a defensible line item.

title: B2B sales workspace startup Aligned raised a $60M Series B led by PeakSpan Capital, taking its total funding to $73.8M, and says it has 1,000+ customers (Chris Metinko/Axios)
blurb: Aligned raised a $60M Series B led by PeakSpan Capital, pushing total funding to $73.8M with more than 1,000 customers already on its B2B sales workspace. The round is a bet that the deal room, where buyers and sellers share documents, pricing, and next steps in one place, becomes the system of record for complex sales. As buying committees grow and cycles stretch, the money is moving toward tools that organize the whole purchase rather than just the seller's pipeline.

title: Uber-Backed Lime Raises $174 Million in Public Debut
blurb: Lime raised $174M in its Nasdaq debut, pricing at the midpoint of its range and adding another mark to a reopening IPO window. CEO Wayne Ting framed the listing as a way to court new investors and fund possible M&A, though he said "the bar is high" on deals. For founders eyeing an exit, the signal is that public markets are taking on mid-cap venture-backed names again, even at cautious pricing rather than a euphoric pop.

title: Lime begins life as a public company after years of uncertainty
blurb: Lime went public on Wednesday, raising $167M in an IPO that pegged the nine-year-old scooter company at around $1.66B, roughly where Bird landed before it collapsed. The Uber-backed company only filed after stringing together three years of positive free cash flow, having told the market it needed the proceeds to cover about $1B in liabilities, more than half due by year-end. Micromobility has been a brutal business, and Lime's path shows that surviving the hype cycles came down to reaching self-sustaining economics before the market would take it seriously.

title: Employers who laid off workers citing AI are already starting to regret it
blurb: Companies that cut staff on the promise that AI could "do it all" are now rehiring the people they let go. Ford is re-employing hundreds of experienced engineers to fix quality issues automation missed, CBA reversed 40-plus service-role cuts after its voice bot buckled and drove call volume up, and IBM is tripling U.S. entry-level hiring after its HR AI handled 94% of requests but stalled on the 6% that needed judgment. Orgvue found 39% of leaders made AI-driven layoffs, and 55% of them now say the call was wrong.

title: How an AI Bust Could Ripple Through The Global Economy
blurb: The AI boom now anchors so much of the economy that a bust wouldn't stay contained to tech. Data-center buildouts are propping up capital spending and GDP growth, while chipmakers are booking bumper profits and South Korea is planning new fabs on the assumption demand keeps climbing. If model progress stalls or returns disappoint, the pullback would hit memory producers, equipment suppliers, and the broader markets that have priced in permanent expansion.

title: The case for (and against) founding solo
blurb: Solo founders made up 63% of the C corps formed through Stripe Atlas in Q2 2026, an all-time high, and the essay argues this isn't just weekend vibe-coding noise. The 2025 cohort crossed $1M in revenue within a year at roughly three times the 2019 rate, with AI-assisted solo founders clearing the top income bands at double the old share. The author's long-held contention is that hiring a cofounder is the most expensive decision in a decade-long startup, and the data is finally catching up to that view.

title: Beck: Iridium acquisition the “logical next step” for Rocket Lab
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to buy Iridium for $8B, or $54 per share in cash and stock, moving from launch and satellite manufacturing into recurring space services. CEO Peter Beck called it the "logical next step," a way to skip the decade it takes to build a constellation and reach firm recurring revenue, and Iridium's positive cash flow and global L-band spectrum let the company enter applications without fighting regulators over its own allocation. The bet is that owning production, launch, and services in one vertically integrated company opens business models a pure-launch provider could never chase. The deal is expected to close in mid-2027.

title: EquiLibre secures Series A at €438 million valutation to scale AI trading agents handling billions daily
blurb: EquiLibre, a Prague-based lab founded by DeepMind and DeepStack researchers, raised a Series A at a valuation above €438 million ($500 million), led by Creandum in its largest check to date. The team applies reinforcement learning to live trading, and its agents now move billions of dollars daily and claim to have never posted a negative month. Most of the capital goes to compute, building one of the largest clusters in the region, a reminder that the frontier AI story in Europe is increasingly about who can afford to scale infrastructure, not just talent.

title: Nothing-backer Tapestry VC raises $80M fund, opens London office
blurb: Tapestry VC, an early backer of Nothing, closed an $80M Fund III, nearly triple its $30M predecessor, to lead seed rounds on both sides of the Atlantic. The check sizes track the market's move: half-million-dollar checks in Fund I, then $1M, now up to $3M leading and co-leading seeds. Founder Patrick Murphy is relocating from San Francisco to London to chase what he calls a European repeat-founder super cycle, pointing to 23,000 repeat-founder startups now employing over two million people. The British Business Bank anchored the fund with $40M.

title: Blackstone Offloads Stakes in Trio of Virginia Data Centers
blurb: Blackstone is selling its stakes in three Northern Virginia data centers for $3.5B, cashing out of a bet it made less than three years ago. The quick flip signals that some of the smartest capital in the AI infrastructure boom is now taking profits rather than doubling down. For founders reading the compute cycle, it's a marker that the easy appreciation phase of the data center land grab may be closing, even as the buildout continues.

title: Six Robotics lands €12 million to advance unmanned systems software with Norwegian defence partners
blurb: Six Robotics raised €12 million in seed funding led by DTCP's new €500 million defence fund to build software-defined autonomy and swarming for unmanned systems, working closely with Norway's defence research establishment. The round lands inside a fast-filling European DefenceTech category, where roughly €204.5 million in disclosed 2026 rounds has gone to drone autonomy and unmanned-systems infrastructure. The bet across all of them is the same: Europe wants to own the software that decides what its forces can do rather than depend on foreign platforms, and the capital is now chasing the autonomy layer specifically.

title: Palantir's Karp bashes OpenAI, Anthropic token model: 'Something has gone completely wrong'
blurb: Palantir CEO Alex Karp called the token-based pricing behind OpenAI and Anthropic broken, telling CNBC that "something has gone completely wrong" as model costs climb and enterprises chase ROI over tokenmaxxing. The pushback is pointing buyers toward open-weight models that do similar work for a fraction of the price, and toward training their own proprietary tools instead of renting frontier ones. Karp's bet, backed by an expanded Nvidia partnership and a 9-point AI-sovereignty manifesto, is that technical customers want to own their compute, models, and data rather than hand the means of production to a lab.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 model on Claude and APIs
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, available now across all Claude plans, Claude Code, and the Claude API at $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens until a scheduled increase after August 31, 2026. The model improves on Sonnet 4.6 in reasoning, tool use, and agentic coding, running multi-step tasks across browsers and terminals while closing the benchmark gap with the pricier Opus 4.8.

title: Claude Sonnet 5
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, its most agentic Sonnet model yet, with performance close to Opus 4.8 on reasoning, tool use, and coding while covering a wider cost-performance range through adjustable effort levels. It ships as the default on Free and Pro plans and in Claude Code, with introductory API pricing of $2 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens through August 31, 2026.

title: I ported Kubernetes to the browser
blurb: A developer ported Kubernetes to TypeScript as webernetes, a partial reimplementation that runs an entire cluster in the browser with pod lifecycles, cluster DNS and networking, container garbage collection, IP allocation, and Deployment and ReplicaSet tracking. Rather than compiling Kubernetes to WebAssembly, which would ship megabytes and fails on unavailable system APIs, the project reimplements the control plane in ~140KiB gzipped and defines images through a TypeScript API backed by an in-browser registry.

title: Anthropic rolls out Sonnet 5 with near-Opus 4.8 performance at a lower price
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, bringing Opus-class agentic features like multi-step planning and browser and terminal tool use to the cheaper Sonnet tier for the first time. The model closes much of the gap with Opus 4.8 on coding, research, and automation, and is notably better at verifying its own output without being prompted, making it a stronger cost-performance option for long multi-step tasks.

title: Start building with Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash
blurb: Google DeepMind released Nano Banana 2 Lite (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-image), its fastest image model at $0.034 per 1K-resolution image and 4-second text-to-image latency, alongside Gemini Omni Flash for video generation and conversational editing. Both are now available in Google AI Studio, the Gemini API, and the Enterprise Agent Platform, with Nano Banana 2 Lite positioned as a drop-in replacement for the original gemini-2.5-flash-image.

title: GuardFall Exposes Open-Source AI Coding Agents to Decades-Old Shell Injection Risks
blurb: Adversa AI's GuardFall research shows that ten of eleven popular open-source coding agents can be tricked into running blocked commands, because their blocklists match commands as plain text while bash rewrites that text before execution, so `r''m` sails past a filter watching for `rm`. The authors frame this as a class of problems rather than a patchable bug, since the gap opens whenever an agent auto-executes shell commands with full account access against untrusted repos or packages.

title: 282 iOS AI Apps Leak API Keys and Open AI Proxy Access in Network Traffic Study
blurb: Wake Forest researchers tested 444 iOS AI chatbot apps and found 282 exposed paid model access in plaintext network traffic, via embedded API keys, replayable tokens, or backends that accept unauthenticated requests. The takeaway for developers is that client-side secrets are not secret, and the fix is routing every model call through an authenticated backend proxy, because three months after disclosure only 28% had patched.

title: Malicious PyPI packages give hackers control of Telegram bot servers
blurb: Malicious PyPI packages disguised as forks of the popular but abandoned Pyrogram Telegram framework carry a hidden secret.py backdoor that runs attacker-supplied Python or shell commands on infected bot servers. Checkmarx tied at least eight packages, totaling over 25,000 downloads since November 2025, to the campaign, which grants full access to the bot's session, environment variables, and anything the host application can read.

title: Claude Sonnet 5 boosts coding, reasoning, and tool use
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5 on June 30, a drop-in upgrade over Sonnet 4.6 that improves coding, reasoning, and agentic tool use while approaching Opus 4.8 performance at lower cost. It ships with adaptive thinking on by default, an updated tokenizer, and 400 errors for manual extended thinking and non-default sampling, with introductory API pricing of $2 per million input and $10 per million output tokens through August 31.

title: What's new in Git 2.55.0?
blurb: Git 2.55.0 adds a `git history fixup <commit-id>` subcommand that stages and amends changes into an existing commit in one step, automatically rebasing any stacked branches that contain it instead of requiring an interactive autosquash rebase. The release also extends the built-in fsmonitor daemon to Linux via inotify, speeding up `git status` in large monorepos without a third-party tool like Watchman.

title: Core dump epidemiology: fixing an 18-year-old bug
blurb: OpenAI traced a batch of "impossible" C++ crashes in its Rockset data infrastructure, where functions returned to bogus addresses or ran with a %rsp misaligned by 8 bytes, by building a population-level dataset of every crash and reasoning about it like an epidemiologist. The symptoms turned out to be two unrelated causes discovered at once: silent CPU corruption on one Azure host, and an 18-year-old race condition in GNU libunwind.

title: Microsoft Warns Poisoned MCP Tool Descriptions Can Make AI Agents Leak Data
blurb: Microsoft Incident Response and Defender researchers show that a poisoned MCP tool description can turn an AI agent into a data-exfiltration path, since the agent treats the description's hidden instructions as legitimate and every step looks routine enough to trip no alarm. The core weakness is that MCP tools ship with plain-text descriptions the agent trusts, and MCP picks up description changes on the fly, so a third-party tool approved once can go malicious without re-review.

title: From monolith to Lakebase to LTAP: rethinking the database from storage up
blurb: Databricks engineer Reynold Xin breaks down the Lakebase architecture, which decomposes a traditional monolithic Postgres into independent, externalized services by separating the write-ahead log from the data files. The post extends this to LTAP, running transactions and analytics on a single copy of data in real time, avoiding the latency and cost of CDC or mirroring pipelines.

title: Zluda 6 release (run unmodified CUDA applications on non-Nvidia GPUs)
blurb: ZLUDA 6 is out, letting unmodified CUDA applications run on non-Nvidia GPUs, with new support for 32-bit PhysX and Blender texture rendering on AMD hardware. The release folds in improved Windows support and better ML workloads, though PhysX remains incomplete, with glitchy fluid simulations and a rough method for loading ZLUDA into Steam games.

title: Box3D, an open source 3D physics engine
blurb: Erin Catto released Box3D, an open source 3D physics engine that forks Box2D and adds triangle mesh collision, height-field collision, and baked compound collision on top of the original's C17 core. It keeps Box2D's architecture, including the sub-stepping solver, continuous collision, wide SIMD contact solving, cross-platform determinism, and large-world support using doubles for position.

title: Fake Bug Report Hijacks AI Coding Agents at Scale
blurb: Tenet Security's "agentjacking" research shows attackers can hijack AI coding agents like Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex by planting a single fake error report in a public bug tracker such as Sentry, which the agent retrieves, trusts, and executes with the developer's own access. Because every step is authorized, IAM, EDR, and network controls flag nothing, leaving cloud credentials, GitHub tokens, and CI/CD secrets exposed.

title: MCP Server Architecture Patterns for LLM-Integrated Applications
blurb: This industry experience paper catalogues five recurring MCP server patterns observed across 15 production and public servers: Resource Gateway, Tool Orchestrator, Stateful Session Server, Proxy Aggregator, and Domain-Specific Adapter, each documented with four anti-patterns and cross-cutting auth, versioning, and observability concerns. A tool-count study found selection accuracy drops below 90% at 10 to 15 tools per context for Claude Haiku 4.5 and 20 to 30 for Sonnet 4.

title: Pollen tried to remove my article and Google is assisting with it
blurb: The Pragmatic Engineer's Gergely Orosz reports that Google delisted his 2022 article on events startup Pollen's $800M collapse after receiving a bogus copyright claim, despite him being the sole copyright holder. The case shows how anyone can file a fraudulent DMCA notice to scrub critical coverage from search results, with Google honoring the takedown by default and leaving the author to appeal.

title: NixOS 26.05
blurb: NixOS 26.05 "Yarara" ships from 2842 contributors and 59703 commits, updating Nixpkgs with 20442 new packages and bumping GNOME to 50 and GCC to 15. Stage 1 initrd now defaults to systemd with the scripted implementation deprecated for removal in 26.11, and this is the final release to support x86_64-darwin following Apple's platform deprecation.

title: Critical Cursor Flaws Could Let Prompt Injection Escape Sandbox and Run Commands
blurb: Cato AI Labs disclosed DuneSlide, two zero-click prompt-injection flaws in Cursor (CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549, both CVSS 9.8) that let instructions hidden in an MCP service or web-search result escape the agent's terminal sandbox and run arbitrary commands. Both bugs trick the agent into writing a file it shouldn't to disable the sandbox, are patched in Cursor 3.0, and affect every prior version.

title: OTel and mesh-derived metrics: A 2026 reference
blurb: This reference shows how Linkerd's sidecar proxy emits golden metrics for every inbound and outbound request the moment a workload is meshed, with no instrumentation, SDK calls, or image rebuilds, giving you the east-west traffic that application-layer OpenTelemetry never sees. It walks through where mesh metrics overlap with OTel and where they don't, then wires the proxy's Prometheus endpoint into an existing OTel Collector pipeline so both layers land in the same backend.

title: ZCode: Claude Code from the Makers of GLM
blurb: ZCode is an agentic coding CLI from Z.ai, the team behind the GLM models, positioning itself as a Claude Code alternative tuned for its own GLM-5.2 backend. It adds Goal-based planning for multi-step tasks that it plans, executes, and verifies in a loop, plus remote invocation through WeChat, Feishu, or Telegram, and ships install packages across the same 20+ tool integrations Claude Code targets.

title: ChocoPoc malware delivered via trojanized exploits on GitHub
blurb: Multiple weaponized proof-of-concept exploits on GitHub were found delivering ChocoPoC, a Python-based RAT targeting security researchers via trojanized dependencies rather than malware embedded in the exploit code itself. The campaign chains PyPI packages 'frint' and 'skytext' to pull the payload from a Mapbox dataset, and Sekoia counted 2,400 downloads across at least seven repos posing as PoCs for trending CVEs.

title: A native graphical shell for SSH
blurb: Outer Shell is an open-source graphical shell for SSH, where each app is a minimal HTTP server exposed over a Unix domain socket rather than a localhost port, with encryption handled at the SSH layer instead of in the app. The shell provides a home screen of apps plus an API for inter-app lookups, so a text-editor app can register itself and open files double-clicked elsewhere.

title: Highlights from Git 2.55
blurb: Git 2.55 shipped with contributions from over 100 developers, headlined by `git repack --write-midx=incremental`, which writes incremental multi-pack index chains directly instead of rewriting a single MIDX over every pack. The append-only mode lets large repositories index newly created packs by adding a layer to the chain, avoiding the expensive full-file rewrite that single-MIDX maintenance forces on each small update.

title: Cursor releases iPhone and iPad app following recent acquisition by SpaceX
blurb: Cursor released its first iOS app, bringing its agentic coding tools to iPhone and iPad following SpaceX's acquisition of the company earlier this month. The app launches cloud coding agents from anywhere, supports voice conversations, diff inspection, and PR merges, with Remote Control handoffs between local and cloud agents planned next and 75% off Composer 2.5 runs through July 5.

title: Announcing the Monetization Gateway: charge for any resource behind Cloudflare via x402
blurb: Cloudflare announced the Monetization Gateway, an engine that lets customers charge per request for any asset behind Cloudflare, including web pages, datasets, APIs, and MCP tools. It offers a single control plane for payment policies and access controls, settles payments in stablecoins over the x402 protocol, and verifies and enforces payments at the edge to shield origins from high payment volumes.

title: Micro-Agent: Beat Frontier Models with Collaboration Inside Model API
blurb: vLLM Semantic Router turns a single OpenAI-compatible model call into a bounded collaboration inside the serving layer, using a "looper" runtime that fans out to worker models, collects a quorum, resolves disagreement, and synthesizes one normal response. The goal is to beat frontier models without touching weights or making each app build a bespoke agent graph, exposing multi-model orchestration as an open serving primitive behind a stable model identity.

title: WATaBoy: JIT-Ing Game Boy Instructions to WASM Beats a Native Interpreter
blurb: A developer building a Game Boy emulator for their final-year project generated WebAssembly bytecode from Game Boy instructions, exploiting the JIT compilation that JavaScriptCore applies to hot Wasm in browsers, the one path around Apple's iOS JIT ban. The JIT-to-Wasm approach stays cycle-accurate by predicting interrupts and falling back to an interpreter, and it beats a natively running interpreter on performance.

title: Rust introduces new Range types
blurb: Rust 1.96.0 stabilizes new `core::range` types (Range, RangeFrom, RangeInclusive) that implement `IntoIterator` instead of `Iterator`, letting them also be `Copy` and fixing the long-standing footgun of implementing both traits on one type. The release also adds `assert_matches!` and `debug_assert_matches!` macros, and makes undefined symbols a hard linker error on WebAssembly targets instead of silently converting them to "env" imports.

title: Inside the Advisory Database and what happens when vulnerability volume breaks records
blurb: The GitHub Advisory Database published 1,560 reviewed advisories in May 2026, five times its typical output and still not enough as private vulnerability reports climbed from ~550 to over 3,000 per week. GitHub argues review times will stay longer until submitters carry more of the load by filing complete data and requesting CVEs only when they intend to publish.

title: Cursor releases its iOS app for vibe coding on the go
blurb: Cursor released a native iOS app in public beta for paid plans that turns the phone into a control surface for its coding agents, letting developers launch agents, select frontier models, inspect diffs, leave follow-up instructions, and merge pull requests from an iPhone. It supports Remote Control for agents running on a local machine and runs cloud agents in isolated VMs, so a session started at the desk can continue without keeping a laptop open.

title: Deno update streamlines creation of desktop apps
blurb: Deno Land released Deno 2.9 with an experimental deno desktop feature that compiles a script or web framework project into a single self-contained binary, running the UI in a webview and the logic in Deno via the same machinery as deno compile. The release also cuts hello-world cold start from 34ms to 17ms, holds resident memory flat near 62MB under load, and advances Node.js compatibility to version 26.

title: What can you confidently guarantee about your software?
blurb: The author argues that most engineers can name what their software is supposed to do but rarely state what it actually guarantees under failure, load, or malformed input. The piece pushes teams to write down explicit, testable invariants rather than relying on informal confidence, since unstated assumptions are where production incidents originate.

title: Memory Safe Context Switching
blurb: Fil-C implements longjmp/setjmp and the ucontext coroutine APIs (getcontext, setcontext, makecontext, swapcontext) so that no misuse can corrupt the stack or escape its capability model. Restoring a returned-from or freed stack, swapping into the currently executing context, or running on a dangling stack all become clean panics instead of the unprintable, exploitable crashes those same bugs produce in ordinary C.

title: Linux for the Sega MegaDrive
blurb: Linux for the Sega MegaDrive runs a real m68k kernel via a Mega EverDrive cartridge, with a QEMU fork included for testing without hardware.

title: ScarfBench: Benchmarking AI Agents for Enterprise Java Framework Migration
blurb: IBM Research released ScarfBench, an open benchmark that evaluates AI coding agents on enterprise Java framework migrations across Spring, Jakarta EE, and Quarkus. Rather than diffing generated code against reference implementations, it checks whether migrated apps actually build, deploy, and pass behavioral validation, and frontier agents struggle, with success rates varying widely across framework pairs and whole-application migrations remaining the hardest case.

title: Nika: Open-source code analysis tool
blurb: PhonePe released Nika, an open-source tool that runs cross-file taint analysis on Java microservices, tracing attacker-controlled input across controllers, service layers, and data objects to determine whether it reaches a security-sensitive sink like a SQL query, file operation, or reflection call. It covers eleven vulnerability categories, outputs HTML or JSON reports showing the vulnerable path, and includes an optional off-by-default AI review step to cut false positives.

title: What’s !important #14: Gap Decorations, random(), <select> field sizing, and More
blurb: What’s !important #14 rounds up recent CSS developments, including Temani Afif's guide to styling gap decorations in flex, grid, and multi-column layouts, Polypane's experiments with the Safari-only random() function, and Una Kravets' walkthrough of modern theming with @property, light-dark(), contrast-color(), and @container style(). It also covers Manuel Matuzović's use of field-sizing: content to size a <select> to its selected option, now Baseline since Firefox 152.

title: Claude Sonnet 5 on GitLab: More reliable, more efficient
blurb: Claude Sonnet 5 is now available on GitLab's Duo Agent Platform across all tiers and deployment models, becoming the first model to complete every task in GitLab's benchmark suite versus 93.8% for Sonnet 4.6. GitLab reports 8.8% more issues resolved, which for teams running Duo agents in production means multi-file refactors and test generation return reviewable output instead of stalling partway through a run.

title: AdaTrans: Automated C to Rust Transformation via Error-Adaptive Repair
blurb: AdaTrans is a framework for automated C-to-Rust translation that maps compiler errors to targeted repairs through a strategy-driven RAG mechanism, an error-stratified transformation strategy, and a multi-stage validation pipeline that checks both compilation and functional equivalence. On 104 algorithmic problems it hits a 95.51% mean compilation pass rate and an 81.09% solve rate while keeping the unsafe-file rate at 1.19%.

title: MOA: A Profiling-Guided LLM Framework for Memory-Optimization Automation at Codebase Scale
blurb: This paper presents MOA, an LLM framework that automates memory-optimization across production codebases using three agents: an Analyzer that mines anti-patterns from profiling data, a Checker Generator that synthesizes static analyzers, and a Patcher that produces semantics-preserving fixes. Evaluated on OpenHarmony's 100M+ lines of C/C++, it generated 769 patches at a 92.5% expert acceptance rate, cutting average heap usage 42.2% and binary size 10.6%.

title: Towards Knowledge Alignment in Code LLMs: Contrastive Unlearning for Evolving APIs
blurb: CURE is a contrastive unlearning method that adapts code LLMs to evolving libraries by jointly discouraging deprecated API calls and encouraging their correct replacements, rather than only suppressing outdated knowledge. On a recent deprecated-API benchmark it cut deprecated usage and improved correct replacement over two SOTA baselines while preserving general code generation performance.

title: Working With AI: A concrete example
blurb: Carson Gross walks through a hyperscript parser regression where Claude correctly found the root cause of an `as JSON` binding conflict but proposed a fix that would have papered over the real grammar ambiguity. He frames the episode as a near-miss with the Sorcerer's Apprentice problem, where leaning on AI without understanding the system leaves a maintainer unable to properly fix the issues it surfaces.

title: Herdr: Agent multiplexer that lives in your terminal
blurb: Herdr is a terminal-based multiplexer for managing multiple coding agents, using workspaces, tabs, and panes that you control with the mouse or tmux-style keybindings. Agents keep running after you detach and reattach, and unlike Electron or native GUI wrappers, you interact with each agent's real terminal process rather than a rewritten view of it.

title: Mutating the "Immutable": A Large-Scale Study of Git Tag Alterations
blurb: Researchers analyzed 328.4M repositories from Software Heritage and found 10.2M Git tag alterations across 189k repos, proving that tags routinely mutate despite being treated as immutable release anchors. A cross-check against Nixpkgs found 32 packages referencing altered tags and 7 with confirmed build errors, and the authors recommend pinning dependencies to commit hashes rather than tags.

title: FFmpeg 9.1's new AAC encoder
blurb: FFmpeg 9.1 ships a rewritten native AAC encoder that listening tests rank at or above the long-dominant Fraunhofer FDK-AAC, making the built-in encoder the default choice for high-quality AAC without external libraries. The new coder improves psychoacoustic modeling and bit allocation, closing a quality gap that pushed many transcoding pipelines to bundle libfdk_aac despite its licensing friction.

title: Optimizing a Neural Reconstruction Pipeline Using NVIDIA Nsight Developer Tools
blurb: NVIDIA walks through profiling and optimizing Omniverse NuRec, a neural reconstruction pipeline that turns multisensor AV and robotics captures into simulation-ready 3D scenes using Gaussian splatting and custom CUDA kernels. The post uses Nsight Systems and Nsight Compute to trace bottlenecks across PyTorch training loops and GPU kernels, chasing a target of reconstructing a 30-second capture in roughly 30 seconds, down from the hours it currently takes.

title: The Shifting Line Between CSS States and JavaScript Events
blurb: This post maps the CSS pseudo-classes that behave like JavaScript event listeners, including :hover, :active, :focus, :focus-visible, and :focus-within, explaining why each tracks state rather than firing on events. It then walks through the proposed event-trigger from the Animation Triggers spec, an unsupported feature that would let CSS respond to events directly and drive animations without JavaScript.

title: No more Java refills for Intel Macs after JDK 27, says Oracle
blurb: Oracle will stop shipping Java builds for Intel-based Macs after JDK 27, pushing remaining x86 Mac developers toward Apple Silicon or third-party OpenJDK distributions. The move follows Apple's own wind-down of Intel Mac support and adds JDK 27, due in late 2026, as the last LTS release that will get official Oracle binaries for the architecture.

title: Kotlin improves compile-time constants
blurb: Kotlin 2.4.0, released June 3, adds experimental compile-time constant evaluation for unsigned type operations, standard library string functions like .lowercase() and .trim(), and the .name property of enum constants. The release also marks compile-time-evaluated functions with a new IntrinsicConstEvaluation annotation, generates Java 26 bytecode, and adds experimental support for the WebAssembly Component Model.

title: Cloudflare Launches Monetization Gateway for Stablecoin Payments via x402
blurb: Cloudflare opened a waitlist for its Monetization Gateway, a tool that lets operators charge for any page, dataset, API, or MCP tool behind its network, with payments settling in stablecoins over the x402 protocol. Policies are set in one control plane and enforced at the network edge before requests reach the origin, targeting sub-cent, per-request billing for AI agents that call an endpoint once rather than hold a subscription.

title: Rehearsed Multi-Agent Live Product Demonstrations with Real-Time Voice Question Answering
blurb: Rhetor is a multi-agent system that turns a running web app and its source repository into a rehearsed live product demo with synchronized narration and real-time voice question answering. The paper's contributions include a cross-modal feature representation merging UI exploration with source-code analysis, a scripter grounded to observed UI elements via semantic locators, and a rehearsal loop that hit a 0.92 locator-firing rate on a 53-action workload.

title: Internal Combustion Engine
blurb: An interactive, draggable 3D explainer that builds up the internal combustion engine from first principles, starting with a simple crank.

title: Microsoft MCP server gives AI assistants access to MSBuild logs
blurb: Microsoft released the Microsoft Binlog MCP Server, a preview Model Context Protocol server that parses MSBuild .binlog files and exposes 15 tools so AI assistants like GitHub Copilot and Claude Code can diagnose build failures, trace property origins, and find performance bottlenecks through natural language. It replaces manual scrolling through the MSBuild Structured Log Viewer, and ships via the dotnet-msbuild plugin for Visual Studio, VS Code, and terminal-based assistants.

title: VS Code stops trusting your code by default
blurb: VS Code 1.126 makes Workspace Trust the default, opening new project folders in Restricted Mode so unfamiliar code cannot execute automatically until the developer explicitly trusts the folder. The release also surfaces total cost across an entire chat session rather than per-turn, and lets a single agent host session run multiple side-by-side Copilot chats that share the same working context.

title: Give Your AI a Memory Layer That Survives Sessions
blurb: This deep dive builds a complete local memory layer for AI agents from scratch using Mem0 and Qdrant, so an agent recalls plans, user identity, and dropped instructions across new sessions instead of resetting each time. It tests the stack against the contradiction-handling case most demos skip and works through the token math, storage costs, and access controls a production memory store needs before customer data lands in it.

title: A Multi-Dimensional, Per-Pass Empirical Study of the LLVM Optimization Pipeline
blurb: This paper decomposes LLVM's -O3 pipeline into 113 cumulative per-pass prefixes and takes 84,750 measurements of runtime, compile time, binary size, hardware counters, and energy across 30 PolyBench/C kernels. The pipeline is non-monotone (6.6-9.7% of transitions regress) and back-loaded, most gains come from a small Pareto-dominant core of passes, and the final -O3 config is Pareto-dominated on size and speedup for 29 of 30 kernels.

title: FeatX: Editing Software by Editing Features for Repository-Level Code Evolution
blurb: FeatX is a feature-oriented tool that extracts a hierarchical epic-feature structure from a repository, maps features to code, then runs a three-stage Evolution Agent to turn feature edits into code patches across a four-panel workflow. On replay over 38 real-world editing commits it beat strong LLM baselines by 42.6% relative on function-level localization F1 at $0.07 total cost, with the tool and dataset released publicly.

title: How do Execution Features Improve Statistical Fault Localization? An Empirical Study
blurb: This study tests whether adding execution features like data-flow, runtime values, and branch conditions to statistical fault localization improves on plain pass/fail line spectra. The authors extract features with EFDD across all Tests4Py subjects, train per-subject random forests, map feature importances to source lines, and fold those weights into established SFL formulas, then measure reference-patch accuracy and inspection effort with a confounder-adjusted mixed-effects model.

title: How We Made IPFS Content Publishing 10x Faster
blurb: IPFS's Kubo 0.39.0 now ships Optimistic Provide by default, a technique from ProbeLab that cuts DHT content publication latency from 13-20 seconds to under 1 second while reducing network overhead by 40%. It stores records with likely-closest peers mid-walk, terminates the walk early using a piggybacked network-size estimate, and returns control to the user once most PUT RPCs succeed, finishing the rest in the background.

title: Google copybara: moving code between repositories
blurb: Google's internal tool for transforming and syncing code between repositories, keeping one authoritative source of truth via stateless, config-driven Git workflows.

title: Do Machines Struggle Where Humans Do? LLM and Human Comprehension of Obfuscated Code
blurb: This paper tests whether LLMs fail to comprehend obfuscated code the same way humans do, evaluating several models across five obfuscation tiers and localizing failures at the atom, block, relational, and macro levels using the Block Model. Reasoning-tuned models track human difficulty patterns closely while instruction and coder-tuned models show near-zero correlation, with control-flow flattening degrading performance in proportion to state-space complexity.

title: Reading the internals of Postgres: Database cluster, databases, and tables
blurb: This post walks through PostgreSQL's logical storage model, explaining that a "database cluster" is a group of databases managed by one instance rather than multiple nodes, and that databases, tables, and other objects are all identified by OIDs starting at 16384 for user-created objects. It shows how the system catalogs like pg_database, pg_class, and pg_index are themselves regular queryable tables, with pg_database shared cluster-wide while most catalogs are created per database.

title: Pong game recompiles its own source code every frame — winning entry at IOCCC29 was generated by a custom compiler
blurb: An IOCCC29-winning Pong recompiles its own source every frame, each run printing the C for the next frame with state baked in.

title: CachyOS June 2026 Release
blurb: CachyOS June 2026 release adds a Hyprland Noctalia desktop, DNS-over-QUIC via blocky, extended Python PGO, and GCC branch-misprediction tuning.

title: Warp CEO Zach Lloyd on why software factories are the next phase of coding
blurb: Warp CEO Zach Lloyd argues that within a year most significant software projects will run on "software factories," automated systems that continuously triage, implement, review, verify, and monitor code changes rather than engineers pairing interactively with agents. Warp's answer is Oz, an orchestration platform that connects multiple models and coding harnesses across local environments and isolated cloud sandboxes while slotting into existing developer tools.

title: Why Agent Loops Fail in Production (and the Database Patterns That Fix Them)
blurb: CockroachLabs maps each common agent-loop failure in production to its root cause in the data layer, arguing that reliability is an architectural property rather than a model-quality one once agents start mutating records, triggering workflows, and approving outputs. The post pairs each failure mode with a database pattern that preserves consistent state, recovers from interrupted iterations, and keeps an auditable record, with working code for both PostgreSQL and CockroachDB.

title: What to Learn to Be a Graphics Programmer
blurb: The author lays out a two-track path into graphics programming: the CPU side of explicit APIs like DirectX12, Vulkan, or Metal plus engine plumbing, and the GPU side of lighting math, rendering techniques, and knowing what runs fast in real time. He recommends learning them separately rather than at once, starting the GPU track with a path tracer via "Ray Tracing in One Weekend" and moving on to physically based rendering.

title: 6 security settings every GitHub maintainer should enable this week
blurb: GitHub's Security Lab walks through six free repository settings maintainers can enable in under half an hour, starting with a SECURITY.md file and one-checkbox private vulnerability reporting to give researchers a confidential disclosure path. The list continues with secret scanning plus push protection, which blocks credentials from being committed rather than flagging them after they land in history.

title: How Far Do On-Prem Open LLMs Get on Text-to-SQL? A Cross-Family Size x Technique Frontier on BIRD
blurb:
This paper benchmarks on-prem open-weight LLMs on Text-to-SQL using BIRD's dev split, testing Qwen2.5-Coder, CodeLlama, and Llama-3.x under one matched protocol with McNemar significance tests. Model generation beats raw size: Qwen2.5-Coder 7B scores 39.1 vs CodeLlama's 20.9, while Llama-3.3-70B hits 49.2. Self-correction is a near-free accuracy win, but schema linking does nothing even with 96.5% gold-table recall, and self-consistency adds 0.13 points for roughly 5x the tokens.

(78 words)

title: Introducing TabFM: A zero-shot foundation model for tabular data
blurb: Google Research released TabFM, a zero-shot foundation model for tabular classification and regression that frames prediction as in-context learning, generating results on unseen tables in a single forward pass without training, hyperparameter tuning, or feature engineering. It targets the XGBoost and random-forest workflows that dominate enterprise churn and fraud prediction, and is available now on Hugging Face and GitHub.

title: From monolith to Lakebase to LTAP: rethinking the database from storage up
blurb: Databricks engineer Reynold Xin walks through Lakebase, the company's serverless Postgres, by decomposing a monolithic database into independent services: the write-ahead log that makes commits fast and the data files that make reads fast, externalized rather than pinned to one machine. The payoff is LTAP, where transactions and analytics run on a single copy of the data in real time, dropping the CDC and mirroring layers most teams bolt on to move OLTP data into the warehouse.

title: Mandol: An Agglomerative Agent Memory System for Long-Term Conversations
blurb: This paper introduces Mandol, an agent memory system that replaces the usual mix of separate vector and graph databases with a single memory-native store, fusing key-value, vector, and graph structures behind unified hybrid retrieval operators. By keeping LLMs out of the retrieval path and adding query-adaptive routing, denoising, and token-constrained context generation, it cuts cross-database I/O. The authors report best overall accuracy on LoCoMo and LongMemEval, plus a 5.4x retrieval and 4.8x insertion speedup under 10 QPS on consumer hardware.

title: Large Databases Need Small, Open-Weight Language Models
blurb: This paper argues that closed-source LM APIs are unnecessary for language-model-enhanced database operators, where token-based pricing can push a single set of experiments past $10,000. The authors show that quantized, open-weight models running locally on 16GB of VRAM match or beat proprietary APIs on accuracy at lower latency. Integrated into BlendSQL v0.1.0, the local setup cut costs 390x and latency 3.8x versus a proprietary API.

title: Designing GPU-Accelerated Query Engines with NVIDIA GQE
blurb: NVIDIA published a technical walkthrough of GQE, a reference GPU query engine that runs SQL over large datasets using cuDF and CUDA-X libraries like nvCOMP for decompression and nvSHMEM for data movement. The post details how GQE overlaps CPU-GPU transfers with computation, prunes partitions, and consumes Substrait plans exported from engines like Apache DataFusion, letting teams benchmark GPU execution against an existing database without a rewrite.

title: Synthesize the big picture and analyze trends with BigQuery's AI.AGG function
blurb: BigQuery's new AI.AGG() function, now in preview, lets you summarize or synthesize across millions of rows of unstructured or multimodal data using natural-language instructions inside a single line of SQL. It targets aggregate questions that per-row AI functions can't answer, like surfacing the top feature requests across negative reviews or clustering Spark logs to find memory thrashing and clock drift before anything throws a FATAL error.

title: Conversational analytics in BigQuery brings trusted agentic reasoning to everyone
blurb: Google made Conversational Analytics in BigQuery generally available, adding an agent built on Gemini that queries data, runs multi-step analyses, and generates visual reports from natural language across BigQuery, Iceberg, and cross-cloud sources like Databricks Unity, AWS Glue, and Salesforce. Teams can build agents grounded in specific datasets inside BigQuery Studio and publish them to Gemini Enterprise, Data Studio, or their own apps via the Conversational Analytics API.

title: When LLMs Read Tables Carelessly: Measuring and Reducing Data Referencing Errors
blurb: This paper presents the first systematic evaluation of data referencing errors (DREs) in LLMs reading tables, where models cite or omit cell values incorrectly even when they understand the table structure. The errors show up in every model tested, from 1.7B to 20B parameters, and corrupt intermediate reasoning steps rather than just final answers. Using a lightweight 4B critic to filter and reject-sample outputs lifted answer accuracy by up to 12% and hit a 78.2% F1 at catching the errors.

title: CLIP: Lightweight Cosine-Law-Based Inverted-List Pruning for IVF-Based Vector Search
blurb: CLIP is a pruning technique for IVF-based vector search that cuts the cluster and vector scans those indexes normally do exhaustively, eliminating irrelevant clusters in O(1) time and filtering vector batches in logarithmic time using cosine-law lower bounds. The paper reports up to 78% pruning and 69% higher query efficiency over static IVF baselines, plus an LSM-inspired variant that defers index maintenance to background compaction for 141% higher throughput on dynamic workloads.

title: DA-Studio: An Agentic System for End-to-End Data Analysis
blurb: DA-Studio is a web-based agentic system for end-to-end data analysis that turns raw files and natural-language requests into executable, multi-step workflows, running generated code in a sandboxed workspace with streamed action traces and artifact previews. The pitch for data teams is inspectability: every intermediate result, code edit, and rerun stays visible, so the agent's analysis path can be audited rather than trusted as a black box.

title: Experience Graphs: The Data Foundation for Self-Improving Agents
blurb: This paper argues that long-horizon agents produce a structured "experience graph" of artifacts, tool outputs, rewards, and causal lineage that most frameworks throw away as JSON checkpoints and session logs. The authors propose Trellis, a database that treats that graph as queryable, governed state, recasting frontier selection as a query, cross-session reuse as vector-seeded graph retrieval, and training-data extraction as a materialized view. In Meta's KernelEvolve kernel optimizer, that reuse hit target speedups roughly 10x faster at 52% lower token cost.

title: Scaling Network Analysis for Fraud Prevention with BigQuery Graph
blurb: Curve, a UK financial super-app, moved its fraud network analysis to BigQuery Graph, using native GQL to run multi-hop queries across millions of users and tens of millions of connections without standing up a separate graph database. Keeping the data in BigQuery avoided a migration to a dedicated graph store, cutting cost while surfacing the shared-device and shared-card links that organized fraud rings use to evade per-transaction models.

title: MaDI-Bench: An End-to-End Data Integration Benchmark
blurb: MaDI-Bench is the first benchmark to evaluate data integration end-to-end across the full pipeline of schema matching, value normalization, entity blocking, entity matching, and data fusion, rather than scoring each step in isolation. The authors validate it against human-engineered, best-of-breed, and LLM-based pipelines, and include a method for generating task variants to slow benchmark saturation as systems improve. It measures both step-wise and end-to-end performance, with all artifacts public.

title: Reading the internals of Postgres: Database cluster, databases, and tables
blurb: A developer's working notes from Hironobu Suzuki's "The Internals of PostgreSQL" walk through how Postgres organizes storage: a database cluster is a group of databases managed by one instance, not a set of nodes, and every object is tracked by an OID in system catalogs that are themselves just regular tables. The useful detail is that pg_database is shared cluster-wide while most catalogs are per-database, and user objects start at OID 16384 with the lower range reserved for init objects.

title: Making Multimodal LLMs Reliable Chart Data Extractors: A Benchmark and Training Framework
blurb: This paper benchmarks how well multimodal LLMs reverse-engineer data tables from chart images without visible value labels, and finds they reconstruct table structure reliably but miss on precise numeric values. The authors then train a model using a progressive, human-like chart-reading process, reaching state-of-the-art numerical accuracy with just a 7B-parameter model and supporting mixed-initiative extraction workflows. For data teams, it points to a cheaper path for turning published charts back into queryable tables.

title: Clean Me If You Can: A Large Collection of Real-World Addresses for Data Cleaning Benchmarking
blurb: This paper releases a large collection of real-world postal addresses paired with ground-truth corrections, aimed at benchmarking data cleaning tools against messy production data rather than synthetic test cases. The authors show that existing cleaning approaches degrade sharply on the dataset and offer guidelines for future work, giving data teams a realistic yardstick for evaluating deduplication and standardization pipelines.

title: Forecasting at the speed of modern retail
blurb: Databricks introduced MMF Agent, a guided AI workflow built on Genie Code that lets demand planning teams run its multi-model forecasting framework without deep data science expertise. The tool compresses forecast setup from days to hours across hundreds of thousands of SKU-level time series, extending multi-model accuracy gains to retail and CPG teams that previously lacked specialist forecasting talent.

title: Beyond dashboards: Introducing Decision Execution Platforms
blurb: Databricks Forward Deployed Engineering introduced Decision Execution Platforms (DEPs), a proposed analytics category that runs the full executive decision loop (signal, decision, execution, outcome) on governed Databricks infrastructure rather than stopping at dashboards. The pitch targets a BI market Databricks pegs at $34.8B in 2025 and $72.2B by 2034, arguing dashboards inform decisions but leave execution scattered across meetings, decks, spreadsheets, and Slack threads.

title: Apple just acquired the app that won last year’s ‘Innovation’ Apple Design Award
blurb: Apple acquired Play, the SwiftUI prototyping tool that won the 2025 Apple Design Award for Innovation, with the app pulled from the App Store and its team offered employment at Apple. The likely path is integration into Xcode or Apple's free developer resources, narrowing the gap between design prototyping and shipped SwiftUI code while removing a standalone tool designers had relied on.

title: The Shifting Line Between CSS States and JavaScript Events
blurb: CSS keeps absorbing behavior that once required JavaScript event listeners, and this walkthrough maps the pseudo-classes that now track interaction states: :hover, :active, :focus-visible, :focus-within, and :has(). It also covers the proposed event-trigger from the Animation Triggers spec, an unsupported feature that would let CSS respond to events directly, with a useful clarification that pseudo-classes match states rather than events even when they feel like listeners.

title: Figma just made your design system debt everyone’s problem. Now use it.
blurb: The author argues that every major thing Figma shipped in 2026 (its native AI agent, MCP-connected third-party agents, Code Connect, Make's pull requests, and Config's Motion and slot guardrails) reads from your design system, so each one fails in public when that system is rotting. The piece's useful turn is reframing maintenance debt as a now-shared cost, since the people with budget authority finally feel the breakage instead of just the maintainer.

title: What’s !important #14: Gap Decorations, random(), <select> field sizing, and More
blurb: Issue #14 of the What’s !important roundup collects a stacked run of CSS craft: Temani Afif's guide to styling gap decorations, Polypane's experiments with the Safari-only random() function, Manuel Matuzović on sizing <select> to its selected option with field-sizing: content, and Una Kravets on theming with @property, light-dark(), and contrast-color(). Most of the newer features covered reached Baseline recently, making it a practical snapshot of what's now shippable.

title: Koto reframes Stack Overflow around the one thing AI can't replace: its community
blurb: Koto rebranded Stack Overflow around its community as the trustworthy human layer beneath AI, repositioning the 18-year-old Q&A site from a place to ask questions to "the world's most vital source for technologists." The pivot leans on Stack Overflow's 83 million human-checked questions and answers, a direct answer to declining traffic since ChatGPT arrived and a bet that verified knowledge outlasts fast machine-generated ones.

title: Game UX: the cursor that wasn’t supposed to be there
blurb: A UX designer reviewed 235 major console releases from 2015 to 2025 and 507 player comments to interrogate the forced-cursor pattern, where an analog stick pushes a pointer across inventories, skill trees, and menus as the primary input. The data reframes a loud, evidence-thin debate: the pattern is less common than complaints suggest, and the friction concentrates in specific genres and studio conventions rather than console navigation as a whole.

title: How I blend 3D and 3D effects to create video game key art art using Blender and Photoshop
blurb: Darek Zabrocki breaks down how he created key art for the steampunk tactics game Veil of Ashes, blocking the scene in Blender from supplied 3D tank and aircraft assets, then handling VFX with cheap 2D image planes for explosions and dust before rendering high-res passes. He finishes in Photoshop with edge brushstrokes and painterly touches that soften the 3D look into something hand-crafted.

title: D&AD New Blood Awards 2026: a student project about periods wins both of the year's top prizes
blurb: Blood Stocks, a student proposal from Denmark's DMJX that reframes menstruation as a donatable medical asset, won both a Black Pencil and a White Pencil at the 2026 D&AD New Blood Awards in London. It was the only project in the program to take both honors, and one of just two Black Pencils awarded this year, signaling that judges rewarded social-impact concept work over polished brand craft.

title: 39 principles for designing human-AI interaction
blurb: A designer distilled research from human-AI interaction, mixed-initiative systems, and trust-in-automation into 39 applied principles covering when a system should suggest versus act, how to surface uncertainty, and how much autonomy a given action earns.
