The framework pulls from established HCI work alongside newer sources like OpenAI's Model Spec and Anthropic's Claude Constitution, aiming to help users rely on AI appropriately rather than blindly.

title: Rick Owens creates Adidas tracksuits that double as personal airconditioning
blurb: Rick Owens debuted inflatable Tyvek Adidas tracksuits at his SS27 Paris menswear show, using a fan built into each garment's lining and an ice vest underneath to create what he calls a "personal air conditioning system." The pieces adapt Japanese fan-jacket cooling tech that Adidas is also fitting to F1 drivers and 2026 World Cup players, blown up here to deliberately Michelin-Man proportions during the city's record heatwave.

title: A Teatro and Balcony Studio tuck Museum Tower House into steep site in Los Angeles
blurb: A Teatro and Balcony Studio completed Museum Tower House, a slender vertical residence wedged into a steep hillside lot in LA's Mt Washington neighborhood. The facades use striated plaster, and interior rooms are sequenced to unfold like museum galleries, with a two-car garage anchoring the single-family home to its grade.

title: Popular design trends that destroy conversion
blurb: The author argues that abstract hero visuals (the ubiquitous gradient blob with a light-leak) have become a herd-mentality default that tells visitors nothing about the product and tanks conversion. Since buyers decide in under two seconds, the piece makes the case for hero creative that shows the actual product over on-trend abstraction.

title: Why Accessibility Is An Operational Capability, Not A Feature
blurb: The author argues that accessibility should be treated as an operational capability like security, privacy, and observability, not a one-time audit or an end-of-project feature. The piece makes the case that audits still matter for procurement and legal documentation but fail during sprint planning, don't catch issues before merge, and go stale within months as the product keeps shipping.

title: The iPhone 18 Pro just leaked, and it might be the single biggest Apple leak since the iPhone 4
blurb: A data breach at Apple supplier Tata Electronics leaked drop-test photos of the iPhone 18 Pro, showing a device nearly identical to the 17 Pro with the same camera plateau and glass unibody cutout. The most visible change is a better color-matched cutout that drops the two-tone look, plus an apparent new deep-red finish, confirming Apple's Pro industrial design has settled into incremental refinement.

title: The New York Times app adopts Liquid Glass on iPhone
blurb: The New York Times iOS app now supports Apple's Liquid Glass, applying the material to its main navigation tab bar and mini audio player while leaving the rest of its interface untouched. The restrained approach has become standard among major apps with established visual identities, which adopt Liquid Glass at the chrome level rather than overhauling their design ahead of iOS 27.

title: iOS 27 breaks 15 years of muscle memory on iPhone and iPad
blurb: Enabling Siri AI in iOS 27 reassigns the swipe-down-from-center gesture that has opened Notification Center since iOS 5, relocating notifications to a narrow top-left corner region roughly two app columns wide. Apple devotes most of the top edge to invoking Siri, and beta 2 already rebalanced the layout after beta 1 shrank the notification target, so the interaction is still shifting before release.

title: Claude Code is steganographically marking requests
blurb: Claude Code (v2.1.196) embeds a hidden marker in its system prompt by altering the apostrophe in "Today's" and the date separator, triggered when ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL points at a decoded list of reseller and Chinese AI-lab domains or when the timezone is Asia/Shanghai or Asia/Urumqi. Researcher thereallo found the domain and keyword lists stored as base64 and XOR-decoded with key 91, suggesting Anthropic uses the steganographic signal to fingerprint API resellers, unauthorized gateways, and distillation pipelines.

title: Phantom Squatting Uses AI-Hallucinated Domains for Phishing and Malware
blurb: Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 disclosed "phantom squatting," a technique where attackers register the nonexistent domains that LLMs hallucinate and stand up phishing kits on them to catch traffic AI tools route their way. Across 685,339 queries about 913 brands, two models produced 2.1M links, 13,229 already flagged malicious and roughly 250,000 invented domains unregistered, and Unit 42 observed an attacker register one predicted domain 23 days later.

title: New BioShocking Attack Tricks AI Browsers Into Leaking User Credentials
blurb: Researchers at LayerX disclosed BioShocking, an indirect prompt-injection technique that frames a malicious web page as a game and coaxes agentic AI browsers into exfiltrating user credentials, defeating OpenAI's ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity's Comet, Anthropic's Claude extension, and three others in testing. Vendor response was uneven: OpenAI patched Atlas, Perplexity closed the report without action, Anthropic's fix reportedly failed, and Fellou, Genspark, and Sigma did not respond.

title: Claude Sonnet 5 includes safeguards against dangerous cyber use
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a general-purpose model with improved reasoning, coding, and agentic tool use, shipping with cybersecurity safeguards enabled by default that detect and block dangerous cyber activity in real time using the same protections as Opus 4.7 and 4.8. Anthropic rates Sonnet 5 as lower cyber risk than its Opus models, cannot develop a working exploit, and points teams needing fewer restrictions to Opus 4.8 or its Cyber Verification Program.

title: Oracle E-Business Suite Flaw CVE-2026-46817 Actively Exploited in the Wild
blurb: Oracle E-Business Suite is under active exploitation via CVE-2026-46817 (CVSS 9.8), an improper-privilege-management and authentication flaw in Oracle Payments that lets an unauthenticated attacker take over instances over HTTP. Oracle patched it in last month's Critical Patch Update for versions 12.2.3 through 12.2.15, and Defused Cyber observed exploitation on its honeypots over the weekend despite no public PoC, so patch immediately.

title: Apple Patches 30+ iOS, macOS, Safari Flaws, Including AI-Discovered WebKit Bugs
blurb: Apple released security updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS Tahoe, and Safari 26.5.2, patching over 30 flaws including four WebKit memory-corruption and use-after-free bugs (CVE-2026-43707, -43716, -43745, -43715) found with AI tools from OpenAI Codex and Anthropic Claude. None are reported as actively exploited, but Apple says it shipped fixes earlier than usual to shrink the discovery-to-weaponization window as AI accelerates exploit development.

title: DHS confirms hackers breached HSIN info-sharing platform
blurb: DHS confirmed that an unidentified threat actor breached the Homeland Security Information Network (HSIN), a sensitive-but-unclassified platform used by federal, state, local, and private-sector partners, in an intrusion believed to have occurred between late May and early June. Attackers hit HSIN servers and an associated SharePoint collaboration system; DHS says classified networks were untouched but has not attributed the attack or confirmed whether documents were exfiltrated.

title: Unpatched Argo CD Repo-Server Flaw Could Let Attackers Take Over Kubernetes Clusters
blurb: Synacktiv disclosed an unpatched, unauthenticated RCE in Argo CD's repo-server, where the gRPC GenerateManifest service abuses kustomize's --helm-command option to execute an attacker-supplied script and, via a revived CVE-2024-31989 Redis cache-poisoning path, enables full Kubernetes cluster takeover. There is no patch or CVE eighteen months after the January 2025 report, so enable Argo CD's Kubernetes network policies, which the Helm chart ships off by default (networkPolicy.create=false).

title: Anthropic Restores Claude Fable 5 After U.S. Lifts Jailbreak-Linked Export Controls
blurb: Anthropic restored Claude Fable 5 worldwide on July 1 after the U.S. Commerce Department lifted the emergency export controls it imposed June 12 following an Amazon-reported jailbreak in the model. To lift the order, Anthropic shipped a classifier that blocks the reported technique in over 99% of attempts and agreed to self-hunt for flaws, coordinate on launches, and report malicious use.

title: Researcher Analyzes 3,000 Live ClickFix Payloads, Exposing API-Driven Malware Delivery
blurb: New research from Bert-Jan Pals dissecting roughly 3,000 live ClickFix payloads found the fake-CAPTCHA lures now pull commands from API-driven backend servers that check an access token and return a freshly encrypted script (rotating Base64, AES, TripleDES, Rijndael, and Deflate) on every request, all decrypting to the same in-memory PowerShell runspace. ClickFix has its own ATT&CK entry (T1204.004) and hit 47% of Microsoft Defender Experts' initial-access cases; defenders should hunt on the runspace execution, not the disposable wrapping.

title: GuardFall Exposes Open-Source AI Coding Agents to Decades-Old Shell Injection Risks
blurb: Adversa AI disclosed GuardFall, a shell-injection bypass that defeats command blocklists in 10 of 11 open-source AI coding agents tested, including opencode, Goose, Cline, Aider, and OpenHands. The agents match commands as plain text while bash rewrites them before execution, so tricks like r''m or base64-piped payloads run destructive commands with full account access; only Continue defended against it.

title: 282 iOS AI Apps Leak API Keys and Open AI Proxy Access in Network Traffic Study
blurb: Researchers at Wake Forest University found that 282 of 444 tested iOS AI chatbot apps leak paid LLM access through network traffic, exposing plaintext API keys (54 apps), keyless open relays to paid accounts (92), or replayable tokens still valid when captured (136), with 28 also leaking their system prompts. The credentials enable LLMjacking that Sysdig estimates could reach $46,000 per day per account, yet only 28% of notified developers had fixed the exposure three months after disclosure.

title: Progress Kemp LoadMaster Flaw Could Let Attackers Run Root Commands Pre-Auth
blurb: Progress Kemp LoadMaster contains a critical command-injection flaw (CVE-2026-8037, CVSS 9.8) that lets an unauthenticated attacker run arbitrary commands as root by sending a crafted JSON request to the /accessv2 API endpoint. Progress patched GA v7.2.63.2 and LTSF v7.2.54.18 on June 4 with no reported in-the-wild exploitation, but watchTowr published a full exploit chain on June 29, so edge appliances with the API enabled should be updated now.

title: ChocoPoc malware delivered via trojanized exploits on GitHub
blurb: Sekoia identified at least seven trojanized PoC repositories on GitHub delivering ChocoPoC, a Python RAT that steals browser credentials, cookies, and shell history and runs arbitrary commands, in a campaign targeting security researchers. The malware hides in a PoC dependency rather than the exploit itself, pulling malicious PyPI packages 'frint' and 'skytext' that fetch the final payload from a Mapbox dataset; skytext was downloaded 2,400 times, mostly on Linux.

title: Malicious PyPI packages give hackers control of Telegram bot servers
blurb: Checkmarx disclosed Operation Navy Ghost, a campaign that has published at least eight trojanized Pyrogram forks to PyPI since November 2025, each hiding a secret.py backdoor that registers Telegram command handlers to run attacker-supplied Python or shell commands on the bot server. Developers using the unmaintained Pyrogram library should audit dependencies for the named forks and rotate any credentials or session tokens exposed to affected bots.

title: Critical Cursor Flaws Could Let Prompt Injection Escape Sandbox and Run Commands
blurb: Two critical flaws in the Cursor AI code editor, tracked as CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549 (CVSS 9.8) and dubbed DuneSlide by Cato AI Labs, let prompt injection escape the agent's default terminal sandbox and achieve zero-click RCE. Both abuse a coerced file write to disable the sandbox helper, and both are patched in Cursor 3.0 (April 2), so update any version prior to 3.0.

title: Armadin details full sandbox escape in Claude Cowork but Anthropic disputes risk
blurb: Armadin published a full sandbox escape chain against Anthropic's Claude Cowork, using DLL sideloading of USERENV.dll into signed claude.exe to gain a trusted client, then abusing two unchecked process-spawn parameters to run arbitrary commands as root inside the Hyper-V VM with network restrictions stripped. Anthropic declined to treat it as a security issue because the chain requires pre-existing local code execution on the host, validated against Claude Desktop for Windows 1.9255.2.0.

title: Hackers target Microsoft 365 accounts with 81 million login attempts
blurb: A password-spraying campaign generated over 81 million Microsoft 365 login attempts between June 12 and 26, authenticating through Azure CLI and the ROPC OAuth flow to bypass MFA where Conditional Access policies didn't cover it. Huntress observed the threat actor compromise 78 accounts across 64 organizations, and defenders should block ROPC at the /token endpoint and extend Conditional Access MFA to All Cloud Apps rather than scoped applications or user groups.

title: Azure CLI Password Spray Hits at Least 78 Microsoft Accounts in 81M+ Attempts
blurb: An ongoing automated password-spray campaign against Microsoft's Azure CLI made over 81M login attempts between June 12 and June 26, compromising at least 78 accounts across 64 organizations, per Huntress. The attackers abused the deprecated ROPC OAuth grant to bypass Conditional Access policies and MFA, so defenders should block ROPC authentication, enforce sign-in risk policies, and reset credentials for any accounts hit from the LSHIY LLC range (2a0a:d683::/32, AS32167).

title: Microsoft Warns Poisoned MCP Tool Descriptions Can Make AI Agents Leak Data
blurb: Microsoft Incident Response and Defender researchers demonstrated a tool-poisoning attack in which a malicious MCP tool description embeds hidden instructions that direct an AI agent to exfiltrate data, using a finance agent that leaks unpaid invoices as the example. MCP hosts pick up description changes on the fly without re-approval, so the agent follows the injected order while every step looks routine and no default alert fires.

title: Langflow RCE Exploited to Deploy Monero Miner on Exposed AI App Endpoints
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-33017 (CVSS 9.3), an unauthenticated RCE in Langflow, to run a single line of Python that drops a shell script and deploys the Go-based "lambsys" Monero miner on exposed AI application endpoints. The malware kills rival miners, disables AppArmor, UFW, iptables, and SELinux, wipes logs, establishes cron persistence, and propagates via reused SSH keys, so patch exposed instances and rotate SSH keys assuming lateral movement.

title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
blurb: Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions tied to a single threat actor it tracks as StegoAd, which used steganography to hide payloads inside PNG, WebP, and WOFF2 image and font files, then activated days post-install to steal credentials and run ad fraud. Active since at least 2021, the extensions posed as ad blockers, VPNs, and translators across an install base of up to 2.6 million, using server-side validation, DevTools detection, and execution gates to evade analysis.

title: Fake Perplexity extension on Chrome Web Store tracked searches
blurb: A malicious Chrome extension called "Search for perplexity ai" impersonated Perplexity AI, routing all Omnibox and address-bar queries through attacker infrastructure at perplexity-ai[.]online before redirecting to legitimate search. Microsoft found no credential theft, but the extension's DNR permissions for traffic redirection and URL rewriting enabled extensive profiling; affected users (ID flkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcd) should remove it and rotate critical passwords.

title: Citrix Patches Six NetScaler Flaws Allowing File Read and Denial-of-Service
blurb: Citrix released patches for six NetScaler ADC and Gateway flaws, the most severe being CVE-2026-10816 (CVSS 7.7), an unauthenticated arbitrary file read when management access is enabled on NSIP, Cluster IP, or SNIP. The other five (CVSS 6.9-8.8) enable memory overreads or denial-of-service across SAML IDP, Gateway, DNS, and HTTP/2 configurations; upgrade to the fixed 14.1 or 13.1 builds.

title: Mustang Panda Uses Zoho WorkDrive as Command Channel in Indian Government Attacks
blurb: Mustang Panda is running two espionage campaigns against Indian government and hydropower targets, deploying SHARDLOADER, MINIRECON, and the novel ZOHOMURK implant, which abuses hardcoded Zoho WorkDrive OAuth credentials to use an attacker-controlled cloud account as a command-and-control dead drop that blends with normal traffic. Acronis confirmed active compromises of senior administrative machines, attributes the activity with high confidence based on reused Toneshell code and shared infrastructure, and worked with CERT-In on cleanup.

title: 236,000 DCloud Uni-App Sites Used in Crypto Scams, Phishing, and Wallet Drainers
blurb: Infoblox identified 236,493 distinct second-level domains running investment-scam templates built on DCloud Uni-App, a legitimate Chinese cross-platform framework, powering fake crypto exchanges, pig-butchering operations, WhatsApp phishing, rigged gambling sites, and wallet drainers since mid-2022. Technical fingerprints, victim-communication patterns, and bulletproof hosting suggest centralized ownership across a large share of the sites, including the RainbowEx Ponzi scheme that defrauded tens of thousands in Argentina.

title: Progress Kemp LoadMaster Pre-Auth RCE Flaw Faces Active Exploitation Attempts
blurb: Progress Kemp LoadMaster is under active exploitation via CVE-2026-8037 (CVSS 9.6), an unauthenticated OS command injection in the /accessv2 API endpoint that stems from an out-of-bounds heap read in escape_quotes() and yields arbitrary code execution. eSentire observed attempts from three IPs starting June 29, all failing so far, but a public PoC and watchTowr's technical writeup make follow-on attacks likely, so patch the appliance immediately.

title: Fake Bug Report Hijacks AI Coding Agents at Scale
blurb: Tenet Security disclosed "agentjacking," a prompt-injection technique that plants a single fake error report in a public bug tracker to make AI coding agents like Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex fetch the poisoned data and execute attacker-controlled code on developer machines. The demo abused publicly exposed Sentry DSNs, which accept unauthenticated telemetry, and yielded cloud credentials, GitHub tokens, and CI/CD secrets while IAM, EDR, and network controls saw only authorized actions.

title: Citrix patches a new NetScaler flaw with echoes of CitrixBleed
blurb: Citrix disclosed six vulnerabilities in NetScaler ADC and Gateway, headlined by CVE-2026-8451 (CVSS up to 8.8), an out-of-bounds memory read triggered by malformed SAML requests to appliances configured as SAML identity providers. WatchTowr traced it to the same root cause as the actively exploited CVE-2026-3055 and the 2023 CitrixBleed class, so admins running SSO deployments should patch immediately and apply the manual configuration change Citrix flags for one CVE.

title: India’s central bank mandated use of .bank domains to enhance trust – but its registry leaked sensitive info
blurb: India's Reserve Bank mandated banks migrate to the .bank.in domain to curb phishing and build customer trust, but the registry running the program exposed sensitive applicant data, including documents and contact details submitted during onboarding. Institutions that filed through the registry should treat their submitted records as compromised and watch for targeted phishing that abuses the leaked details to impersonate the very trust scheme meant to prevent it.

title: New attack provides one more reason why AI browsers are a bad idea
blurb: LayerX researcher Roy Paz demonstrated a context-manipulation attack that tricks AI browsers into a "fantasy" state where safety guardrails no longer apply, by presenting a malicious puzzle site that rewards false answers like 2+2=5. Once the embedded LLM accepts the altered reality, an attacker can invoke destructive actions such as exfiltrating code from private repositories or extracting credentials from the browser's built-in password manager.

title: Google Patches 382 Chrome Vulnerabilities
blurb: Google released Chrome 151 with fixes for 382 vulnerabilities, 15 rated critical and 67 high, including use-after-free, type confusion, and out-of-bounds flaws in the renderer that could allow arbitrary code execution or sandbox escape via crafted web content. Google discovered 358 of them internally in a surge it attributes to AI, and the advisory reports no in-the-wild exploitation, so patch on the normal update cycle.

title: 'Phantom Squatting': An Emerging AI-Driven Supply Chain Threat
blurb: Unit 42 disclosed "phantom squatting," a supply-chain attack vector in which adversaries register nonexistent domains that LLMs repeatedly hallucinate for legitimate brands, then intercept AI-directed traffic to phishing kits, fake API endpoints, or credential-capture portals. Researchers found 250,000 hallucinated domains across 913 brands and observed one attacker use an AI coding assistant to build a full phishing kit for a flagged phantom domain.

title: iOS 26.5.2 has fixes for 25+ security issues on iPhone, details here
blurb: Apple released iOS and iPadOS 26.5.2 and macOS 26.5.2, patching nearly 30 security issues that center on WebKit and WebRTC, with additional kernel-level fixes. The flaws were first addressed in the iOS 26.6 betas; Apple recommends all iPhone, iPad, and Mac users install the update, since web-engine bugs are common drive-by exploitation vectors.

title: The Bear Necessities: A Look at the Drivers, Dynamics, and Applications of the Pro-Russia Influence Ecosystem
blurb: Google Threat Intelligence Group reports that the pro-Russia influence ecosystem has matured from a wartime tool into a global strategic asset, pivoting from Ukraine-specific goals back toward the EU, NATO, and other long-standing Kremlin targets. The group expects influence operations and hacktivism to intensify outside Ukraine, increasingly powered by generative AI for planning, research, and content creation. Defenders should treat the ecosystem as resilient to narrow takedowns, since its interconnected components survive limited-scope disruption.

title: Adobe Patches 7 CVSS 10.0 Flaws in ColdFusion and Campaign Classic
blurb: Adobe patched seven CVSS 10.0 flaws across ColdFusion and Campaign Classic, including unrestricted file upload, improper input validation, path traversal, and incorrect authorization bugs (CVE-2026-48276 through CVE-2026-48286) that enable unauthenticated arbitrary code execution. No exploitation has been observed yet, but admins should apply ColdFusion 2023 Update 21, 2025 Update 10, and Campaign Classic ACC v7 build 9397, prioritizing internet-facing on-premise instances.

title: Gamaredon Expands Ukraine Attacks with New Malware and Cloud Service Abuse
blurb: ESET reported that Russian APT Gamaredon ran 35 spear-phishing campaigns against Ukrainian government and military targets in 2025, deploying six new PowerShell tools and weaponizing a patched WinRAR flaw (CVE-2025-8088) to drop HTA downloaders into the Startup folder for persistence. The group increasingly routed command-and-control through tunnel services and serverless worker platforms to mask its back-end infrastructure, so defenders should monitor outbound traffic to these providers and patch WinRAR.

title: 'Djinn' Stealer Targets Cloud, AI Credentials
blurb: Djinn Stealer is being deployed via CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication-bypass in the SimpleHelp RMM platform, which an attacker exploited to obtain an authenticated technician session and mass-deploy an obfuscated JavaScript loader (TaskWeaver) across endpoints. The payload sweeps developer machines for cloud credentials, SSH keys, API keys, and package-registry tokens (npm, PyPI, Maven, NuGet), so organizations running internet-facing SimpleHelp should patch immediately and rotate any exposed infrastructure and registry secrets.

title: Supreme Court delivers ‘major win’ for tech privacy in Chatrie ruling
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Chatrie v. United States that collecting phone location data from a geographic area constitutes a Fourth Amendment search, extending the Carpenter precedent to geofence warrants. The decision means law enforcement requests for bulk location data from providers like Google now face Fourth Amendment scrutiny, though the court declined to rule on whether Chatrie's specific warrant was valid.

title: US offers $10m bounty for info on Russia-linked hackers behind Signal and WhatsApp attacks
blurb: The US State Department offered up to $10M for information identifying members of UNC5792 (FSB-linked) and UNC4221 (Russian military intelligence), which phished the Signal and WhatsApp accounts of US and allied government, defense, and diplomatic personnel. The groups abused legitimate device-linking features via social engineering rather than encryption flaws, compromising thousands of accounts; CISA and the FBI warn the actors have shifted toward harvesting victims' backup recovery keys.

title: Researchers spot exploitation of another critical Oracle defect
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-46817 (CVSS 9.8), a low-complexity flaw in the payments module of Oracle E-Business Suite that Oracle disclosed and patched in late May, with threat-intel firm Defused observing six honeypot hits from a single IP before any public PoC existed. Shadowserver counts roughly 950 exposed instances, over half in the US, and the same suite drew Clop's mass-extortion campaign last year, so patch and hunt for pre-patch access.

title: Securing the AI Agent: A Unified Framework for Multi-Layer Agent Red Teaming
blurb: AI-Infra-Guard is an open-source framework that red teams AI agents by matching a detection paradigm to each layer of the attack surface: infrastructure, protocol/tool, agent behavior, and model. It spans deterministic rule matching over 75+ components and 1,400+ vulnerability rules, LLM-driven auditing of MCP servers and agent-skill packages, multi-turn black-box red teaming, and a jailbreak harness with 26+ attack operators. It also audits the agent-skill supply chain, which most tooling ignores.

title: An Empirical Study of Security Calibration in Large Language Models for Code
blurb: A new empirical study evaluated security calibration in GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.0-Flash, and Qwen3-Coder-Next, testing whether models can tell when the code they generate is insecure. The authors found overconfidence is pervasive, calibration-guided automated repair yields only limited fixes while frequently introducing functional regressions, and architectural gating that helps on controlled benchmarks degrades in realistic repository-level settings. For teams shipping LLM-generated code, model confidence is not a reliable signal of security, so keep independent SAST and review in the loop.

title: Security--Fidelity Tradeoffs: The Hidden Cost of Prompt Injection Defense
blurb: This paper introduces SecFid, a benchmark showing that indirect prompt injection defenses buy security by suppressing untrusted text, which corrupts tasks like translation and document editing that must preserve it. Across 1,168 examples and 48 configurations, no model or defense wins both: the most secure reach 99.3% security at 71-74% fidelity, while the highest-fidelity model hits 96.5% fidelity at 47.8% security. The right tradeoff depends on deployment, so reporting security without fidelity hides its cost.

title: US offers $10 million to identify hackers targeting Signal and WhatsApp users
blurb: The State Department is offering a $10M Rewards for Justice bounty for information identifying members of Russian state groups UNC5792 (FSB Border Guards) and UNC4221 (military intelligence) behind a phishing campaign that has compromised thousands of Signal and WhatsApp accounts. The attacks trick targets into linking an attacker device or surrendering recovery keys, defeating Signal's linked-device protections, so defenders should audit linked devices and train high-value users against verification-code phishing.

title: Silent Swap Crypto Clipper Uses Fake Google Notes Extension to Replace Wallet Addresses
blurb: McAfee Labs detailed Silent Swap, an active crypto-clipper campaign that installs a fake "Google Notes" Chromium extension via unsigned .NET and Golang installers, then rewrites wallet addresses copied to the clipboard to reroute funds to attacker wallets. It uses EtherHiding to resolve C2 through blockchain smart contracts and hijacks browsers by editing Secure Preferences files, so defenders should block unsigned installers and audit extensions across Chrome, Edge, Brave, and Vivaldi.

title: Security researchers tricked LLMs into giving them cocaine recipes by abusing role models for prompt injection
blurb: Security researchers bypassed LLM safety guardrails to extract prohibited content, including drug-synthesis instructions, by abusing role-play and persona prompts to smuggle in prompt injections. The work underscores that current alignment filters remain reactive and easily circumvented, so teams deploying LLMs in production should layer input/output content filtering and treat model-side guardrails as insufficient on their own.

title: Inside the Advisory Database and what happens when vulnerability volume breaks records
blurb: GitHub's Advisory Database published 1,560 reviewed advisories in May 2026, more than 5x its typical monthly output and the highest on record. Private vulnerability reports jumped from ~550/week in January to over 3,000/week, and GitHub CNA CVE requests hit nearly 4,000 in May, roughly 10x year-over-year. Review times are now longer, but advisories remain human-validated. Defenders can help by submitting complete vulnerability data, coordinating with maintainers, and requesting CVEs only with clear intent to publish.

title: Massive Password Spray Campaign Targeting Azure CLI
blurb: A large-scale password spray campaign against Azure CLU logged over 81 million login attempts between June 12 and 21, compromising 78 accounts across 64 organizations by abusing the deprecated OAuth ROPC flow, which sends credentials straight to the /token endpoint with no interactive MFA prompt. Because ROPC bypasses MFA and SSO, defenders should block legacy authentication via Conditional Access and confirm MFA policies cover every cloud app and authorization flow, not just interactive logins.

title: Windows Defender 'BlueHammer' vulnerability now exploited as part of malware campaigns — CISA issues warning despite patch release on April 14
blurb: CISA added BlueHammer, a race-condition privilege-escalation flaw in Windows Defender that yields a SYSTEM shell from a small script, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active use in ransomware campaigns. Microsoft shipped the fix through standard Windows updates on April 14, so exposed fleets should prioritize patching immediately, as SYSTEM-level access lets operators encrypt the OS or boot process and render machines unbootable.

title: AI-Generated PowerShell Malware: An Experimental Framework and Dataset
blurb: This paper proposes an experimental framework and sandbox for dynamic analysis of LLM-generated PowerShell malware, and releases a manually curated dataset of real-world PowerShell samples annotated in natural language. Testing permissive open-weight models adapted to malware generation, the authors found AI-generated scripts closely mirror real malware in triggered OS-level malicious events, with a median Jaccard index of 84.5% and complete overlap in 48.4% of cases. Behavioral detections keyed to OS events, rather than static signatures, will hold up better against this generation.

title: Microsoft accelerates quantum-safe roadmap as risks grow
blurb: Microsoft is accelerating its Quantum Safe Program, committing to transition critical products and services to post-quantum cryptography by 2029 and folding quantum-safe requirements into its Secure Future Initiative. The company cites a shortened risk horizon and "harvest now, decrypt later" threats, and urges organizations to prioritize crypto-agility and TLS 1.3 now rather than waiting for cryptographically relevant quantum computers to arrive.

title: Apple’s Hide My Email feature has a bug that’s been exposing real email addresses, researcher claims
blurb: A researcher disclosed a bug in Apple's Hide My Email that unmasks the real address behind its disposable aliases, with 100% of tested addresses exploitable in limited trials with volunteers. Researcher Tyler Murphy says he reported it to Apple over a year ago and it remains unpatched; technical details are being withheld to prevent exploitation, but anyone relying on the feature for anonymity should treat their real address as linkable.

title: What the Saga Over Anthropic’s Mythos Tells Us About the Cyber Risks From AI
blurb: Anthropic has restricted its Mythos model to 200 partner organizations, saying its ability to find software vulnerabilities is too dangerous for general release. The company warned that broad access could let attackers more easily steal data or disrupt critical infrastructure, framing the limited rollout as a guardrail against offensive misuse.

title: VEIL#DROP Malware Chain Uses Blogger Platform to Deliver PureLogs Stealer
blurb: Securonix disclosed VEIL#DROP, a multi-stage malware chain that uses a fake document JavaScript file (e.g., transcript.pdf.js) run through Windows Script Host to launch PowerShell and pull next-stage payloads from attacker-controlled Blogger pages, ultimately deploying the PureLogs .NET infostealer. The chain abuses Google's trusted blogspot infrastructure to evade reputation-based defenses and builds per-execution URLs with randomized slashes to defeat static URL signatures and indicator-based blocking.

title: Certified Speculative Execution for Untrusted AI Agents
blurb: This paper introduces Certificate-Gated Prefix Acceptance (CGPA), a speculative-execution contract that lets hard-constrained systems use untrusted LLM agents without forfeiting feasibility guarantees. A trusted verifier rejects constraint-violating transitions while a conformally calibrated value boundary accepts the longest safe prefix, deferring the rest to a solver. Across six frozen LLMs, including one violating constraints in 98% of direct rollouts, it drove applied violations to zero and delivered a 2.96x speedup at 2.1% regret.

title: RustDuck Botnet Rebuilds in Rust to Hijack Routers and Servers for DDoS
blurb: RustDuck, a two-stage DDoS botnet tracked by QiAnXin's XLab since February 2026, is being rewritten from C into Rust and spreads by guessing weak Telnet/SSH credentials plus exploiting old device flaws including CVE-2017-17215, CVE-2025-29635, and CVE-2024-1781. It also hits known holes in ThinkPHP, Jenkins, and Hadoop YARN, extending reach from home routers and IP cameras to exposed server software.

title: Microsoft Adds New Teams Controls to Block Unauthorized AI Bots From Meetings
blurb: Microsoft added a new "Manage external bots and their access to meetings" policy to the Teams Admin Center that detects AI meeting bots, holds them in the lobby under a "Suspected threats" group, and requires explicit organizer confirmation before admission. ISVs can register bots with a self-identification marker to be recognized as known participants, and the existing CAPTCHA verification is being retired.

title: China-Linked Group Targets Southeast Asia Critical Systems
blurb: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 attributed more than 10 intrusions across Southeast Asia to China-linked group CL-STA-1062, which compromised electricity and water providers, government agencies, and military organizations while deploying a new backdoor dubbed TinyRCT. The activity mirrors Volt Typhoon-style pre-positioning against critical infrastructure, so defenders in the region should hunt for lateral movement between linked government entities and treat OT-adjacent networks as targets.

title: Microsoft to assist European Commission in defense of EU-US data-sharing agreement
blurb: Microsoft agreed to support the European Commission's legal defense of the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, the transatlantic agreement that lets companies move Europeans' personal data to US servers under approved safeguards. A court strike-down would invalidate the legal basis many cloud and SaaS providers rely on for EU-to-US transfers, forcing security and compliance teams back to standard contractual clauses and fresh transfer-risk assessments.

title: Nika: Open-source code analysis tool
blurb: PhonePe released Nika, an open-source tool that performs cross-file, inter-procedural taint analysis on Java microservices, tracing attacker-controlled input from sources to security-sensitive sinks like database queries, file operations, and reflection calls. It covers eleven vulnerability classes including SQL injection, SSRF, path traversal, and deserialization flaws, and offers an optional off-by-default AI review step to cut false positives.

title: SCOTUS says detailed cellphone location data is protected by the Fourth Amendment
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that obtaining a person's detailed cellphone location history from a tech company is a Fourth Amendment search, meaning police generally need a warrant backed by probable cause and particularized scope before accessing such records. The decision does not ban geofence warrants and is not company-specific, so the protection could extend to comparable location data held by Apple and others.

title: A Lifecycle and Application-Stack Survey of Large Language Model Vulnerabilities: Attacks, Risks, Defenses, and Open Problems
blurb: This paper systematizes LLM system vulnerabilities across eight lifecycle stages, from data collection and pretraining through retrieval, tool/agent execution, and deployment, arguing that risk now lives in the full application stack rather than the model weights. The authors map attacks to confidentiality, integrity, availability, safety, and agency-control objectives, emphasizing where trust boundaries fail, how untrusted data becomes executable instruction, and why point defenses rarely compose. They close with a research agenda covering provenance-aware retrieval, tool-call containment, and long-horizon agent evaluation.

title: Understanding and Evaluating Claw-like Agent Security Through a Computer-Systems Lens
blurb: This paper introduces SafeClawArena, a benchmark of 406 adversarial tasks that tests always-on "Claw-like" agents (OpenClaw, NemoClaw, SeClaw) across skill supply-chain integrity, persistent state exploitation, cross-boundary data flow, and indirect prompt injection. Attack success rates hit 70%, and malicious plugins succeeded 100% of the time regardless of the underlying LLM. SeClaw cut GPT-5.4's rate to 22%, partly via utility tradeoffs, while Claude-Opus-4.6 already sat near that floor everywhere.

title: Microsoft builds a bouncer to keep bots out of Teams meetings
blurb: Microsoft is adding bot-detection controls to Teams that let organizers block unauthorized AI notetakers and meeting-bot agents from joining calls. The feature targets the growing wave of third-party transcription assistants that silently enter meetings and exfiltrate audio, giving admins policy-level controls over which automated participants are permitted.

title: Red teamers turned Claude Desktop into a double agent to do their evil bidding
blurb: Pentera Labs red teamers chained a compromised email inbox into a victim's Claude Desktop account, then abused the app's cross-device session sync to reach full remote code execution on the developer's machine. The technique targets agentic AI tools with local code-execution access like Claude's Cowork and Code, so defenders should require manual approval for every agent-issued command and treat AI account access as a path to endpoint compromise.

title: Exploring Side-Channel Protections in Hardware Implementations of PQC ML-KEM Verification
blurb: Researchers evaluated side-channel protections for ML-KEM's Fujisaki-Okamoto decapsulation verification across microcontroller and FPGA implementations, testing unprotected, first-order hash-based, and higher-order masked designs. FPGAs leaked more than microcontrollers despite their speed advantage, and the parallelized processing of even higher-order masked designs produced enough first-order leakage for full secret-key recovery. Teams deploying PQC in hardware should not assume masking alone hardens FPGA implementations against power and EM analysis, especially in high-bandwidth configurations.

title: CVE-TTP KG: Knowledge Graph Linking Software Vulnerabilities to Attack Behaviors
blurb: This paper introduces CVE-TTP KG, a knowledge graph that links CVEs to MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques, filling the gap where CVE and NVD entries describe vulnerabilities but not the attacker behaviors that exploit them. A fine-tuned CySecBERT model reaches macro F1 of 87.71% on technique classification and 96.16% on tactics, with the extracted entities and relations loaded into a Neo4j graph for structured threat analysis.

title: A forgery attack on the Block.co blockchain-based digital credential certification system
blurb: Researchers demonstrated a forgery attack against Block.co, a blockchain-based digital credential certification system, producing forged academic certificates that the system validates as genuine. The flaw stems from the inability to bind an institution's real-world identity to its on-chain issuer identity without a trusted authority, so the authors expect the attack to generalize to other decentralized certification schemes using the same approach.

title: MESA: Prioritizing Vulnerable Communication Channels for Securing Multi-Agent Systems
blurb: MESA is a label-free framework for ranking which communication edges in a multi-agent system are most security-critical, so defenders can harden them before any attack is observed. The authors find channel-level risk is highly concentrated, with a single compromised edge accounting for up to 75% of attack success. Across three scenarios, eight topologies, and five open-source LLMs, monitoring the top 10% of MESA-ranked edges intercepted roughly 3x the attacks of random allocation (mean Spearman ρ=+0.60).

title: The Decomposition Is the Fingerprint: Per-Component Identity for Agent Skills
blurb: This paper introduces a locality-sensitive fingerprint for agent skills, storing each skill as a per-component triple (prompt, code, tools) in a 120-byte signature compared by Hamming distance. Keeping the components separate lets the fingerprint recover skill-family lineage through paraphrase, renaming, and refactoring, localize which component was reused, and flag tampered copies, hitting 0.974 AUC over 4,950 comparisons. The authors stress it signals identity and lineage for a skill registry, not behavioral safety, and still requires separate behavioral verification.

title: SEO-Poisoned Software Sites Abuse ScreenConnect to Deploy AsyncRAT
blurb: Kaspersky detailed a "massive, multi-domain, multi-language" campaign that uses SEO-poisoned sites spoofing OBS Studio, Bandicam, and DS4Windows to distribute installers that DLL-side-load ScreenConnect and ultimately deploy AsyncRAT via process hollowing. Researchers identified 90+ domains across 10 languages; defenders should flag ScreenConnect service installs from user-downloaded archives and hunt for the "MasterPackager.Updater" scheduled task and Defender exclusion changes.

title: Mutating the "Immutable": A Large-Scale Study of Git Tag Alterations
blurb: Researchers analyzed 328.4M repositories from Software Heritage and found 10.2M Git tag alterations across 189k repos, disproving the assumption that tags are immutable release anchors. A cross-check against Nixpkgs found 32 packages referencing altered tags, 7 with confirmed build failures, showing that force-pushed tag changes break reproducible builds and supply-chain integrity. Pin dependencies to cryptographic commit hashes rather than tags, and add monitoring for tag mutations as standard practice.

title: EnclaveX: End-to-End Confidential AI with CPU/GPU TEEs
blurb: This paper presents an end-to-end confidential AI workflow that combines CPU TEEs (Intel TDX, AMD SEV-SNP) with NVIDIA H200 GPU TEEs to keep data and model code confidential from the cloud provider. The authors flag that Kubernetes administrators can still access confidential VM contents, a gap application-level protections must close, and benchmark the performance overhead of running Intel TDX alongside H200 GPUs.

title: Supreme Court rules constitutional privacy protections apply to geofence warrants
blurb: The U.S. Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that geofence warrants, which sweep up location data on every device near a crime scene, constitute searches under the Fourth Amendment and require constitutional privacy protections. The ruling limits law enforcement's use of bulk Google location history requests and signals that opting into a service does not waive an expectation of privacy in one's movements.

title: WhatsApp is Finally Getting Usernames to Help Keep Phone Numbers Private
blurb: WhatsApp began global rollout of optional usernames, letting its 3B+ users be contacted without sharing phone numbers and hiding numbers from other accounts entirely. Users can also set a username key, a second secret required for first contact that can be reset to cut off inbound messages, arriving more than two years after Signal shipped the same protection.

title: Infosec professionals sour on automated pentesting tools
blurb: Security professionals have grown sharply more skeptical of fully autonomous pentesting tools, with those open to the idea dropping from 29% last year to 9% now. The reversal reflects concerns about false positives, missed context, and the limits of removing human judgment from offensive testing.

title: Palo Alto, CrowdStrike both have best quarter ever as AI threats bolster cyber demand
blurb: CrowdStrike and Palo Alto Networks each posted their best quarter on record, rallying 95% and 113% between April and June as Mythos-class models drove enterprises to overhaul cyber defenses. Both firms gained early access to Mythos as Project Glasswing partners and are betting on agentic security and identity, with Palo Alto's $25B CyberArk acquisition and CrowdStrike's SGNL deal underpinning the surge in demand.

title: DHS to unveil replacement council for critical infrastructure cybersecurity
blurb: DHS is standing up ANCHOR-CI, a CISA-managed council that restores government-to-industry cyber information sharing for critical infrastructure sectors, replacing the Critical Infrastructure Partnership Advisory Council shuttered over a year ago. Unlike its predecessor, ANCHOR-CI is exempt from the Federal Advisory Committee Act, so its meetings on threats, vulnerabilities, and mitigations for water, power, and telecom operators will fall outside public transparency requirements.

title: Apple accelerates security updates in response to AI-powered hacking risks
blurb: Apple released iOS, iPadOS, and macOS 26.5.2 with fixes for kernel, WebKit, and WebRTC flaws, pulling several patches forward from the planned 26.6 update to shorten the window attackers have to weaponize them. Apple told Reuters the move responds to AI models accelerating the development of exploit tooling, though it found no evidence the newly patched vulnerabilities had been exploited.

title: CSO-LLM: Class Subspace Orthogonalization for Post-Training Backdoor Detection and Trigger Inversion in LLMs
blurb: This paper introduces CSO-LLM, a post-training framework for detecting backdoors and inverting triggers in LLMs treated as classifiers, addressing the discrete input space and lack of token blacklists that make image-domain methods unworkable on language models. Its class subspace orthogonalization penalizes candidate trigger tokens that push signals toward the attack's target class, providing implicit blacklisting while improving detector sensitivity and specificity. The authors report strong detection and accurate ground-truth trigger recovery across multiple LLM classification domains and architectures.

title: Curvature-Guided Module Localization for Low-Rank Detoxification of Backdoored Large Language Models
blurb: This paper proposes a post-hoc method for removing backdoors from LLMs without full retraining, treating detoxification as localized structural repair rather than broad behavioral realignment. The framework uses activation patching and Fisher/K-FAC curvature analysis to localize the modules propagating trigger-induced behavior, then applies targeted low-rank repair to only those modules. Tested on poisoned Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct variants with triggers at the start, middle, and end of prompts, it suppressed trigger-conditioned malicious responses while preserving benign behavior.

title: Tell HN: Installing Cursor on iOS irreversibly changes your privacy settings
blurb: Installing the Cursor iOS app silently switches accounts off Privacy Mode (Legacy), the "do not store my code" setting, onto Cursor's current Privacy Mode, which permits code retention for Background Agents and other features. Cursor support confirmed the change is triggered by the Cloud Agents setup prompt and cannot be reversed, as the legacy option is no longer available in the app.

title: Crafty Phishing Campaigns Auto-Adapt to Victim's Device, OS
blurb: Cofense researchers documented phishing campaigns that fingerprint victims via browser user-agent data to deliver OS-specific payloads, such as serving FleetDeck to macOS targets and Tiflux RAT to Windows targets from the same landing page. Attackers are increasingly abusing Cloudflare user-agent blocking to route traffic by detected OS before the victim reaches the page, letting them ship tailored malware without custom detection scripts and evade sandboxes running mismatched environments.

title: Apple's Hide My Email may not be hiding anything
blurb: Researchers at EasyOptOuts disclosed a vulnerability in Apple's Hide My Email that lets attackers link the anonymous relay addresses back to a user's real email, with 100% of tested addresses exploitable in limited trials. The exploit details remain undisclosed and Apple has not shipped a fix a year after private disclosure, so iCloud+ subscribers should treat their relay aliases as potentially deanonymizable.

title: 6 security settings every GitHub maintainer should enable this week
blurb: GitHub Security Lab published a guided "Protect Your Project" flow of six free settings maintainers can enable in under 30 minutes to close the easiest attack paths. The list starts with a SECURITY.md file and private vulnerability reporting to route bug reports out of public issues, then adds secret scanning with push protection to block credentials before they land. None of it makes a project unhackable, but each setting measurably raises the cost of attacking it.

title: Attackers Hijack Exposed AI Endpoints to Power Offensive Ops
blurb: Zenity researchers observed three campaigns between March and May in which attackers pointed offensive tooling at exposed self-hosted AI inference endpoints, including Ollama on port 11434 and LiteLLM on port 4000, using them as a free model backend with no authentication or software exploit required. The operators ran autonomous pentest frameworks Strix and HexStrike AI and a jailbroken Codex agent, so defenders should firewall these endpoints off the public internet and require auth on every inference API.

title: Phishers Gain Persistence at EU, Asia Hospitality Orgs
blurb: Microsoft and Trend Micro reported separate but similar phishing campaigns against hospitality organizations in Europe and Asia, using zip archives with Windows shortcut files disguised as guest photos to install malware. Attackers impersonate guests with complaints to social-engineer front-desk and reservation staff, and rather than deploy ransomware they establish persistent remote access to later steal credentials, move laterally, or drop additional payloads.

title: Amazon blames piracy apps with malware for killing new Fire Stick sideloading
blurb: Amazon's VP of Fire TV, Aidan Marcuss, cited malware from piracy apps as the reason its new Vega OS Fire Sticks drop sideloading support, moving the devices off the Android-based Fire OS. The shift also gives Amazon tighter control over ad placement, tracking, and app distribution, since Vega OS blocks the custom launchers and third-party apps users relied on to bypass those measures.

title: A Giant Cable Exposes Ireland’s AI Ambitions and Security Risks
blurb: A new Amazon transatlantic fiber-optic cable landing in Ireland underscores the country's position as a European AI and data-center hub. Bloomberg notes the same buildout exposes a chronic gap in Irish defense spending, leaving critical subsea and cloud infrastructure underprotected against sabotage and state-level threats.

title: Trump budget boss Russell Vought open to re-staffing CISA
blurb: OMB Director Russell Vought told a House Appropriations subcommittee Tuesday that he is willing to work with DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin on re-staffing CISA, which has shed more than 1,000 of its roughly 3,400 employees under the Trump administration. Mullin has floated hiring 600 additional staff, but Vought said he has received no formal request and cautioned that clearances and onboarding make rapid rebuilding unlikely.

title: Digital signature schemes based on code equivalence and syndrome decoding from restricted errors
blurb: This paper surveys CROSS and LESS, the two code-based digital signature schemes advancing as second-round candidates in NIST's Additional Signatures call for post-quantum standardization. The authors walk through their hardness assumptions, syndrome decoding from restricted errors for CROSS and code equivalence for LESS, and show how sigma protocols become signatures via the Fiat-Shamir transform. Useful background for teams tracking PQC options beyond lattice-based schemes.

title: Off the Rails: Hijacking the Scoring Head in Generative End-to-End Driving Planners with Safety-Violating Adversarial Perturbations
blurb: Researchers introduced Derail, an adversarial framework that targets the scoring head in generative end-to-end autonomous driving planners, flipping trajectory selection from a safe to an unsafe candidate with score drops of 39-80% and collision rates up to 50%. The attack outperforms generic loss-maximization and feature-divergence methods, and the authors argue the scoring-head inference pattern shared across diffusion and vocabulary-based planners is a recurring attack surface that needs explicit defensive hardening.

title: Your Kids' School Bus Is About to Become a Roaming Surveillance Vehicle
blurb: BusPatrol, which operates over 40,000 school-bus stop-arm cameras across 24 states, plans to convert them into always-on automatic license plate readers that capture nearby vehicles and sell the data to law enforcement, according to leaked documents reported by 404media. The cameras would run continuously rather than activating only on a violation, expanding an already-unregulated ALPR surveillance network that civil-rights groups argue amounts to unconstitutional mass tracking.

title: Amplifying Membership Signal Through Chained Regeneration
blurb: This paper introduces MADreMIA, a model-agnostic framework that strengthens membership-inference and dataset-inference attacks by chaining generations, feeding each output back as input. Memorized training samples stay coherent and degrade slowly across iterations, while non-members break down faster, yielding stronger membership evidence at low false-positive rates. The authors show gains across diffusion, autoregressive, and language models, with early results for audio, and note it avoids shadow-model training.

title: Authentication in Quantum Networks
blurb: A review of quantum-network authentication argues it's not an intrinsic limitation, and surveys quantum-secure schemes for classical messages, quantum messages, and entity authentication.

title: Robot Police Officers
blurb: The Sacramento County Sheriff's Office deployed a drone equipped with a high-powered magnet to disarm a barricaded suspect, using it to pull a knife from the person's hand inside a residence after negotiations failed. The June 22 incident marks an early operational use of remote robotics for direct physical intervention in police standoffs, raising open questions about use-of-force policy and oversight as the tactic spreads.

title: Huntress CEO says threat hunter used 'poor judgment' in alerting ransomware crim about law enforcement probe
blurb: Huntress CEO Kyle Hanslovan said one of the firm's threat hunters used "poor judgment" by tipping off a ransomware operator that law enforcement was investigating them. Hanslovan said the analyst acted alone and was not directed to make contact, and the company is reviewing its internal controls around analyst communications with tracked actors.

title: Aikido acquires Root to patch open-source software without forced upgrades
blurb: Aikido Security acquired Root.io, folding its agentic patching technology into the Aikido platform as a feature called Aikido Libraries. Root generates verified fixes for vulnerable open-source dependencies at the exact pinned versions teams already run, avoiding the version upgrades or vendor migrations that tend to break applications, and had raised $37.6M before the deal.

title: Robocalls: A Worldwide or US-only Problem? Analyzing Spam and Fraud in International Phone Calls
blurb: Researchers analyzed international robocalls across 65 countries, releasing the first public multimodal dataset: 8.7M call detail records, 839 transcripts from 28 campaign clusters, and 677 recordings collected over nine months. They found robocalls are a global problem but hit the US far harder than elsewhere, with US losses reaching $1.1B in 2025. The paper maps calling patterns, co-targeting attacks, and callback-number extraction, and proposes mitigations to blunt campaign effectiveness.

title: 19-Year-Old Scattered Spider Suspect Extradited to Face U.S. Hacking Charges
blurb: Finland extradited 19-year-old Peter Stokes, a dual U.S.-Estonian citizen tied to Scattered Spider, to face U.S. conspiracy, computer intrusion, and fraud charges, the DOJ announced July 1. Court records link Stokes, known as "Bouquet," to at least four intrusions including a May 2025 breach of a jewelry retailer with an $8M crypto demand, part of a wider crackdown on a crew the DOJ ties to over 100 intrusions and $100M in ransom payments.

title: A Brazilian banking trojan is targeting Santander and BBVA customers with fake PDF lures
blurb: Ousaban, a Brazilian banking trojan, is targeting Windows users of Santander, BBVA, CaixaBank, and two dozen other Spanish and Portuguese banks via phishing PDFs that hide a ZIP payload inside a fake PDF-icon image (steganography) and auto-launch through embedded JavaScript. Fortinet's FortiGuard Labs, which disclosed the May campaign this week, notes it evades detection with server-side geofencing to Spain and Portugal and a date-seeded DGA that resolves a new C2 server daily, defeating static blocklists.

title: And the Winner in Dominant Malware Delivery? ClickFix
blurb: ClickFix, a social engineering technique that tricks users into pasting attacker-supplied commands into system dialogs, dominated initial-access and defense-evasion activity from March to May, per ReliaQuest research. The technique now spans macOS, where operators use applescript:// links to run commands in Script Editor and bypass the paste warning Apple added to Terminal in macOS 26.4, so detection and training should cover both platforms.

title: Ousaban Banking Trojan Targets Iberian Bank Users with Fake PDF Lures
blurb: Ousaban, a Brazilian banking trojan, is targeting Windows users in Spain and Portugal via phishing PDFs disguised as corrupted files, per Fortinet's FortiGuard Labs, which identified the campaign in May 2026. The malware uses steganography to hide its payload in an image, geofences victims to Iberian IPs, and monitors more than two dozen banks including Santander, BBVA, and CaixaBank to capture keystrokes, screenshots, and hijack live banking sessions.

title: Apple Patches 30+ iOS, macOS, Safari Flaws, Including AI-Discovered WebKit Bugs
blurb: Apple released security updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS Tahoe, and Safari 26.5.2 that fix more than 30 flaws, including four WebKit memory-corruption bugs found with AI tools from OpenAI Codex Security and Anthropic Claude. Apple told Reuters it shipped the patches earlier than usual to shrink the window between discovery and weaponization, citing concerns that AI accelerates exploit development.

title: Oracle E-Business Suite Flaw CVE-2026-46817 Actively Exploited in the Wild
blurb: Oracle E-Business Suite is under active exploitation through CVE-2026-46817, a 9.8-severity flaw in Oracle Payments that lets unauthenticated attackers take over instances over HTTP, affecting versions 12.2.3 through 12.2.15. Oracle patched it in last month's Critical Security Patch Update, but the in-the-wild attacks on unpatched systems extend a run of critical EBS and PeopleSoft bugs that already hit Nissan and drew Cl0p and ShinyHunters.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 AI model with coding, safety upgrades as Fable and Mythos controls lifted
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, a midrange model that improves on Sonnet 4.6 by 5.1% on SWE-Bench Pro and 13.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.1, though OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Terra still leads Sonnet on the latter by about 4%. It becomes the default across the Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise tiers and lands via API in September at $3 per million input and $15 per million output tokens, slightly above Terra.

title: Sources: Meta plans a cloud infrastructure business that will sell access to AI compute and models, to compete with AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud; META jumps 9% (Bloomberg)
blurb: Meta plans to launch a cloud infrastructure business selling access to its AI compute and models, putting it in direct competition with AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud, according to Bloomberg sources; the stock jumped 9% on the report. The move would let Meta monetize its massive GPU buildout as a third-party provider, opening a new front in the enterprise cloud market where the three incumbents control the bulk of spend.

title: Researcher Analyzes 3,000 Live ClickFix Payloads, Exposing API-Driven Malware Delivery
blurb: Security researcher Bert-Jan Pals analyzed roughly 3,000 payloads from live ClickFix campaigns and found the malicious commands are now dispensed by API-driven backend servers that hand each visitor a freshly scrambled command wrapped in rotating Base64, AES, and TripleDES encryption. The technique, which tricks users into pasting attacker commands into their own machines and accounted for 47% of initial-access cases in Microsoft's 2025 Defender data, leaves little for traditional email and endpoint controls to catch.

title: Progress Kemp LoadMaster Flaw Could Let Attackers Run Root Commands Pre-Auth
blurb: Progress disclosed CVE-2026-8037, a CVSS 9.8 flaw in Kemp LoadMaster that lets an unauthenticated attacker send a crafted /accessv2 API request and run arbitrary commands as root on the appliance. It affects GA v7.2.63.1 and LTSF v7.2.54.17 and earlier with the API enabled, fixed in GA v7.2.63.2 and LTSF v7.2.54.18, and watchTowr published a full exploit walkthrough on June 29.

title: Unpatched Argo CD Repo-Server Flaw Could Let Attackers Take Over Kubernetes Clusters
blurb: Security researchers at Synacktiv disclosed an unpatched remote-code-execution flaw in Argo CD's repo-server, where an unauthenticated gRPC request can abuse kustomize to run attacker-supplied scripts and escalate to full Kubernetes cluster takeover. There is no fix, no CVE, and the firm went public after 18 months of no response, so the only defense is enabling the Kubernetes network policies that Argo CD's Helm chart leaves off by default.

title: DHS confirms hackers breached HSIN info-sharing platform
blurb: DHS confirmed that an unknown threat actor breached the Homeland Security Information Network, an unclassified platform federal, state, local, and private-sector partners use to share sensitive incident and threat data, sometime between late May and early June. DHS says it isolated the affected systems and found no impact to classified networks, but the timing raises concern given the agency is coordinating World Cup security across the country.

title: ChocoPoc malware delivered via trojanized exploits on GitHub
blurb: Sekoia identified at least seven trojanized proof-of-concept exploit repositories on GitHub delivering ChocoPoC, a Python-based RAT that runs shell commands, steals browser credentials, and exfiltrates data through abused Mapbox datasets. Rather than embedding malware in the exploit code, the campaign hides it in PyPI dependencies named frint and skytext, the latter pulled 2,400 times and aimed at security researchers testing trending CVEs.

title: Hackers target Microsoft 365 accounts with 81 million login attempts
blurb: A password-spraying campaign generated more than 81 million Microsoft 365 login attempts over two weeks, using breached credentials to authenticate through Azure CLI's ROPC OAuth flow, which bypasses MFA where Conditional Access policies don't cover it. Huntress observed 78 accounts compromised across 64 organizations between June 12 and 26, exposing how narrowly scoped MFA policies leave legacy auth flows unprotected in enterprise tenants.

title: Azure CLI Password Spray Hits at Least 78 Microsoft Accounts in 81M+ Attempts
blurb: An automated password spray campaign made over 81M login attempts against Azure CLI between June 12 and 26, compromising at least 78 Microsoft accounts across 64 organizations, according to Huntress. The attack bypassed Conditional Access policies by abusing ROPC, a deprecated OAuth flow incompatible with MFA, meaning enterprises that enabled CAP but left legacy authentication paths open remain exposed regardless of their MFA posture.

title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
blurb: Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions tied to a single threat actor it calls StegoAd, which hid payloads inside PNG, WebP, and WOFF2 files using steganography to evade scanners before stealing credentials and running ad fraud. The campaign had been live since 2021 across a 2.6 million install base, exposing how thinly the Edge Add-ons store vets the ad blockers, VPNs, and translators enterprise users sideload onto managed browsers.

title: Anthropic to restore Claude Fable access on Wednesday
blurb: Anthropic will begin restoring access to Claude Fable 5 on Wednesday after the Department of Commerce lifted export controls on the model, while the more powerful Mythos 5 stays limited to select companies. Anthropic is also rolling out Persona-based identity verification for certain Claude capabilities, meaning some enterprise users may need government ID and a live selfie before regaining access to the restored models.

title: Fake Perplexity extension on Chrome Web Store tracked searches
blurb: A malicious Chrome extension named "Search for perplexity ai" impersonated Perplexity's AI answer engine, routing every Omnibox and address-bar query through attacker-controlled infrastructure at perplexity-ai[.]online before redirecting users to legitimate search services. Microsoft Threat Intelligence found no credential theft, but the extension's DNR permissions for traffic redirection and URL rewriting would let operators expand data collection at will, and IT teams should force removal of extension ID flkebkiofojicogddingbdmcmkpbplcd and rotate affected passwords.

title: Progress Kemp LoadMaster Pre-Auth RCE Flaw Faces Active Exploitation Attempts
blurb: Attackers began exploiting CVE-2026-8037, a CVSS 9.6 pre-auth command injection flaw in Progress Kemp LoadMaster, on June 29, letting unauthenticated attackers run arbitrary commands on the load-balancer appliance via crafted requests to its /accessv2 API endpoint. eSentire's observed attempts failed, but a public PoC and detailed writeup from watchTowr Labs are expected to drive wider attacks, so IT teams running LoadMaster should apply Progress's patch immediately.

title: Upgrade Amazon EKS clusters with confidence using Kubernetes version rollbacks
blurb: AWS added Kubernetes version rollbacks to Amazon EKS, letting cluster administrators reverse a control plane upgrade within seven days and return to the previously validated version, one minor version at a time. The feature removes the one-way-door risk that pushed regulated teams to delay upgrades, and EKS cluster insights automatically checks rollback readiness for node compatibility and add-on dependencies before proceeding.

title: Citrix Patches Six NetScaler Flaws Allowing File Read and Denial-of-Service
blurb: Citrix released patches for six vulnerabilities in NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway, including CVE-2026-10816 (CVSS 8.8), an unauthenticated arbitrary file read affecting appliances with management access enabled, plus five flaws that allow memory overreads or denial-of-service across SAML IDP, Gateway, AAA, and HTTP/2 configurations. Admins running affected 14.1 and 13.1 branches should upgrade to the fixed releases, and HTTP/2 deployments must also tune the Http2SmallWndTimeout parameter to close CVE-2026-13474.

title: Mustang Panda Uses Zoho WorkDrive as Command Channel in Indian Government Attacks
blurb: Mustang Panda, a China-aligned espionage group, is running two campaigns against Indian government and hydropower targets that abuse Zoho WorkDrive, a cloud storage platform common in India's public sector, as a covert command-and-control channel where malware reads commands and exfiltrates data through an attacker-controlled account. Acronis, which found active compromises on machines used by senior administrative staff and notified CERT-In, named three new tools and attributes the activity to the group with high confidence.

title: Automate public TLS certificate issuance with ACME support in AWS Certificate Manager
blurb: AWS Certificate Manager now provides a fully managed ACME server endpoint that works with any ACMEv2-compatible client, including Certbot, cert-manager, and acme.sh, letting teams issue public TLS certificates from Amazon Trust Services without external CAs. PKI administrators get centralized control through IAM-bound ACME accounts, endpoint-level domain scoping, and CloudTrail logging, consolidating certificates that previously lived in fragmented external tools ahead of the CA/Browser Forum's 47-day validity mandate in 2029.

title: Apple Seeks to Buy Chinese-Made Memory Chips With Lobbying Push
blurb: Apple is negotiating to buy memory chips from two Chinese semiconductor makers on a Pentagon blacklist, aiming to blunt a global memory shortage that has pushed it to raise prices across its product line. The move risks regulatory and political backlash in Washington, but signals that supply constraints are severe enough for Apple to court blacklisted suppliers rather than absorb further cost increases.

title: Citrix patches a new NetScaler flaw with echoes of CitrixBleed
blurb: Citrix disclosed six vulnerabilities in NetScaler ADC and Gateway on Tuesday, including CVE-2026-8451, a high-severity memory disclosure flaw that watchTowr traced to the same SAML-parsing root cause as CitrixBleed and a March bug already exploited in the wild. IT teams running NetScaler as a SAML identity provider for SSO should patch immediately and, in one case, manually adjust a configuration parameter, since the recurring memory-management issues make these appliances a durable attack surface.

title: India’s central bank mandated use of .bank domains to enhance trust – but its registry leaked sensitive info
blurb: India's Reserve Bank mandated that regulated banks migrate to .bank.in domains to curb phishing and boost consumer trust, but the registry it stands up exposed sensitive applicant data including PAN numbers and internal documents. The leak undercuts the trust premise of the program and hands enterprise security teams a fresh third-party risk to audit before onboarding to the new domain regime.

title: Google Patches 382 Chrome Vulnerabilities
blurb: Google released Chrome 151 with patches for 382 vulnerabilities, 358 of which it discovered internally in a months-long surge the company attributes to AI without naming the tools involved. Fifteen flaws are rated critical and 67 high severity, many affecting the renderer process where crafted web content could enable arbitrary code execution or sandbox escape, though Google reports no in-the-wild exploitation.

title: Adobe Patches 7 CVSS 10.0 Flaws in ColdFusion and Campaign Classic
blurb: Adobe patched seven CVSS 10.0 flaws across ColdFusion and Campaign Classic, most enabling arbitrary code execution via file upload, input validation, and path traversal bugs, with fixes shipped in ColdFusion 2023 Update 21, ColdFusion 2025 Update 10, and Campaign Classic build 9397. The Campaign Classic flaw hits only on-premise deployments, and Adobe is moving to twice-monthly security bulletins starting July 14 as AI-assisted discovery accelerates its vulnerability pipeline.

title: iOS 26.5.2 has fixes for 25+ security issues on iPhone, details here
blurb: Apple released iOS 26.5.2, iPadOS 26.5.2, and macOS 26.5.2 with patches for nearly 30 security vulnerabilities, most affecting WebKit and WebRTC along with several kernel-level fixes. The updates ship no new features and are recommended for all users, pulling fixes from the iOS 26.6 betas forward ahead of that release's public launch.

title: The Bear Necessities: A Look at the Drivers, Dynamics, and Applications of the Pro-Russia Influence Ecosystem
blurb: Google's Threat Intelligence Group reported that the pro-Russia influence ecosystem is pivoting from Ukraine-focused operations back toward broader targeting of the EU, NATO, and other strategic priorities, with growing use of generative AI for planning, research, and content creation. The report warns that the ecosystem's interconnected structure makes it resilient to narrow takedowns, so enterprise security teams should plan for coordinated disruption rather than single-point mitigations.

title: US reverses export restrictions on Anthropic’s Fable 5, Mythos 5 AI models
blurb: The US government lifted export restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 frontier models on June 30, restoring global access after a nearly three-week block prompted by concerns over the models' cybersecurity capabilities. Fable 5 begins rolling out globally on July 1 across Claude Platform, Claude.ai, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork, with AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Foundry access to follow, signaling a shift toward negotiated, conditional oversight of frontier AI rather than blanket prohibition.

title: 'Djinn' Stealer Targets Cloud, AI Credentials
blurb: Attackers exploited CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication bypass in SimpleHelp, to deploy the new Djinn Stealer infostealer across enterprise networks, harvesting cloud credentials, SSH keys, API keys, and registry tokens for npm, PyPI, Maven, and NuGet. SimpleHelp manages millions of endpoints at over 6,000 organizations, so the RMM foothold gives attackers admin-grade access and a direct path to supply-chain compromise via stolen package-publishing credentials.

title: AWS funnels $1B into forward deployed engineering hub
blurb: AWS committed $1B to a forward deployed engineering organization that embeds five-to-six-person pods into customer AWS accounts on 45-day sprints, pairing human engineers with AI agents that stay running long-term. The move matches Google Cloud's and Microsoft-plus-Accenture's FDE pushes and deepens vendor lock-in through a semantic layer and knowledge graph deployed directly into the customer's account, with Cox Automotive, the NBA, and the NFL already onboard.

title: Amazon EC2 C9g and C9gd instances powered by AWS Graviton5 processors are now available
blurb: AWS made its EC2 C9g and C9gd instances, powered by new Graviton5 processors, generally available, delivering up to 25% higher performance per vCPU than C8g and the fastest memory of any cloud instance via DDR5 8800MT/s DIMMs. The C9gd variant adds local NVMe SSD storage, and both target CPU-bound and agentic AI workloads across 11 sizes plus bare metal, giving enterprises a lower-cost alternative to x86 compute.

title: Supreme Court delivers ‘major win’ for tech privacy in Chatrie ruling
blurb: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Chatrie v. United States that government collection of phone location data from a geographic area constitutes a Fourth Amendment search, extending the privacy protections established in Carpenter v. United States to geofence-style data demands. The decision raises the legal bar for law enforcement requests to providers like Google and signals that enterprises holding customer location data will face tighter constraints on what they can hand over without a warrant.

title: US offers $10m bounty for info on Russia-linked hackers behind Signal and WhatsApp attacks
blurb: The US State Department offered up to $10M for information identifying members of UNC5792 and UNC4221, two Russia-linked groups that phished the Signal and WhatsApp accounts of US officials, NATO diplomats, and defense personnel by abusing the apps' legitimate device-linking features. The campaigns compromised thousands of accounts without breaking either platform's encryption, and follow last week's FBI and CISA advisory warning of a shift toward harvesting victims' backup recovery keys.

title: Anthropic's new Sonnet 5 model is better at the tasks that are running up enterprise bills
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5, which matches recent Opus-class performance on agentic tasks while pricing at $3 per million input tokens and $15 per million output tokens, below Opus 4.8's $4 and $25. A new tokenizer targets the runaway token spend that agentic tools inflict on enterprise budgets, giving heavy agentic buyers an Opus-grade option that meaningfully lowers per-workload inference costs.

title: Work with delegated Gmail accounts from mobile devices
blurb: Google Workspace extended Gmail delegation to Android and iOS, letting users switch between their own inbox and delegated accounts, view unread counts, and see mail intermingled in an "All inboxes" view. The feature follows existing web delegation settings with no separate admin control, rolling out to all Workspace tiers, Individual subscribers, and personal accounts by late July.

title: Get started with the Claude apps gateway for Google Cloud
